The Salt Woman (or salt spirits) became angry and moved the salt deposits far away, to the Grand Canyon and other distant locations. Ever since, the Hopi have had to undertake long, dangerous journeys to gather salt, a sacred substance used in ceremonies and daily life. The story teaches respect for natural resources and the consequences of taking blessings for granted.
source: Harold Courlander, People of the Short Blue Corn (1993); various Hopi oral traditions

title: The Journey to the Land of the Dead
summary: In Hopi tradition, the journey to the land of the dead begins at the sipapu in the Grand Canyon. The spirit travels westward, following the path of the setting sun, to the land of the ancestors. Along the way, the spirit encounters various challenges and must have the proper knowledge, gained through ceremonial instruction during life, to pass safely. The spirit is judged by Masauwu and the Kachinas who determine its fate. Good souls pass into a land of plenty, while those who lived badly face difficulties. The dead eventually become cloud people who can bring rain to their living descendants. The spirits of the dead are associated with the Kachinas and reside at the San Francisco Peaks. This cycle of life, death, and return as rain-giving spirits reinforces the Hopi connection between proper living, ceremonial participation, and the well-being of the community.
source: Harold Courlander, The Fourth World of the Hopis; H.R. Voth, The Traditions of the Hopi (1905)

title: Mockingbird Gives Out the Calls
summary: In this Hopi story, Mockingbird was responsible for distributing the languages and calls among all the peoples of the world. During the migrations after the emergence into the Fourth World, the people were all speaking the same language. Mockingbird called the people together and assigned different languages and dialects to different groups, effectively creating the diversity of tribes and nations. He gave various bird species their distinctive calls as well. According to some versions, Mockingbird received this task from Spider Grandmother or the Kachinas, and his imitative ability was used to create the range of human languages. The story explains why Mockingbird can mimic other birds and why there are so many different languages among the peoples of the earth.
source: Harold Courlander, People of the Short Blue Corn (1993)

title: Sikakokuh and the Hunting Dog
summary: A Hopi story about a boy named Sikakokuh who had a remarkable hunting dog. The dog was no ordinary animal but possessed spiritual power, able to track game without fail and communicate with its master. Sikakokuh's success as a hunter made others jealous. Through the dog's guidance, Sikakokuh learned the proper rituals for hunting, including prayers and offerings to the spirits of the animals he killed. When enemies or jealous rivals tried to harm the dog, supernatural forces protected both boy and animal. The story teaches the sacred relationship between humans and animals, the importance of gratitude, and the proper conduct of hunters who must respect the spirits of the animals they take.
source: Harold Courlander, People of the Short Blue Corn (1993)

title: The Iatiku and Nautsiti Creation (Acoma Pueblo)
summary: In the Acoma Pueblo creation myth, two sisters named Iatiku (Life Bringer) and Nautsiti (Full Basket) emerged from the underworld through a sacred place called Shipapu, guided by Tsichtinako (Thought Woman). They were given two baskets containing seeds, animal figurines, and sacred objects. Tsichtinako instructed them to pray to the Sun (Uchtsiti), their father, who had created the world from a clot of his own blood. The sisters created the mountains, directions, plants, and animals. Iatiku created the Corn Mother and the Kachinas. Nautsiti introduced death into the world when she disobeyed a taboo. The sisters became the mothers of the Acoma people, with Iatiku's children becoming the Pueblo clans. The story explains the origins of ceremonies, clans, and the relationship between the people, the land, and the spirits.
source: Edward Proctor Hunt, The Origin Myth of Acoma Pueblo (1928); Peter Nabokov edition (2015)

title: The Pueblo Emergence from the Underworld
summary: A central Pueblo creation theme across multiple Pueblo tribes (Acoma, Zuni, Hopi, Laguna, etc.) involves the emergence of the first people from the underworld through a sacred opening called the sipapu. In the beginning, the people lived in darkness beneath the earth. Guided by spirits, culture heroes, or twin war gods, they climbed up through four underground worlds, each representing a stage of development. They used a giant reed, spruce tree, or ladder to ascend. As they climbed, they transformed from insect-like or unfinished beings into fully formed humans. The last people to emerge were often an old man and woman who became trapped, becoming the rulers of the underworld — establishing that all must return there after death. The sipapu is often identified with the Grand Canyon or a specific location within each Pueblo's traditional territory.
source: Various Pueblo oral traditions; Edward Proctor Hunt, The Origin Myth of Acoma Pueblo; Wikipedia: Pueblo mythology

title: The Pueblo Hero Twins
summary: In Pueblo mythology, the Twin War Gods (Masewi and Oyoyewi in some traditions) are the most important heroic figures. They were born to a virgin mother who conceived from the Sun, much like the Navajo Hero Twins. These twins are credited with creating the world and all living creatures, as well as slaying a giant monster that terrorized the Pueblo people — a beast so large it could eat entire villages in one bite. The twins tracked the monster and killed it with their magical weapons, which included lightning arrows and shields. They established the directions, brought order to the world, and taught the people how to live. After completing their work, they returned to the underworld but continue to watch over the Pueblo people. They are often celebrated in ritual performances and dances, reinforcing their cultural significance as protectors and culture heroes.
source: Various Pueblo oral traditions; mythologyworldwide.com

title: The Ants That Pushed on the Pine Tree
summary: A Pueblo story from Isleta about Kahp-too-óo-yoo (Corn-stalk Young Man), a famous hunter, warrior, and wizard who controlled the clouds and brought rain. After being bewitched by a false friend, he was imprisoned in the top of a giant pine tree that reached the sky. His family and village suffered from drought without his rain powers. The Little Black Ant discovered Kahp-too-óo-yoo in the tree and summoned all the armies of the Little Black Ants and Big Red Ants. They climbed the tree, placed a cup of cornmeal and honey at the top, and then — with the Black Ants pushing from above and the Red Ants pulling from below — drove the giant pine tree deep into the earth, freeing Kahp-too-óo-yoo. He returned home, playing his flute, and the rains returned. The story reflects Pueblo values of community, cooperation, and the power of even the smallest creatures to help.
source: Pueblo Indian Folk-Stories (Sacred Texts Archive); sacred-texts.com

title: Tsichtinako Teaches the Sisters
summary: In the Acoma Pueblo creation story, Tsichtinako (Thought Woman) appeared as a voice guiding the sisters Iatiku and Nautsiti from within the earth. She couldn't be seen because she was a spirit without a human body. Tsichtinako instructed the sisters in all aspects of life: how to plant seeds from their baskets, how to make prayer sticks (pahos), how to conduct ceremonies, and how to organize their society into clans. She explained that Uchtsiti (the Sun, their father) had created the world but it was not yet complete, and the sisters were to complete it. Tsichtinako also taught them language, naming each plant, animal, and feature of the landscape. She established the four sacred directions and the relationship between the people and all beings. Tsichtinako continues to guide the Acoma people invisibly, representing the power of thought and the spiritual foundation of existence.
source: Edward Proctor Hunt, The Origin Myth of Acoma Pueblo (1928)

title: The Kachina Spirits of the Pueblo
summary: Across Pueblo cultures, the Kachinas (Katsinam) are supernatural beings who are the spirits of ancestors, natural forces, and all things in the universe — rocks, stars, animals, and plants. They reside in the San Francisco Peaks (north of Flagstaff, Arizona) and come to the pueblos for six months each year during the winter season. The Kachinas bring rain, fertility, and other blessings through ceremonial dances. Each Kachina has a specific identity, mask, songs, and role in the ceremonial calendar: Crow Mother brings rain, the warrior Kachinas police behavior, and the clown Kachinas expose human pretension through comedy. When a man wears the Kachina mask, he becomes the spirit, not merely a representation. The tihu (Kachina dolls) given to children are teaching objects to help them learn to recognize each Kachina. The Kachinas emerged from the underworld with the people and taught them how to live properly.
source: Various Pueblo oral traditions; Wikipedia: Kachina; Susanne and Jake Page publications

title: The Origin of Corn (Pueblo)
summary: In Pueblo mythology, corn was a gift from the Holy People. The origin story varies between communities but commonly involves a culture hero or deity who obtained corn from the underworld or from supernatural beings. In one version, the Corn Maidens (Blue Corn Maiden, Yellow Corn Maiden, etc.) brought corn to the people. In another, a boy discovered corn growing in a hidden place and brought it to his village. The sacred nature of corn is reflected in its inclusion in nearly every Pueblo ceremony. The different colors of corn (white, yellow, blue, red, black, and speckled) each have spiritual significance and are associated with the six directions. The successful growth of corn depends not only on farming skill but on the proper performance of ceremonies and the people's moral conduct. Corn is understood as a sentient being with which humans have a reciprocal relationship — humans care for corn, and corn nourishes humans.
source: Various Pueblo oral traditions; Frank Cushing, Zuni Folk Tales

title: The Jicarilla Apache Creation
summary: In the beginning there was nothing — no earth, no living beings, only darkness, water, and Cyclone the wind. Only the Hactcin (supernatural beings) existed. They made the earth as a woman facing upward and the sky as a man facing downward. Black Hactcin, the most powerful, created the first four-legged animal from clay, then made many animals from its body. He made birds from mud mixed with raindrops. When the animals and birds asked for a companion, Black Hactcin created the first man from turquoise, coral, opal, jet, abalone, and other precious materials brought by the animals. Four times Black Hactcin commanded the man to speak, laugh, and shout, and each time he did. Man dreamed of a woman when lice were placed on his head, and he awoke to find Ancestral Woman beside him. They had children and the people multiplied, living in darkness until the sun and moon were created.
source: Morris Edward Opler, Myths and Tales of the Jicarilla Apache Indians (1938); World History Encyclopedia

title: The Emergence (Jicarilla Apache)
summary: In the Jicarilla Apache emergence story, White God of the East made a mountain that grew toward the sky and caused reeds to stand in the center. Four girls twisted the reeds, stopping their growth. Four ladders were made — black, blue, yellow, and variegated — and placed at the mountain. Whirlwind went to the upper world and found much water. Beaver built dams, Badger followed him, and they reported the land was exposed. The people climbed the ladders, starting with black, then blue, then yellow, and finally variegated. After the last ladder wore out, an old woman and old man remained behind. 'You will come back here to me,' one called after the departing people, establishing that the dead return to the underworld. The people moved east along four parallel trails under four chiefs, and leadership alternated between fortunate and unfortunate. They finally settled near Taos. The two who remained became the rulers of the dead.
source: Pliny Earle Goddard, Jicarilla Apache Texts (1911)

title: Holy Boy Makes the Sun and Moon
summary: In Apache tradition, Holy Boy was unhappy with the darkness in the underworld and decided to create a sun. He tried many materials without success. Cyclone, spying on the Hactcin, told Holy Boy that White Hactcin had the sun. Holy Boy went to White Hactcin who gave him the sun, and to Black Hactcin who gave him the moon. Black Hactcin taught Holy Boy how to make a sacred sandpainting on a buckskin to hold the sun and moon. Holy Boy, Red Boy, Black Hactcin, and White Hactcin held a ceremony with songs, and White Hactcin released the sun while Black Hactcin released the moon. Light grew stronger as the sun moved from north to south, becoming daylight. The medicine people immediately claimed credit for making the sun, arguing with each other. The Hactcin warned them about eclipses. When an eclipse came, the medicine people could not restore the sun — only the Hactcin could, teaching that humans should not claim divine powers.
source: Morris Edward Opler, Myths and Tales of the Jicarilla Apache Indians (1938); nativeamerican-art.com

title: The Apache Origin of the Animals
summary: After the Apaches emerged from the underworld, they traveled south for four days with only seeds to eat. A Raven brought a quiver with meat to a teepee, and when children ate the meat they became instantly fat. The Raven's meat was stolen, and he flew to his eastern mountain home. An Apache boy was transformed into a puppy to spy on the Ravens. Hidden in a young raven's blanket, the puppy watched as an old raven removed a flat stone, revealing an opening to the underworld. Each evening, a buffalo emerged and was eaten. After four days, the boy resumed human shape, descended into the underworld with white and black feathers, and claimed the buffalo. All the animals followed him to the Apache camp. But an old grandmother struck a deer's nose with a fire stick, leaving a white mark and decreeing that animals would avoid humans. Thus the harmony between humans and animals ended, and deer now use their noses to detect humans from afar.
source: Morris Edward Opler, Myths and Tales of the Jicarilla Apache Indians (1938); Margot Edmonds and Ella Clark, Voices of the Winds (2021)

title: The Gahe: Apache Mountain Spirit Dancers
summary: The Gahe (called Gaan by Western Apache) are the Mountain Spirits, powerful supernatural beings who live inside sacred mountains. The first Mountain Spirit ceremony originated when children of the Sun taught the Apache how to call and honor these spirits. The Mountain Spirits appear as masked dancers wearing distinctive tall, wooden-slat headdresses, with black-and-white body paint and black buckskin kilts. The Gahe ceremony is part of the Girls' Puberty Ceremony (Sunrise Ceremony), the longest and richest Apache ritual. Four Mountain Spirits typically dance together — one for each cardinal direction — along with a clown figure. The dancers move in a syncopated, swaying rhythm to singing and drumming, tracing sacred patterns in the dance ground with their feet. The Gahe are believed to possess healing powers and can cure illness, drive away evil influences, and bring blessings. Sierra Blanca (White Mountain) in New Mexico is considered the spiritual origin place of many Mountain Spirit dance groups.
source: Hoijer, Chiricahua Apache Texts (1938); openpublishing.psu.edu dissertation on Gahe/Gaan

title: White Painted Woman and Child of the Water (Chiricahua Apache)
summary: In Chiricahua and Mescalero Apache tradition, the creation begins not with an emergence from the underworld but with a great flood that destroyed a malevolent world. After the flood, White Painted Woman (Changing Woman) appeared. She conceived Child of the Water (also known as Killer-of-Enemies in Lipan tradition) from the rays of the Sun. Child of the Water became the dominant culture hero who rids the world of monsters. He is sometimes described as the Sun himself in some versions, and his younger brother is the Wise One. White Painted Woman is identified with the Moon and Thunder. The Girls' Puberty Ceremony reenacts White Painted Woman's first menstruation, when she received her power from the Sun. The ceremony lasts four days and includes the Mountain Spirit dance, blessing the young woman with health, fertility, and a long life, just as White Painted Woman was blessed.
source: Morris Edward Opler, Myths and Tales of the Chiricahua Apache Indians (1994); nativeamerican-art.com

title: The Lipan Apache Killer-of-Enemies
summary: In Lipan Apache tradition, Killer-of-Enemies is the primary creator and culture hero, identified with the Sun. Killer-of-Enemies seems synonymous with Child-of-the-Water, and he is considered the child of Changing Woman (who is identified as the Moon and Thunder). The Lipan emergence story includes Killer-of-Enemies creating the world and fighting monsters. He has a younger brother called Wise One, who assists him. Killer-of-Enemies journeyed through the world, establishing the proper order of things, teaching the Apache how to hunt, conduct ceremonies, and live properly. He defeated powerful enemies including giant animals and spirits who threatened the people. His battles established the sacred geography of the Lipan Apache world. After completing his work, Killer-of-Enemies departed, promising to return if the people ever truly needed him. The Lipan version shares similarities with the Navajo Hero Twins tradition but emphasizes the role of the single Sun-figure more strongly.
source: Morris Edward Opler, Lipan Apache narratives; nativeamerican-art.com

title: The White Mountain Apache Creation
summary: In the White Mountain Apache creation myth from Noze (recorded in 1910), a maiden went to the top of a high mountain where the rays of the rising Sun first struck. She raised her skirt and the breath of the Sun entered her. She went up the mountain four mornings, and four times the Sun's breath entered her. This unmarried girl became pregnant, and the people gossiped about her. She gave birth to Naiyenezgani (Monster Slayer) and Tobatc'istcini (Child of the Water). The twins grew rapidly and were given weapons by their father the Sun. They killed the monsters threatening the people, including Naiye' who devoured humans. After proving their power by cutting a tree in half with a thrown weapon, they assured the people that no more monsters would trouble them. They established that a messenger would circle the earth in one day to keep them informed. The twins then resumed their roles as protectors of the people and warriors against evil.
source: Pliny Earle Goddard, Myths and Tales from the White Mountain Apache (1911/Project Gutenberg)

title: The Awonawilona Creation (Zuni)
summary: In the Zuni creation myth recorded by Frank Cushing, only Áwonawílona, the All-Father, Maker and Container of All, existed in the beginning. He made himself into the Sun Father (Yatoka). The mist clouds formed into the Great Waters, from which Earth Mother (Áwitelin Tsíta) and Sky Father (Ápoyan Ta'chu) emerged. Together they conceived all men and creatures in the four-fold womb of the world. The Sun Father and Earth Mother brought forth the Twin Children of the Sun — the brothers Ko'wituma and Wats'usi (the Ahayuta twins) — who were given sacred knowledge, caps, bows, arrows, and shields to have dominion over all beings. The twins descended into the first underworld (Ánosin Téhuli) and formed a ladder for people to ascend to the second cave-world (Moss World, K'ólin Téhuli), then to the third (World of Mud, Áwisho Téhuli), and finally the fourth cave-world (Wing World, Tépahaian Téhuli), where the twins taught men the ways of life before they entered the Daylight World (Ték'ohaian Úlahnane).
source: Frank Cushing, Outlines of Zuni Creation Myths (1896); The Mythic World of the Zuni (1988)

title: The Zuni People Emerge into Daylight
summary: In Ruth Benedict's version of the Zuni emergence, people dwelt in total darkness deep in the fourth world. Awonawilona, the Sun, took pity on them. His two sons, the bow priests, located the opening to the fourth world in the southwest and led the people upward. They planted seeds that grew into pine, spruce, silver spruce, and aspen. Each tree became a prayer stick stretched to the next world. The people climbed through four worlds, each time finding themselves still incomplete. They had no mouths, anuses, or fingers; their hands and feet were webbed, and they had horns and tails. In the first world (the one below daylight), they could finally see — they were covered in filth and slime. The bow priests cut mouths into their faces with a knife sharpened on a red whetstone, then cut anuses with a soot-blackened knife. They cut fingers and toes, and finally removed the people's horns and tails. When the people emerged into sunlight at last, they cried from the brightness, and sunflowers grew where their tears fell.
source: Ruth Benedict, Zuni Mythology Volume 1 (1935)

title: The Trial of Lovers: Maiden of Matsaki
summary: A Zuni folk tale about the beautiful Maiden of Matsaki, desired by many suitors. After the war god Áhaiyúta and his brother courted her, they discovered she was cruel — she had let the children of Field-mice die of cold while she gambled. The gods punished her but also showed compassion for the helpless mouse children, taking them to their grandmother who raised them. The story teaches that even gods can be cruel, but those who find helpless children must care for them. The Maiden of Matsaki's story illustrates Zuni values about compassion, the consequences of cruelty, and the duty to care for orphans and vulnerable children. It also demonstrates that even divine beings are subject to moral judgment and that mercy can follow justice.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: The Youth and His Eagle
summary: A Zuni story about a young boy who raised an eagle and developed a deep spiritual bond with the bird. The eagle became his protector and provider, bringing him gifts from the sky world. However, jealous members of the village conspired against the boy and his eagle. The eagle, being a sacred creature connected to the Sun Father, possessed supernatural powers and could communicate with the spirit world. Through his relationship with the eagle, the boy learned important spiritual lessons about power, responsibility, and the consequences of greed. The eagle eventually returned to the sky world, but it left the boy with sacred knowledge that benefited his people. The story emphasizes the Zuni belief in the spiritual power of birds and the importance of maintaining proper relationships with animal spirits.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: The Poor Turkey Girl
summary: A Zuni Cinderella-like story about a poor girl who was mistreated by her community and found companionship among the wild turkeys. The turkeys, taking pity on her, transformed her appearance with beautiful clothing and feathers so she could attend a sacred dance at the village. She danced beautifully and won the heart of a young man, but had to flee before dawn to return the turkeys' gifts. She lost one of her turkey-feather garments, which the young man used to find her. However, when the turkeys discovered she had not properly thanked them or kept her promises, they abandoned her and took back their gifts. The girl was left with nothing, learning that ingratitude and broken promises have consequences. The story teaches Zuni values of humility, gratitude, and keeping one's word.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: How the Summer Birds Came
summary: A Zuni myth explaining the origin of small summer birds. In the days of the ancients, there were no little birds — only large birds like eagles, crows, and hawks. A Zuni man discovered a magical bundle of feathers left by the spirits. When he walked into a field of flowers, the bundle began to flutter, and small wings flew out from it, becoming the Sacred Birds of Summerland. The feathers were made living by the lives of the birds that had once borne them, and they came to life again in the environment of flowers and summer that they had loved. This is why, according to Zuni tradition, there are jaybirds, sparrows, finches, willow-birds, and all the beautiful little birds that herald summer and hover over flowers. The story concludes with the storyteller noting that Zunis have books too — not with marks but with words in their hearts from the ancients.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: The Serpent of the Sea (Kolowisi)
summary: A Zuni legend about Kolowisi, the great horned water serpent that lives in the sacred lake. A maiden from Zuni went to the lake and was seen by Kolowisi, who took her as his bride beneath the waters. She lived with the serpent in his underwater home, where there was no light but she was treated well and given abundance. The people mourned her loss and held ceremonies to bring her back. Eventually, Kolowisi allowed her to return to the surface, but she was transformed and carried spiritual power from her time in the serpent's realm. She brought knowledge of healing ceremonies and water-songs back to the Zuni people. Kolowisi is generally a benevolent figure, associated with water, fertility, and the life-giving forces of the natural world. The serpent's horn symbolizes power, and offerings are made to Kolowisi during ceremonies asking for rain.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901); various Zuni oral traditions

title: The Maiden of the Yellow Rocks
summary: A Zuni story about the beautiful daughter of the high priest of the Village of the Yellow Rocks. Despite her beauty, her pride and coldness drove away suitors. The twin war gods Áhaiyúta and Mátsailéma, living with their grandmother on Thunder Mountain, decided to court her. They appeared as ugly pygmies — boys who never grew to manhood. Meanwhile, the Field-mice, who were prolific and needed wool for their families, gambled with the gods. The mice lost everything, including the wool for their nests, and their babies died of cold. The war gods, despite their terrible nature, took the orphaned mouse babies to their grandmother, who raised them. The story teaches the Zuni value of caring for helpless children — if even the cruelest gods showed mercy, surely humans must care for abandoned infants and orphans.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: The Foster-Child of the Deer
summary: A Zuni story about a child abandoned in the wilderness who was adopted and raised by deer. The deer taught the child to live in harmony with nature, to understand the language of animals, and to know the sacred plants of the forest. When the child grew up and encountered human hunters, a conflict arose between the human and deer worlds. The deer-mother bid farewell to her foster-child, who was torn between two worlds — the human world of their birth and the deer world of their raising. The story teaches respect for deer and the proper relationship between hunters and prey. It is connected to Zuni hunting rituals and the belief that deer willingly give themselves to respectful hunters who perform the correct ceremonies and prayers. The foster-child became a mediator between humans and deer, teaching people the rituals that honor the spirits of slain animals.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: The Boy Hunter Who Never Sacrificed to the Deer
summary: A Zuni story about a boy who was an excellent hunter but never performed the proper sacrifices or prayers to the spirits of the deer he killed. As a result, the deer spirits became angry. The boy's disrespectful hunting caused supernatural consequences — his kills became tainted, and he began to have bad luck. The spirits of the deer he had killed without proper ceremony confronted him. To make amends, the boy had to learn the proper rituals of the hunt, including making prayer sticks, offering cornmeal, and speaking words of gratitude to the deer's spirit. This story explains the origin of the Society of Rattlesnakes, which has the power to heal snakebites and conduct ceremonies that maintain balance between humans and animals. The story reinforces the Zuni teaching that all life is sacred and must be taken with gratitude and proper ceremony.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: Átahsaia the Cannibal Demon
summary: A Zuni legend about Átahsaia, a giant cannibal demon who terrorized the people. Átahsaia is described as a huge, monstrous being with burning eyes and an insatiable hunger for human flesh, especially children. The demon would descend from the mountains to raid villages, carrying off victims in a large basket on its back. The twin war gods Áhaiyúta and Mátsailéma confronted Átahsaia to save their people. They used their divine weapons and cunning to defeat the demon. In some versions, they tricked Átahsaia into falling into a pit of fire or into a canyon. The battle between the divine twins and the cannibal demon represents the eternal struggle between good and evil, order and chaos. After Átahsaia's defeat, the people were safe, but the story reminds Zuni children to be cautious and to respect the boundary between the civilized pueblo and the wild lands where dangerous spirits dwell.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901); Wikipedia: Zuni mythology

title: How the Coyote Joined the Dance of the Burrowing-Owls
summary: A Zuni trickster tale about Coyote who stumbled upon a sacred dance of the Burrowing-owls. The owls were performing a ceremonial dance in the moonlight, and Coyote, ever the opportunist, wanted to join. At first the owls were suspicious, but Coyote insisted he could dance as well as they. He joined their circle, but his clumsy, undisciplined dancing disrupted the sacred ceremony. The owls, annoyed by his foolish behavior, played a trick on him. When Coyote tired and wanted to leave, he found that the owls' dance had placed him under a spell — he could not stop dancing. He was forced to dance until dawn, exhausted and humiliated. The story explains why coyotes are often seen running in circles or acting strangely at night. It teaches the Zuni lesson that not all dances are for everyone and that sacred ceremonies require proper preparation and respect.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: The Coyote and the Locust
summary: A Zuni trickster story about Coyote who heard Locust singing and playing his flute on a piñon branch. Coveting the song, Coyote demanded Locust teach it to him. Locust, wary of Coyote's intentions, tricked him by shedding his skin and placing a stone inside the empty shell on the branch. When Coyote returned and Locust refused to sing again, Coyote angrily bit the locust shell — but it was actually a stone wrapped in skin. Coyote broke his teeth, which is why coyotes have broken-looking teeth to this day. The story explains both the physical characteristic of coyotes and warns against anger, impatience, and not minding one's own business. Locusts, for their part, still sometimes shed their skins and leave their likenesses on trees to fool predators.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: The Prairie-Dogs and Their Priest the Burrowing-Owl
summary: A Zuni story about a village of Prairie-dogs who were suffering from a drought. They held a council and decided to ask their grandfather, the Burrowing-owl who lived on top of their mountain, for help. The Burrowing-owl, a powerful priest, agreed to intercede with the rain gods. He collected a foul-smelling beetle and instructed his wife to cook beans. He placed the beetle and beans in a bag and climbed to the top of his mountain. As the storm clouds gathered, the Owl struck the bag with a stick, releasing such a terrible odor that even the Rain-gods themselves fled in retreat. The clouds scattered and the sky cleared. The Prairie-dogs emerged from their holes and cheered their grandfather, the Burrowing-owl. The story explains why burrowing-owls make their nests in prairie-dog holes — they have been friends ever since. The Burrowing-owl's control over the weather demonstrates the Zuni belief that spiritual power can reside in unexpected beings.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: How Áhaiyúta and Mátsailéma Stole the Thunder-Stone and Lightning-Shaft
summary: In this Zuni myth, the twin war gods Áhaiyúta and Mátsailéma acquired the powerful weapons of thunder and lightning. The thunder-stone and lightning-shaft were kept by powerful beings who did not want to share their power. The twins, known for their cunning and bravery, devised a plan to steal these sacred weapons. Using their shapeshifting abilities and the help of their grandmother (Spider Woman often fills this role), they infiltrated the home of the thunder beings. They stole the thunder-stone (which creates thunder when struck) and the lightning-shaft (which shoots bolts of lightning). These weapons made them invincible in battle and allowed them to defeat enemies too powerful for ordinary weapons. The acquisition of thunder and lightning represents the transfer of cosmic power to the heroes who protect the Zuni people and maintain order in the world.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: The Giant Cloud-Swallower
summary: A Zuni story about a giant being who swallowed clouds, causing drought and suffering among the people. With the clouds gone, no rain fell, and the crops withered. The people performed ceremonies and made prayer offerings, but nothing worked. The twin war gods took action, pursuing the Giant Cloud-swallower across the sky. They shot their lightning arrows at the giant, who was so large he could be seen against the horizon. The battle raged across the heavens. Finally, the twins struck the giant in the throat, causing him to release all the clouds he had swallowed. Rain poured down upon the parched earth, and the people rejoiced. The dying giant's body became the first rainbows and his scattered bones became the clouds that still drift across the sky. The story reinforces the Zuni understanding of weather phenomena as the actions of spiritual beings and the ongoing role of the war gods as protectors.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: The Maiden the Sun Made Love To
summary: A Zuni story about a maiden whom the Sun desired. The Sun came to her in human form and courted her, and she gave birth to twin boys — the ancestors of the Zuni war gods. The Sun's attention to the maiden brought both blessing and burden. The boys grew rapidly and displayed extraordinary abilities. They became the first culture heroes, teaching their people how to hunt, farm, and perform ceremonies. The story explains the origin of anger in the world — the maiden's anger at the Sun's departure (or at the gossip of the villagers) introduced this emotion to humanity. This origin myth establishes that both positive and negative emotions are part of the human condition and that even divine parentage does not exempt one from human struggles. The twins' journey to find their father the Sun and receive his blessing mirrors the Navajo Hero Twins tradition.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: The Hermit Mitsina
summary: A Zuni story about Mitsina, a hermit who lived in seclusion with spiritual power. Mitsina had withdrawn from human society to live in a remote cave, where he communed with the spirits and gained deep knowledge of the sacred world. When the people faced a crisis, they sought him out for his wisdom and power. Mitsina, despite his isolation, felt compassion for his people and agreed to help. He used his spiritual knowledge to perform ceremonies and prayers that resolved the village's problems. Afterward, he withdrew again to his cave, teaching that spiritual power requires solitude and discipline to maintain. The story presents the Zuni concept of the shaman or medicine person who lives between worlds — part of the human community yet separate, possessing power that comes from sacrifice and isolation.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: The Kachina Tradition (Zuni)
summary: In Zuni tradition, the Kachinas (Kâkâ) originated from the search for itiwana, the Middle Place. The priest Ka'wimosa sent his sons in different directions to find the center of the world. Kiaklo went north, the Ánahoho áchi went south, and Síweluhsiwa and Síwiluhsitsa went east, where they had ten children from an incestuous union — the first Koyemshi (Mudheads), sacred clowns who speak idocies yet utter prophecies. Meanwhile, the Zuni divided into groups led by the Beloved Twins. Children who fell into the river during a crossing became lizards, frogs, turtles, and newts — the lost Little Ones who learned the Dance of Good (Kókokshi) from the god priests of Kóthluwalawan. Duck brought Kiaklo to the council hall where he learned the story of creation and the sacred dances. He returned to teach the Zuni the Kachina ceremonies, the masks, costumes, and rituals that connect the living with the spirits and the ancestors. The masks are considered so sacred that they guarantee the owner's admission to the dance house of the gods after death.
source: Frank Cushing, Outlines of Zuni Creation Myths (1896); Ruth Bunzel, Introduction to Zuni Ceremonialism

title: Yanauluha Brings Medicine and Agriculture
summary: In Zuni mythology, Yanauluha is the benevolent culture hero who brought the arts of medicine and agriculture to the people. Unlike trickster figures in some traditions, Yanauluha is a dignified, wise teacher. After the emergence into the daylight world, the people were struggling to survive. Yanauluha appeared among them carrying seeds, digging sticks, and sacred objects. He taught them which plants were edible and medicinal, how to plant corn, beans, and squash together in hills, when to plant by observing the stars and the position of the sun, and how to prepare and store food. He also taught them how to use plants for healing — which roots, leaves, and barks cured various illnesses. Yanauluha established the pattern of the agricultural year and the ceremonies that accompany planting and harvest. He is associated with the directions and with the sacred mountains that surround the Zuni world. After teaching the people, Yanauluha departed, but his knowledge continues through Zuni farmers and medicine people.
source: Frank Cushing, Outlines of Zuni Creation Myths (1896); various Zuni oral traditions

title: Coyote and Eagle Steal the Sun and Moon
summary: A Zuni story explaining the origin of day and night and the changing seasons. In the beginning, the Sun and Moon were kept locked in boxes by a powerful being who would not release them. The world was in perpetual twilight. Coyote and Eagle, two of the most clever animals, devised a plan. Eagle flew to the being's home with Coyote hidden in his feathers. While the being slept, Coyote crept out, opened the boxes, and Eagle seized the Sun and Moon with his talons. They fled, pursued by the angry being. Eagle rose higher and higher into the sky, placing the Sun and Moon in their proper positions. But in the chaos, Coyote dropped the box containing half the daylight, which shattered. This is why days are shorter in winter than in summer, and why daylight decreases after the summer solstice. The story reflects the Zuni understanding of astronomical cycles and the partnership between sky and earth creatures.
source: Native-languages.org; various Zuni oral traditions

title: The Corn Maidens (Zuni)
summary: In Zuni tradition, the Corn Maidens are sacred beings who embody the spirit of corn. They are associated with Paiyatuma, the god of the dawn and of music, and with the Kachina society. According to the myth, Paiyatuma courted the Corn Maidens, who were beautiful but shy. He played his flute for them, and they danced. Through this union, the sacred connection between corn and the people was established. The Corn Maidens taught the Zuni women how to grind corn, make piki bread, and prepare corn ceremonially. Each color of corn has its own maiden — Blue Corn Maiden, Yellow Corn Maiden, White Corn Maiden, Red Corn Maiden, Black Corn Maiden, and Speckled Corn Maiden. During ceremonies, the Corn Maidens are represented by Kachina dancers who carry ears of corn and bless the fields. The story of the Corn Maidens is central to Zuni agricultural ceremonies and reinforces the sacredness of corn as a gift from the spirit world.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901); Ruth Bunzel, Zuni Ceremonialism

title: The Cock and the Mouse (Zuni)
summary: A Zuni folktale of the hybrid type, combining Native and European story elements. The Cock (rooster) and the Mouse were friends living together. Mouse was industrious, gathering food and maintaining their home, while Cock was boastful and lazy. When danger came in the form of a cat or predator, Cock's crowing alerted everyone, but his bravado failed when actual confrontation occurred. Mouse, though small, used cleverness to escape danger. The story humorously contrasts the small, practical Mouse with the showy but ineffective Cock. It teaches that real value lies not in appearance or loudness but in practical skills and quiet diligence. Like many Zuni animal tales, it contains elements of social commentary, humor, and moral instruction wrapped in an engaging narrative.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: The How the Twins of War and Chance Fared with the Unborn-Made Men
summary: In this complex Zuni myth, Áhaiyúta and Mátsailéma, the Twins of War and Chance (or Morning Star and Evening Star), journeyed to the underworld to confront the Unborn-made Men. These were unfinished beings who had not yet been given their final form — they existed in a state of potential. The twins had to navigate the dark underworld, using their wits and divine weapons. They encountered various spirits and obstacles, including the guardians of the underworld. The story explores themes of destiny, free will, and the nature of creation — why some beings are completed and others are not. The twins' journey through the underworld mirrors the soul's journey after death and the process by which spirits become ancestors. The Unborn-made Men represent the untold potential that exists in every being before it takes its final form in the daylight world.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: The Warrior Suitor of Moki (Hopi)
summary: A Zuni story about a warrior from Zuni who traveled to the land of the Hopi (called Moki in Zuni) to win a bride. The warrior had to prove his worth through feats of strength, courage, and skill. He faced challenges set by the Hopi elders, including hunting dangerous game, competing in races and games, and demonstrating his knowledge of proper ceremonial conduct. Through his bravery and skill — and with the help of the war gods — he succeeded in all the trials and won the maiden. The marriage between the Zuni warrior and the Hopi woman established ties between the two nations. The story reflects historical relationships between the Zuni and Hopi peoples, who share similar cultural roots and maintain trade and marriage connections. It also illustrates the Zuni values of courage, perseverance, and cross-cultural respect.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: How the Rattlesnakes Came to Be What They Are
summary: A Zuni story explaining the origin of rattlesnakes and their relationship with humans. In the days of the ancients, rattlesnakes did not have their distinctive rattle or their dangerous venom. A man who had been saved by a rattlesnake or had made a pact with one became the founder of the Rattlesnake Society. The snake was given its rattle as a warning device so that humans could hear it and avoid being bitten. The venom was given as a defense but also carries healing power in the hands of those who know the proper ceremonies. The story teaches that rattlesnakes are not merely dangerous creatures but sacred beings with a role in the spiritual ecology of the world. Members of the Rattlesnake Society can handle rattlesnakes without harm and use them in healing ceremonies. The rattle is understood as a gift — a way for the snake to say 'I am here, please step carefully.'
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: The Two Little Ones and Their Turkeys
summary: A Zuni story about two orphaned children who were cared for by a flock of wild turkeys. The turkeys provided them with feathers for warmth, eggs for food, and protection from danger. The children lived in harmony with the turkeys, learning the ways of the forest and the language of birds. When the children grew older, they faced pressure to return to human society, but the turkeys warned them about the dangers of human greed. Eventually, the children had to choose between the freedom of the wild and the community of humans. The story explores themes of adoption — being raised by non-human caretakers — and the tension between wild and civilized life. The turkeys, like the deer in other Zuni stories, represent the generosity of the natural world and the reciprocal obligations between humans and animals.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901)

title: White Buffalo Calf Woman and the Sacred Pipe
summary: During a time of great famine, two Lakota scouts encountered a mysterious woman in white buckskin. One scout approached her with lustful intent and was reduced to bones. The woman instructed the remaining scout to prepare the tribe. She gave the people the chanupa (sacred pipe) made of red stone carved with a buffalo calf, taught them the Seven Sacred Rites including the Sun Dance and vision quest, and showed them how to pray to Wakan Tanka. Before departing she transformed through four colors—black, red, brown, and white—becoming a white buffalo calf who promised to return when the people needed her most.
source: Wikipedia / Lakota Times / World History Encyclopedia

title: Iktomi the Spider Trickster
summary: Iktomi is the spider trickster of Lakota mythology, born fully grown with the body of a spider and the cunning of a man. He is known for his clever schemes and predictions, but his selfishness and mischievous nature often backfire. In one tale, Iktomi tried to keep a pot of fish soup for himself rather than share with a visitor, devising a race trick that ultimately failed. In another, Iktomi angered a boulder and was crushed, only to be freed by bats who were turned into stones. Despite his flaws, Iktomi is considered wise and his stories teach moral lessons about generosity and respect.
source: Wikipedia / Readmio / ALA

title: Unhcegila the Serpent Monster
summary: Unhcegila was a gigantic serpent monster who wreaked havoc upon the land, devouring anyone who stumbled upon her. A red crystal in the seventh spot on her head functioned as her heart and granted its bearer great power. In one version, a warrior from the Bear Clan, guided by a Weasel spirit, allowed himself to be devoured then cut his way out, freeing other victims. In another, two brothers—one blind—killed her with arrows from a medicine woman. A third version tells of Unhcegila emerging from primordial waters, only to be struck down by Wakinyan the Thunderbird whose lightning shattered her heart, scattering her bones across the Badlands.
source: Wikipedia / Native American Mythology A to Z

title: The Lakota Creation: Inyan, Maka, and the First Dawn
summary: In the beginning there was only Inyan (Rock) and the omnipresent spirit Wakan Tanka, the Great Mystery, and the darkness Han. Inyan wished to exercise his powers of compassion, so he created Maka (Earth) from himself, sacrificing much of his blood which became water. Inyan shriveled and lost his powers. The water gave birth to Skan, who became the supreme judge. When Maka complained of cold and darkness, Inyan created Anpo the Dawn and later Wi the Sun. Maka tried to separate from Inyan, but Skan ruled that rock and soil must remain bound together, explaining why rocks are embedded in the earth.
source: Wikipedia

title: The Thunderbird Wakinyan
summary: Wakinyan is the great Thunderbird of Lakota mythology, a powerful sky spirit associated with thunder, lightning, and storms. His flapping wings create thunderclaps and his eyes flash lightning. When Unhcegila emerged from the primordial waters to flood the land, Wakinyan flapped his wings to dry the earth and shot lightning to destroy the serpent's heart. The Thunderbird represents the destructive yet renewing power of storms and is considered one of the most potent spirits. Wakinyan is also associated with the west direction and is a protector of the Lakota people against chaos and disorder.
source: Wikipedia

title: The Great Race of the Animals
summary: The Great Race is a Lakota legend about a competition between the four-legged animals and the two-legged birds to determine who would eat whom. The race was proposed by Ksapela the Wise One to settle conflicts among the creatures. Representing the four-leggeds was the buffalo, while the magpie represented the two-leggeds. As the race progressed, various animals helped their champion. The buffalo was winning until the magpie hitched a ride on his back, then flew ahead at the last moment. The Great Spirit declared the race a draw, decreeing that henceforth the two-leggeds would hunt the four-leggeds but must always show respect for their sacrifice.
source: Wikipedia

title: The Happy Hunting Ground
summary: The Lakota believe the soul journeys after death to the happy hunting ground, a realm resembling the living world with better weather and more plentiful animals that are easier to hunt. Some traditions say the soul travels along the Sky-Road (Milky Way) to reach this destination. At the entrance, the deceased must present proper tattoos to Hihankara (Owl-Maker). Those with correct tattoos are admitted; those without are pushed back to Earth to wander as restless ghosts. The dead may also travel along the Spirit Path, another name for the Milky Way, to reach their final resting place among the stars.
source: Wikipedia

title: Iktomi and the Boulder
summary: Iktomi the spider trickster wrapped himself in a fine robe and went walking. He came upon a large boulder and, feeling generous, offered it his robe. The boulder declined, but Iktomi insisted and placed the robe upon it. Later, when Iktomi wanted his robe back, the boulder refused. Iktomi grew angry and insulted the boulder, which chased him and crushed him flat. Bats came to help Iktomi, pecking at the boulder until it cracked open, freeing him. But the bats' faces were smashed flat in the effort, and Iktomi—ever ungrateful—scattered pebbles that became the stones covering the plains.
source: ALA / Paul Goble collection

title: The Seven Sacred Rites
summary: White Buffalo Calf Woman gave the Lakota people the Seven Sacred Rites that would guide their spiritual life. The rites are: 1) Keeping of the Soul (preserving the spirit of the deceased for a year before release); 2) The Purification Rite (sweat lodge ceremony for cleansing); 3) Crying for a Vision (vision quest for guidance); 4) The Sun Dance (annual renewal ceremony with sacrifice and dancing); 5) The Making of Relatives (adoption ceremony); 6) The Girls Coming of Age (womanhood ceremony); and 7) The Throwing of the Ball (symbolizing the Creator's love). Each rite uses the sacred pipe given by the White Buffalo Calf Woman.
source: World History Encyclopedia / Lakota Times

title: Lakota Star Husband Story
summary: Two Lakota girls lay outside their tipi one night, and one pointed at the brightest star saying she wished to marry it. The next day the girls found themselves in the sky world, where the first girl became the wife of a star. She was told not to dig around a certain turnip-like root, but curiosity overcame her. When she pulled the root, she saw the Earth far below through the hole. Homesick, she and her husband wove a rope to descend, but it was too short and she fell. She survived and returned to her people, but the star husband remained in the sky and can be seen as the evening star.
source: Native American folklore collections

title: The Blackfoot Creation: Apistotoke, Na'pi, and the World
summary: Apistotoke (Ihtsipatapiyohpa), the Great Spirit, created first the Sky Beings: Naato'si the Sun, Ko'komiki'somm the Moon, and the stars including Aapisowaahs the Morning Star. He then created Ksahkomitapi (Earth) as mother to all creation. Apistotoke created Na'pi (Old Man), the first man and demigod who shaped the world with his wife Kipitaakii (Old Lady). Using mud collected by Turtle during a great flood, Na'pi designed the earth, then created men, women, animals, and plants. He taught the Blackfoot people which plants to eat and animals to hunt, especially the buffalo.
source: Wikipedia / Maclean 1893

title: Origin of the Wind
summary: Na'pi discovered two bags containing summer and winter. Determined to make the seasons equal, he tried to steal them but failed. He sent a prairie chicken who successfully grabbed the summer bag. The guardians chased the bird, slicing at the grass and cutting the bag open. Strong wind burst forth. Later, Napi burned himself and climbed a mountain to summon cooling wind through medicine-making. The wind blew so fiercely he was nearly thrown off the mountain. He survived by grabbing a birch tree, his fingers leaving marks that gave the birch its distinctive patterned bark visible today.
source: Wikipedia / Maclean 1893

title: Language on a Mountain
summary: After a great flood swept through the land, Na'pi (Old Man) gathered all the people on top of a large mountain. He gave them water of different colors—black, red, yellow, and white—and told them to drink and then speak. Each group that drank the colored water spoke a different language. Only those who received the black water spoke the same language; these were the bands of the Blackfoot Confederacy: the Piegan, Siksika, and Blood (Kainai) people. This event was said to have taken place on the highest mountain in the Montana reservation.
source: Wikipedia / Duvall and Wissler

title: The Legend of Red Coulee
summary: The Blackfoot were told by the Snake First Nations about a powerful medicine stone located between McLeod and Fort Benton, Montana, near the Marias River. Years later, a group of Blackfoot men traveled to find the stone. When they discovered it, their leader laughed at the story, calling it a child's tale, and rolled the stone down the hill. On their return journey, they were ambushed in battle. All but one man were killed. The place became known as Red Coulee, and ever after the Blackfoot would leave offerings when passing it, to ensure safety and protection on their journey.
source: Wikipedia / Maclean 1893

title: Na'pi Creates the Buffalo
summary: After Na'pi (Old Man) created the world and the first people, he saw that they were hungry. He went to a cave deep in the mountains where the buffalo were kept. Na'pi opened the cave and the buffalo poured out across the plains, providing food, clothing, and shelter for the people. He taught the Blackfoot how to conduct buffalo hunts with respect, using every part of the animal and giving thanks to the buffalo spirit. Na'pi emphasized that the buffalo were a gift from Apistotoke and must never be wasted, or they would disappear.
source: Grinnell / Blackfoot folklore

title: The Beaver Bundle Ceremony
summary: The Beaver Bundle is the most sacred medicine bundle of the Blackfoot people, said to have been given by the underwater spirits. According to legend, a Blackfoot man encountered beavers who taught him sacred songs, rituals, and the use of medicinal plants. The bundle contains various sacred objects including animal skins, pipes, and herbs, each with specific powers. The Beaver Bundle ceremony involves elaborate rituals, singing, and smoking the pipe. It is used for healing, ensuring good fortune, and maintaining the balance between the human and spirit worlds. The bundle is passed down through generations with great ceremony.
source: Wissler / Blackfoot ethnography

title: Napi and the Rolling Skull
summary: Na'pi was walking across the prairie when he kicked a buffalo skull and told it to get out of his way. The skull began to roll after him, chasing him across the plains. Na'pi ran to a tree and climbed it, but the skull called to the tree, asking it to bend down. The tree bent, and the skull rolled up to Na'pi's hiding place. Na'pi was trapped and the skull would have killed him, but he tricked it by saying he saw more buffalo over the hill. When the skull turned to look, Na'pi leaped down and escaped.
source: Blackfoot folklore collections

title: The Origin of the Medicine Pipe
summary: A beautiful woman appeared to a Blackfoot man who had gone on a vision quest. She gave him a pipe decorated with eagle feathers and taught him the prayers and songs that went with it. She told him the pipe would be a mediator between the people and the spirit world. When he returned to camp, the elders recognized the pipe's power and established the Medicine Pipe society. The pipe is central to Blackfoot ceremonies, used for healing, making peace, and communicating with Apistotoke. Its keeper must follow strict taboos and conduct annual renewal ceremonies.
source: Grinnell / Blackfoot oral tradition

title: Sweet Medicine and the Four Sacred Arrows
summary: Sweet Medicine (Motsé'eóeve) was the great prophet and law-giver of the Cheyenne people. He journeyed to Nóávóse (Bear Butte) in the Black Hills where Ma'heo'o (the Wise One Above) gave him the four sacred arrows—the Maahótse. Two arrows were for hunting, to ensure success in finding buffalo, and two were for warfare, to protect the people from enemies. Sweet Medicine also received the structure of Cheyenne government, the Council of Forty-Four peace chiefs, the military societies, and the system of legal justice. He predicted the coming of horses, white men, and the loss of the buffalo. He also invented the hoop-and-stick game (chunkey).
source: World History Encyclopedia / Wikipedia

title: Erect Horns and the Sacred Buffalo Hat
summary: Erect Horns (Tomȯsévėséhe) was the prophet of the Só'taeo'o people who later merged with the Cheyenne. He received the Ésevone (Sacred Buffalo Hat) at Tȯhóonévose (Stone Hammer Mountain) near the Great Lakes in Minnesota. The sacred hat represents female power, while the Sacred Arrows represent male power—together they form the two great covenants of the Cheyenne Nation. Erect Horns gave the people the Sun Dance ceremony and convinced the tribe to abandon their sedentary agricultural lifestyle, adopt the nomadic Plains horse culture, and rely on buffalo for survival. His vision transformed the Cheyenne from earth-lodge dwellers to tipi-dwelling Plains warriors.
source: Wikipedia / Cheyenne oral tradition

title: Cheyenne Creation Story
summary: In the beginning, all was water and nothing existed but the Creator Ma'heo'o and aquatic birds. The Creator sent various waterbirds—the loon, otter, beaver, and turtle—to dive to the bottom of the endless water to bring up mud. After several failures, the turtle succeeded in bringing up the primordial mud. From this mud, the Creator formed the earth. Then he created grass, trees, animals, and finally human beings. The Cheyenne emerged from beneath the earth onto the surface, guided by the Creator. The story teaches that all creation is interconnected and that humans must respect the earth as their mother.
source: World History Encyclopedia

title: The Pawnee Capture of the Sacred Arrows
summary: Around 1830, the Cheyenne attacked a Skidi Pawnee hunting party in central Nebraska, carrying their most sacred possession—the Maahótse (Four Sacred Arrows) bundle. During the battle, the Pawnee captured the sacred arrows, described as the greatest disaster in Cheyenne history. The Cheyenne made replica arrows and desperately tried to recover the originals. They recovered one arrow directly from the Pawnee; the Lakota captured another and returned it for horses. The replicas were ceremonially returned to Bear Butte. This loss was believed to have been caused by the people's failure to properly honor the arrows, and it foreshadowed the misfortunes that would come to the Cheyenne.
source: Wikipedia / World History Encyclopedia

title: Old Woman's Water and the Buffalo Cap
summary: An old woman who lived by a sacred spring found that her water had healing powers. She shared it with a young warrior who was wounded, and he was miraculously healed. The woman then revealed herself as a manifestation of the earth's power. She gave the warrior a buffalo cap made from the scalp of a sacred buffalo, which became the Ésevone (Sacred Buffalo Hat). The cap could summon buffalo herds and protect the people from harm. She taught the warrior the rituals needed to care for the cap and warned that if it was ever neglected, the buffalo would disappear and hardship would follow.
source: Grinnell / Cheyenne oral tradition

title: Wihio the Spider Trickster
summary: Wihio is the Cheyenne trickster figure, a spider-like being similar to Iktomi of the Lakota. He is clever but greedy, often getting into trouble because of his selfishness. In one story, Wihio tried to kill some ducks for a feast by tricking them into a dance with their eyes closed. A loon peeked and exposed the trick, and Wihio was shamed. In another tale, Wihio's greed caused him to be trapped inside a hollow log by his own scheme. Wihio stories serve as cautionary tales, teaching Cheyenne children about the consequences of selfish behavior and the importance of community values.
source: Cheyenne oral tradition / Grinnell

title: Nonoma the Spirit of Thunder
summary: Nonoma is the Cheyenne spirit of thunder, a powerful being who controls storms and brings rain to the plains. When Nonoma flaps his wings, thunder rolls across the sky. He is both feared and respected, as his storms can bring life-giving rain or destructive hail. The Cheyenne perform ceremonies to honor Nonoma and ask for his blessing on their crops and buffalo hunts. Thunder is associated with the renewal of life in spring, and the first thunder of the season signals the time for planting and the Sun Dance ceremony.
source: Cheyenne folklore

title: The Animal Dance
summary: The Animal Dance was a hunting ceremony received by Sweet Medicine at the Sacred Mountain (Bear Butte) to bring success to Cheyenne hunters. In this elaborate ceremony, men dressed as various animals—buffalo, deer, elk, antelope—while members of the Bowstring Society pretended to hunt them. Everything was done backwards for amusement: the animals chased the hunters, and all moved in reverse directions. The ceremony involved the entire community, with women preparing special foods and regalia. It was both a serious spiritual appeal for successful hunts and a joyful celebration that reinforced social bonds.
source: New World Encyclopedia / Cheyenne tradition

title: Comanche Creation: The Dust People
summary: The Great Spirit collected swirls of dust from the four directions—north, south, east, and west—and molded them into strong, powerful figures. He breathed life into them, and the Comanche people were born from the earth itself, infused with the strength of mighty storms and the spirit of the wind. However, a shape-shifting demon formed in the shadows, envious of the people's power. It tormented the Comanche by taking many forms. The Great Spirit cast the demon into a bottomless pit, but before falling, the demon shattered into countless pieces that took refuge in the fangs and stingers of rattlesnakes, scorpions, and spiders, continuing to harm people to this day.
source: Ya-Native Network / The Singing Scoutmaster

title: Coyote the Comanche Trickster
summary: Coyote is the central trickster figure in Comanche folklore. Unlike the spider tricksters of other Plains tribes, Coyote is a clever but flawed creature who often helps humans while constantly getting into trouble due to greed, dishonesty, and recklessness. Many Comanche storytellers refer to Coyote affectionately as the brother of the Comanches. In one tale, Coyote tries to steal fire from the fire people and is burned, explaining his singed fur. In another, he tricks prairie dogs into leaving their holes so he can catch them, but is himself outwitted by Skunk. Coyote stories teach social values through humor.
source: Native Languages of the Americas

title: How the Buffalo Were Released on Earth
summary: Long ago, all the buffalo were kept hidden in a cave deep beneath the earth by a powerful spirit who did not want to share them with humans. A young Comanche warrior, guided by a wolf spirit, discovered the cave entrance. Following the wolf's advice, he transformed into a buffalo calf and entered the herd. When the keeper opened the cave door to let the buffalo graze, the warrior-turned-calf stampeded the herd out of the cave. The buffalo poured across the plains, and the Comanche people had food and clothing. The wolf spirit taught the people how to hunt the buffalo with respect.
source: Native Languages of the Americas / Comanche folklore

title: The Legend of Manitou Springs
summary: A great battle once took place between the Comanche and the Shoshone near a sacred spring. Both tribes claimed the spring as their territory, and many warriors fell on both sides. The Great Spirit, seeing the bloodshed, caused the waters of the spring to bubble up with healing powers. The spring's mineral waters were said to have medicinal properties that could heal wounds and cure sickness. After the battle, the tribes agreed that the spring would be neutral ground where warriors from all nations could come to heal. The site, now Manitou Springs, Colorado, remains a place of spiritual significance.
source: Native Languages of the Americas

title: Skunk Outwits Coyote
summary: Coyote came upon a prairie dog village and decided to catch himself a meal. He blocked all the holes except one and waited with his mouth open at the entrance. But Skunk saw what Coyote was doing and decided to teach him a lesson. Skunk approached Coyote and asked what he was doing. Coyote explained his plan, and Skunk offered to help by driving the prairie dogs toward Coyote's mouth. Instead, Skunk walked up to Coyote and sprayed him directly in the face. Coyote ran away howling, and the prairie dogs escaped. Skunk laughed at the trickster, proving that even the cleverest schemer can be outsmarted.
source: Native Languages of the Americas

title: The Release of the Wild Animals
summary: In the beginning, all wild animals were confined in a great underground cavern by a spirit who wanted to protect them. The Comanche people had only dogs and horses. A brave warrior followed a trail to the cavern entrance and heard the stamping and snorting of many animals within. With the help of the wind spirit, he opened the cavern door. Out rushed deer, antelope, elk, bears, wolves, and mountain lions, spreading across the plains. The warrior was nearly trampled but survived. The spirit, angry at the release, caused some animals to become dangerous to humans, while others remained gentle.
source: Comanche folklore / Native Languages

title: Wolf Spirit and the Warrior's Vision
summary: A young Comanche warrior went on a vision quest alone in the mountains, fasting for four days without water. On the fourth night, a Wolf appeared to him in a vision. The Wolf said, 'I am your brother. I will give you strength, courage, and the ability to track your enemies. But you must never kill my kind, for we are connected.' The warrior returned to his people with Wolf as his guardian spirit. He became a great hunter and warrior, always guided by Wolf's wisdom. This story reflects the Comanche belief in animal spirit guides who grant powers to individuals through vision quests.
source: MythBeasts / Comanche tradition

title: Old Man Coyote Creates the World
summary: In the beginning, Old Man Coyote (Isáahkawuattee) was alone upon a great ocean. He saw two male ducks floating on the water and asked them what lay beneath. One duck dove deep and returned with a root in its beak; a second more dangerous dive brought up mud. Using this mud, Old Man Coyote built an island that grew into all the lands of Earth. He used the root to create plants and trees. Noticing the world was too flat, Old Man Coyote shaped mountains, rivers, and valleys. Then he molded man from clay, and created woman so that humans would be happy and multiply. He created female ducks so the ducks could be happy too.
source: Wikipedia / Hoxie 1989

title: The Sweat Lodge Origin: Seven Bison and Bones Together
summary: Seven Bison and their male human companion fought against the angry bison Bones Together, whose pure bone armor was impenetrable. As each bison charged, they broke their legs against Bones Together. Finally the human companion, blessed by an eagle with the power to transform into a feather, faced Bones Together. The monster charged four times, but the man turned into a feather and wafted out of harm's way. On the fourth charge, the man shot an arrow into Bones Together's rectum, piercing his heart. Though victorious, the bison were grievously injured. Together they performed the first Sweat Lodge ceremony, which healed all the bison. Afterward, the Seven Bison rose into the sky to become the Big Dipper, and the man became the Little Dipper.
source: Wikipedia / Yellowtail 1991

title: The Man and the Seven Rams
summary: A Crow man of immense ability and power once encountered the Seven Rams, magnificent creatures of the mountains. The man, confident in his strength, thought he could overpower them. But the Seven Rams taught him humility. Despite his great power, the man learned that he must respect the Sweat Lodge and its ceremonies, for the lodge's power was greater than any individual's. The Rams demonstrated that true strength comes from spiritual purity and connection to the Creator, not from physical prowess. The story emphasizes that even the most gifted individuals must submit to spiritual discipline and communal rituals.
source: McClearly 2012 / Crow tradition

title: Mannegishi (Little People)
summary: The Mannegishi are small, bald humanoid beings in Crow mythology, described as having large, bulging eyes and tiny, tan bodies. They are tricksters who live in rocky areas and along riverbanks. These little people are known for playing pranks on humans, leading travelers astray, and causing mischief. Despite their troublesome nature, they are not considered evil. Some stories say they can be helpful to those who treat them with respect. The Mannegishi are similar to fairies in European folklore and are found in various forms across many Plains Indian traditions, often associated with mysterious sounds and disappearing objects.
source: Wikipedia / Crow oral tradition

title: Tobacco Society: Chief No Vitals Discovers Tobacco
summary: The Crow people believe that tobacco was first discovered by Chief No Vitals at Devils Lake in eastern North Dakota. The Creator instructed him to seek the sacred plant, telling him that the worship of tobacco would help the Crow honor God and maintain their connection to the spirit world. Chief No Vitals found the plant and brought it back to his people, establishing the Tobacco Society (Bacu'sua). The society ensures that all rites and rituals for cultivating tobacco are performed correctly. Smoking tobacco is believed to carry prayers directly to God, as the smoke ascends to the heavens. The sacred pipe society uses tobacco daily to maintain closeness with the Creator.
source: Wikipedia / Sturtevant 2001

title: Baaxpée and the Vision Quest
summary: Baaxpée is the sacred power of God (Akbaatatdia) in Crow belief, meaning 'power transcending the ordinary.' To acquire Baaxpée, a Crow must go on a vision quest (bilisshíissanne—'to fast from water'). The quester goes alone to an isolated peak, usually in the Wolf Mountains, fasting without food or water for two to three days. Self-mortification, including finger removal, is sometimes offered to show devotion. When a spirit pities the quester, it induces a vision, adopting the Crow as a child and bestowing Baaxpée. The spirit becomes a 'Medicine Father' who guides the Crow throughout life. The quester creates a Xapáaliia (medicine object) to represent the bond.
source: Wikipedia / Frey 1997

title: The Sun Dance Lodge Symbolism
summary: The Crow Sun Dance lodge (Ashé Isée) is built as a microcosm of the universe. The central forked pole, representing God, faces east. A white flag tied to one branch represents Earth; a blue flag represents the sky. Three charcoal rings on the pole symbolize the three days of the dance and the three worlds of Crow cosmology. A buffalo head and eagle body are hung on the pole as patrons. Twelve cottonwood posts form the lodge boundary, representing the twelve months and the circle of life. For contemporary Christian Crows, the twelve posts also symbolize the twelve apostles and the three rings represent Christ's three days in the tomb.
source: Wikipedia / Voget 1984 / Yellowtail 1991

title: Crow Morning Star
summary: In Crow belief, the Morning Star (Aapisowaahs) is a heroic figure who lights the night sky. He is the son of Naato'si (the Sun) and Ko'komiki'somm (the Moon), created by the first being Apistotoke. Old Man Coyote traveled to the sky world and brought knowledge of the Morning Star back to the people. The Morning Star is associated with new beginnings, hope, and guidance. In Crow ceremonies, the Morning Star is honored at dawn with prayers and songs. The star's appearance signals the time for certain rituals and serves as a reminder of the Crow's connection to the celestial realm.
source: Crow oral tradition / Frey 1997

title: The Girl Who Married a Star
summary: A Crow girl gazed at the sky and wished to marry the Morning Star. Her wish was granted, and she was taken to the sky world where she became the wife of a star. She was happy but was told never to dig up a certain root. Lonely for her family, she eventually disobeyed and pulled the root, revealing a hole through which she could see her earthly home far below. Overcome with longing, she wove a rope to descend, but it was too short. She fell to Earth and survived, finding her way back to her people. The star husband, heartbroken, can still be seen in the morning sky.
source: Crow folklore / Native Languages

title: Akbaatatdia the Creator
summary: Akbaatatdia (One Who Has Made Everything) is the supreme Creator in Crow religion. Also known as Iichíkbaalee (First Doer) and Isáahkawuattee (Old Man Coyote), this singular omnipotent god created the universe. The Crow believe the universe consists of three worlds: the physical world (the smallest), the spirit world (the intermediate realm), and the third world where God alone dwells. Akbaatatdia is omnipresent and omnipotent, and Crows believe they are in contact with God during every aspect of daily life. Because God created everything, all of nature is sacred, and all religions are considered valid paths to the same Creator.
source: Wikipedia / Frey 1997

title: Tirawa Creates the Universe
summary: Tirawa (Atíʼas Tirawa), 'Our Father Above,' was the supreme creator god of the Pawnee. He created the universe and placed the Morning Star in the East and the Evening Star in the West. The Morning Star, a male warrior god, was created first. The female Evening Star resisted the divine plan to create humankind, so Morning Star fought and overcame forces in the western sky with his fireball to mate with her. The first human thus created was a girl. Tirawa taught the Pawnee tattooing, fire-building, hunting, agriculture, speech, clothing, religious rituals, and sacrifice. His wife was Atira (Mother Corn), goddess of the Earth.
source: Wikipedia / Murie 1981

title: The Morning Star Sacrifice Ceremony
summary: The Morning Star ceremony was a Skidi Pawnee ritual involving the sacrifice of a captive girl to ensure agricultural fertility and cosmic order. It was performed when a man dreamed that Morning Star commanded it. A captive girl was taken from an enemy tribe, treated with respect but kept isolated. In spring, she was ritually cleansed and painted, symbolically transformed into the Evening Star. At dawn on the final day, she was placed on a scaffold and shot through the heart with a sacred arrow while a priest opened her chest. Her blood fell to fertilize the earth. The ceremony reenacted the Morning Star's cosmic battle and marriage to the Evening Star that created humanity.
source: Wikipedia / Murie 1981 / Linton 1926

title: Atira—Mother Corn
summary: Atira is the Pawnee goddess of the Earth and wife of Tirawa, the creator god. Her name literally means 'Mother Corn,' and she is associated with the corn that sustained the Pawnee people. Atira is a nurturing figure who provides food and life from the earth. In the Hako (Calumet) ceremony, a symbol representing Atira was created, showing corn painted so that the Rainstorm, Thunder, Lightning, and Wind are represented. The South Bands of the Pawnee particularly honored Atira through agricultural ceremonies, giving thanks for the harvest and praying for continued fertility of the soil.
source: Wikipedia / Murie 1981

title: The Nahurac: Spirit Animal Lodges
summary: Tirawa conferred miraculous powers on certain animals who would act as his messengers and servants. These spirit animals, called nahurac, could intercede with Tirawa on behalf of the Pawnee. The nahurac had five sacred lodges: Pahuk (hill island on the Platte River), Lalawakohtito (dark island in the Platte), Ahkawitakol (white bank on the Loup River), Kitzawitzuk (a sacred spring on the Solomon River), and Pahur (guide rock hill). These five lodges were places of spiritual power where the nahurac gathered. The Pawnee would visit these sites to seek guidance, healing, and blessings from the animal spirits.
source: Wikipedia / Grinnell 1893

title: The Spring Awakening Ceremony
summary: The most important Pawnee ceremony was the Spring Awakening, meant to awaken the earth and prepare it for planting. The timing was determined by celestial observation: the priest would track 'two small twinkling stars known as the Swimming Ducks in the northeastern horizon near the Milky Way.' When these stars appeared and the first rolling thunder was heard from the West, the ceremony began. The ritual involved songs, prayers, and the renewal of sacred bundles. The Skidi Pawnee performed this as the Thunder ceremony, focusing on corn renewal, while the South Bands performed the White Beaver ceremony, which revitalized hibernating animals.
source: Wikipedia / Murie 1981 / Weltfish

title: The Celestial Star Gods
summary: The Skidi Pawnee organized their entire society around the stars. Six major stars represented gods controlled by Tirawa: the North Star (watched over the people), Northeast Star (Big Black Meteoric Star, controlled animals and bison), Southeast Star (Red Star, regulated day and animals), South Star (ruled the land of the dead), Southwest Star, and Northwest Star (both female). The Milky Way contained paths that guided the dead to South Star's dominion. The sun (Shakuru) and moon (Pah) were the last gods placed in the heavens.
