The four villages of the Skidi were given by the four leading stars.
source: Wikipedia / Murie 1981

title: The White Beaver Ceremony
summary: Among the Chawi band of the Pawnee South Bands, the White Beaver ceremony served the same purpose as the Skidi's Spring Awakening ceremony—renewing life and ensuring fertility. However, instead of focusing on corn, the White Beaver ceremony revitalized hibernating animals. The white beaver was a sacred animal associated with the water spirits. The ceremony involved elaborate rituals, songs, and the use of sacred bundles. This difference between the Skidi (celestial-focused) and South Bands (animal-focused) reflects the two approaches to Pawnee spirituality: the Skidi looked to the stars while the South Bands relied on animal spirits as intermediaries.
source: Wikipedia / Murie 1981

title: Pahuk and the Sacred Sites
summary: Pahuk (hill island) is a sacred bluff on the south side of the Platte River near Cedar Bluffs, Nebraska. It is the foremost of the five nahurac lodges, where the animal spirit messengers of Tirawa gather. According to Pawnee legend, a young man once visited Pahuk seeking a vision. After four days of fasting, the animals spoke to him, teaching him the rituals of the Hako ceremony. The site is considered a place of great spiritual power where the boundary between the physical and spirit worlds is thin. The Pawnee made pilgrimages to Pahuk to offer prayers, seek healing, and receive guidance from the nahurac.
source: Wikipedia / Grinnell 1893

title: The Hako (Calumet) Ceremony
summary: The Hako ceremony, also known as the Calumet ceremony, was a complex Pawnee ritual centered on the sacred pipe. It was a peace-making and adoption ceremony that created bonds between individuals, families, and even tribes. The ceremony lasted several days and involved singing, dancing, smoking the sacred pipe, and the exchange of gifts. A decorated pipe (the Hako) served as the central symbol of the ritual. The ceremony was dedicated to Atira (Mother Corn) and involved a corn symbol painted to represent the Rainstorm, Thunder, Lightning, and Wind. The Hako reinforced social ties and ensured the continuity of the community.
source: Wikipedia / Murie 1981

title: Flat Pipe and the Arapaho Creation
summary: In the beginning, there was nothing but water covering everything. A man carrying a Flat Pipe walked on the water for six days, weeping and fasting, searching for a safe place to rest his pipe. On the seventh day, he called to the four directions and summoned seven cottonwood trees and all the creatures. One by one, various animals dove into the water to bring up mud—Grebe, waterfowls, Kingfisher, Otter, Beaver, Coot, Garter Snake, Black Snake, ducks, goose, crane—all failed. On the seventh dive, Turtle and the Man (who transformed his Flat Pipe into a Red-headed Duck) succeeded in bringing up mud. The Man cast the mud in four directions, creating the Earth.
source: University of Colorado Boulder / Dorsey 1903

title: Nihancan the Spider Trickster
summary: Nihancan is the Arapaho trickster figure, a spider-like being similar to Iktomi of the Lakota and Wihio of the Cheyenne. He is both clever and foolish, often causing trouble through his greed and selfishness. In one tale, Nihancan tricked a bear family and killed the children, only to be outwitted himself. Another story tells of Nihancan encountering a dwarf with a powerful arrow; the dwarf taught Nihancan a lesson about humility and respect. Despite his flaws, Nihancan's stories serve as important moral lessons for the Arapaho people, teaching the consequences of antisocial behavior.
source: Native Languages of the Americas / Arapaho oral tradition

title: Found-in-the-Grass
summary: Found-in-the-Grass is a rags-to-riches hero of Arapaho folklore. In some versions, he is an orphan abandoned as an infant and found by villagers in the tall grass. In others, he is the magical twin Spring-Boy grown older. Despite his humble beginnings, Found-in-the-Grass rises to become a great warrior and leader through courage, wit, and the help of spirit allies. He performs incredible feats, defeats enemies, and brings prosperity to his people. His story embodies the Arapaho value that one's origins do not determine one's destiny, and that perseverance and spiritual strength can overcome any disadvantage.
source: Native Languages of the Americas

title: Splinter Foot Girl
summary: A childless Arapaho woman found a splinter embedded in her foot. To her amazement, the splinter grew and transformed into a baby girl. The girl grew rapidly and possessed supernatural abilities. She could communicate with animals and had the power to heal. She brought good fortune to her family and community, helping them find buffalo and warning them of danger. One day, a rival tribe kidnapped her, but she used her powers to escape and return home. The story teaches that blessings can come from unexpected places, and that even something as small as a splinter can hold great potential.
source: Native Languages of the Americas

title: The Girl Who Climbed to the Sky
summary: An Arapaho girl was picking berries when she saw a porcupine in a tree. She climbed after it, but the tree grew taller and taller, carrying her into the sky world. In the sky, she found a beautiful land populated by spirits. She married a sky spirit and was happy, but she missed her earthly family. The spirits taught her songs and ceremonies that she would bring back to her people. Eventually, a spider spun a web to lower her back to Earth, where she taught the Arapaho the sky ceremonies she had learned. She became a powerful medicine woman.
source: Native Languages of the Americas / Arapaho tradition

title: The Sun Dance Wheel
summary: The Arapaho Sun Dance, known as the Offerings Lodge, is a central spiritual ceremony. Unlike other Plains tribes that practiced self-torture, the Arapaho version involved donating personal items, space, or land to the community as sacrifice. The sacred Flat Pipe was central to the ceremony, symbolizing the Arapaho's connection to the Creator. The Sun Dance lodge was built with a central pole representing the Creator, surrounded by a circle of posts representing the community. The ceremony lasted four days and included dancing, prayer, singing, and offering. The wheel symbolism represents the cycle of life and the interconnectedness of all things.
source: World History Encyclopedia / Arapaho tradition

title: How Medicine Man Resurrected Buffalo
summary: During a terrible famine, all the buffalo had disappeared from the plains. The Arapaho people were starving. A powerful medicine man went into the mountains to seek a vision. After four days of fasting, the spirit of the Earth came to him and told him that the buffalo had gone underground because the people had been wasteful. Following the spirit's instructions, the medicine man gathered the tribe for a ceremony. Through songs, prayers, and the use of his medicine bundle, he opened a passage to the underground buffalo world. The buffalo emerged, and the people were saved. He taught them never to waste the buffalo's gift.
source: Native Languages of the Americas

title: The Lame Warrior and the Skeleton
summary: A lame Arapaho warrior, unable to fight alongside his people, was left behind when the village moved. As he sat alone, a skeleton approached him. The skeleton offered to carry the warrior to battle if the warrior would promise to give the skeleton a proper burial afterward. They formed a pact. The skeleton carried the warrior into battle against their enemies, and the warrior's arrows struck true. They won a great victory. True to his word, the warrior gathered the skeleton's bones and performed the burial rites. The skeleton's spirit appeared in a dream, thanking the warrior and granting him the gift of healing. The warrior became a powerful medicine man.
source: Native Languages of the Americas

title: Nihancan and the Dwarf's Arrow
summary: Nihancan the trickster was wandering when he encountered a small dwarf armed with a bow and arrow. Nihancan laughed at the dwarf, mocking his small size and tiny weapon. The dwarf challenged Nihancan to shoot the arrow at a target. Nihancan agreed, but when he tried to pull the bow, he found it impossibly heavy. The dwarf took back his bow and shot an arrow that flew with incredible power, splitting a boulder in half. Nihancan, humbled, realized that size does not determine power. The dwarf taught Nihancan to respect all beings, regardless of their appearance.
source: Native Languages of the Americas

title: The Star Husband (Arapaho)
summary: Two Arapaho women lay outside their tipi at night. One pointed at a bright star and said she wished to marry it. The next day, a handsome young man appeared at their camp. He was the star in human form, come to claim his bride. The woman went with him to the sky world, where she lived among the star people. But she grew homesick and was told she could return to Earth if she did not eat certain food. She broke the taboo and immediately found herself back on Earth. Her star husband appeared no more, but she gave birth to a son who became a legendary Arapaho hero.
source: Native Languages of the Americas

title: The Great Race (Arapaho)
summary: Long ago, the animals decided to hold a great race to determine which would be the fastest and most powerful. All creatures gathered from across the plains. The two-leggeds and four-leggeds each chose their champion. The race was long and grueling, crossing rivers and mountains. The buffalo was winning for the four-leggeds, but the magpie—representing the two-leggeds—hitched a ride on the buffalo's back. Near the finish line, the magpie flew ahead and won. The Creator declared it a draw, ruling that two-leggeds would hunt four-leggeds for food, but must always show respect for their sacrifice. The Arapaho version emphasizes cooperation and cleverness over brute strength.
source: Arapaho oral tradition

title: The Release of the Buffalo (Arapaho)
summary: In the time before buffalo roamed the plains, all the buffalo were confined in a cave beneath a mountain, guarded by a powerful spirit. The Arapaho people had only small game and were often hungry. A young man on a vision quest learned from a mouse spirit that the buffalo were hidden underground. With the help of various small animals, he found the cave entrance. Using songs given by the mouse spirit, he opened the cave. The buffalo stampeded out, covering the plains. The guard spirit, angry at the release, cursed the Arapaho to always need the buffalo, but also blessed them that the buffalo would always provide.
source: Arapaho folklore / Native Languages

title: The First Sweat Lodge (Lakota)
summary: According to Lakota tradition, the sweat lodge was given to the people by the spirits as a means of purification and healing. A warrior who had been wounded in battle was near death when a spirit appeared and instructed him to build a framework of willow branches and cover it with buffalo hides. He was to heat stones in a fire, place them in a pit at the center, and pour water over them. The steam would purify his body and spirit. The warrior followed these instructions and was healed. The inipi (sweat lodge) ceremony became one of the Seven Sacred Rites, used for physical healing, spiritual cleansing, and preparation for other ceremonies.
source: Lakota oral tradition / Black Elk

title: The Woman Who Married a Buffalo
summary: A Lakota woman was picking berries when a buffalo bull approached her. To her surprise, he spoke and asked her to be his wife. She agreed and went to live with the buffalo people, where she learned their ways and language. She gave birth to a son who was half-human, half-buffalo. One day, her human brothers came hunting and nearly killed the buffalo people. The woman pleaded with her buffalo husband to spare them. In return, she agreed to return to her human family. Before leaving, she gave her son a sacred buffalo robe that would protect him. The story explains the special relationship between the Lakota and the buffalo.
source: Lakota folklore

title: The Pipe of Peace Treaty
summary: The sacred pipe given by White Buffalo Calf Woman serves as the ultimate symbol of peace among the Lakota. When two warring tribes wished to end hostilities, they would smoke the pipe together. The pipe bowl, made of red stone, represents the earth; the wooden stem represents all that grows upon the earth. The smoke rising carries prayers to Wakan Tanka. To smoke the pipe in ceremony is to pray with and for all the universe. The pipe is pointed to the sky (for the Creator), to the earth (for Grandmother Earth), and to the four directions before smoking. The pipe ceremony remains central to Lakota spirituality.
source: Black Elk / The Sacred Pipe

title: The Gift of Fire (Comanche)
summary: Long ago, the Comanche people had no fire and ate their food raw. Coyote, always clever, decided to steal fire from the fire people who guarded it jealously. Coyote crept into their camp and, when they weren't looking, grabbed a burning branch and ran. The fire people chased him across the plains. Coyote threw the fire to the animals, who tossed it among themselves like a hot game. The fire singed the animals in passing—the badger still has black marks, the raccoon's tail was burned, and the coyote's nose was scorched. Eventually, the fire reached the Comanche people, and they have kept it ever since.
source: Comanche oral tradition

title: The Rolling Stone Legend (Cheyenne)
summary: A Cheyenne boy was left in camp while the adults went hunting. A mysterious rolling stone appeared and began chasing him. The boy ran for his life, but the stone rolled faster and faster, crushing everything in its path. The boy encountered various animals who tried to help—a magpie pecked at the stone, a badger dug a hole for the boy to hide—but none could stop the pursuing boulder. Finally, a wise old turtle advised the boy to climb a certain tree. When the stone rolled under the tree, a branch caught it and held it fast. The boy was saved, and the stone remains there to this day.
source: Cheyenne folklore / Grinnell

title: The Origin of the Sun Dance (Cheyenne)
summary: The Sun Dance was given to the Cheyenne by the culture heroes Sweet Medicine and Erect Horns. Sweet Medicine brought the Sacred Arrows and the structure of the Sun Dance from his journey to the Sacred Mountain. The Sun Dance is held each summer when the tribe gathers to renew their connection to Ma'heo'o. Dancers fast and dance for four days, blowing eagle bone whistles and gazing at the central pole. In the Cheyenne version, dancers are pierced with thongs through their breast muscles as a sacrifice. The ceremony renews the earth, ensures the return of the buffalo, and maintains the harmony of all creation.
source: Wikipedia / Cheyenne tradition

title: The Great Medicine Road
summary: The Pawnee believe that after death, the soul travels along the Milky Way, which they call the Great Medicine Road or the Path of the Dead. This celestial road leads the deceased to the land of the dead in the south, ruled by the South Star. Spirits must walk this path, and the journey is long. The stars in the Milky Way are the campfires of the dead. When a falling star streaks across the sky, it is a soul traveling to its final rest. The dead live in a world much like Earth, where they hunt, feast, and dance. The living honor their ancestors with prayers and offerings.
source: Wikipedia / Murie 1981

title: Shakuru and Pah: The Sun and Moon
summary: Shakuru (the Sun) and Pah (the Moon) were the last of all gods placed in the heavens by Tirawa. They are of relatively minor standing in Skidi Pawnee mythology compared to the star gods. The Sun and Moon produced a son, a boy who was placed on Earth to be the ancestor of the Pawnee people. The Sun is associated with warmth, light, and the daily cycle. The Moon governs the night and is associated with women's cycles. In Pawnee earth lodge construction, the door faces east to welcome the rising sun, and the smoke hole is aligned to observe the moon and stars.
source: Wikipedia / Murie 1981

title: Hotuiyi (Antelope) and the Warning
summary: A Comanche hunting party was pursuing a herd of antelope across the plains when a strange antelope with glowing eyes appeared. The lead hunter recognized it as a spirit messenger and halted the chase. The antelope led the party to a hidden spring where they found water, then traced a path around a dangerous canyon where enemy warriors lay in ambush. The hunter realized the antelope had saved them from both thirst and attack. The Comanche gave thanks to the antelope spirit and thereafter considered antelope sightings as omens—if the antelope runs east, good fortune follows; if west, danger approaches.
source: Comanche folklore

title: The Mossy Rock (Blackfoot)
summary: Na'pi (Old Man) was traveling across the plains when he came upon a large moss-covered rock. He stopped to rest and draped his robe over the rock to dry. When he tried to retrieve his robe, the rock refused to give it back. Na'pi grew angry and struck the rock with his stick. The rock split open and Na'pi retrieved his robe, but the rock's anger caused the earth to shake. Na'pi realized he should not have disturbed the rock's peace. The story teaches that one must respect all things in nature, even those that seem inanimate.
source: Blackfoot folklore / Grinnell

title: The Star Woman (Blackfoot)
summary: A Blackfoot chief's daughter married a star and went to live in the sky world. Each day she was given the task of digging roots, but she was forbidden from digging up one particular turnip. Curiosity overcame her, and when she pulled the turnip, she saw the Earth far below. Overcome with homesickness, she wove a rope of sinew to lower herself back. But the rope was too short and she fell, landing safely but finding herself alone. Following a stream, she found her way back to her people, forever changed by her time among the stars. Her story became a lesson about obedience and the bond between Earth and sky.
source: Blackfoot folklore / Grinnell

title: The Legend of the Big Dipper (Crow)
summary: After the seven bison and their human companion defeated Bones Together and were healed by the first Sweat Lodge ceremony, the Creator raised the seven bison into the sky to become the Big Dipper (the Seven Bison). The human companion became the Little Dipper, following them eternally across the night sky. The Crow look at the Big Dipper and remember the sacrifice and healing of the first Sweat Lodge. The stars serve as a constant reminder of the power of prayer, purification, and the bond between humans and animals. This story connects the Crow people to the cosmos and their daily rituals.
source: Yellowtail 1991 / Crow tradition

title: The Sacred Pipe (Blackfoot)
summary: The Blackfoot believe the sacred pipe was given to them by the Thunder spirit, who taught them the songs and rituals for its use. A man once heard thunder and saw a lightning-struck tree. Where the lightning had struck, he found a beautifully carved pipe. The Thunder spirit spoke to him, instructing him to form a society of pipe keepers. The pipe would be used for healing, peace-making, and communicating with the spirit world. The Blackfoot Medicine Pipe ceremony involves elaborate rituals, the smoking of the pipe, and the offering of prayers to Apistotoke for the well-being of the people.
source: Blackfoot oral tradition

title: The Woman Who Fell from the Sky
summary: In the Sky World above the earth, a pregnant woman named Atsi'tsaká:ion (Mature Flower) fell through a hole created when the Great Tree of Life was uprooted. As she descended, she grasped seeds from the tree. Water birds saw her falling and caught her, placing her on the back of the Great Turtle. The muskrat dove to the ocean floor and brought up mud, which she spread on the turtle's back. She danced in a circle, and the mud grew into the land known as Turtle Island. She planted the seeds from the Sky World, and the first plants grew. This is the Haudenosaunee account of how the earth was formed on the back of the Great Turtle.
source: Kanehsatake Voices Creation Story; sacred-texts.com Onondaga version; native-languages.org Sky Woman

title: The Birth of the Twin Brothers: Sapling and Flint
summary: After Sky Woman's daughter Tekawerahkwa (Breath of the Wind) gave birth, the twin boys emerged in different ways. The right-handed twin, Sapling (Hahgwehdi-yu or Kanehtó:ton), was born naturally and became the creator of all that is good and beautiful in the world. The left-handed twin, Flint (Hahgwehdaetgah or Okarahtsikó:wa), forced his way out through his mother's armpit, killing her. From her buried body grew the sacred plants: tobacco from her heart and strawberries from her feet. The twins then set about completing the creation, each making their own contributions to the natural world, with Flint attempting to undo all of Sapling's good work.
source: Kanehsatake Voices; native-languages.org Iroquois Creation Story; sacred-texts.com Onondaga version

title: The Battle of the Twins
summary: Sapling and Flint's conflict over the world they had created came to a head when they agreed to settle their rivalry through combat. Sapling chose the branches of the giant crab apple tree as his weapon, while Flint preferred poisonous roots, monster's teeth, and serpent fangs. They fought for many days across the landscape. The battle ended when Sapling overthrew Flint. He sent his evil brother to live in the underworld, but could not destroy him entirely. It was agreed that the world would need both good and evil, light and darkness. Even in victory, Sapling decreed that his brother would continue to influence the world, bringing winter, darkness, and death to balance the good he had created.
source: native-languages.org; Smith, Erminnie A. - Myths of the Iroquois (Project Gutenberg)

title: The Three Sisters: Corn, Beans, and Squash
summary: In Iroquois tradition, the Three Sisters are the agricultural spirits Corn (Onatah), Beans, and Squash. They are inseparable companions who grow best when planted together. According to legend, the Creator gave these three plants to the people as a sacred gift. Corn provides a stalk for the beans to climb, beans fix nitrogen in the soil for the corn, and squash spreads its broad leaves to shade the ground, preventing weeds and retaining moisture. The sisters represent the principle of mutual support and cooperation that underlies Haudenosaunee society. Ceremonies honor them throughout the growing season, especially the Green Corn Festival when the first corn ripens.
source: native-languages.org; Kanehsatake Voices Creation Story

title: The Flying Head (Kanontsistóntie)
summary: The Flying Head is a terrifying monster in the form of a giant disembodied head as tall as a man, covered in thick black hair with bat-like wings and long sharp claws. It comes to the homes of widows and orphans at night, beating its wings on the walls and issuing terrible cries, presaging death within days. According to one legend, the Flying Head was born from a massacre where a famine-stricken tribe decapitated their dead and sank the heads in the lake. The Seneca call it Takwánö'ë:yët, meaning whirlwind. One story tells of an old woman who defeated the Flying Head by sitting by her fire eating roasted acorns; the monster mistook the acorns for coals of fire and fled in terror, never to be seen again in that place.
source: Wikipedia - Flying Head; Bruchac, Joseph - Stone Giants & Flying Heads; Smith - Myths of the Iroquois

title: The Stone Giants (Otneyarhed / Stone Coats)
summary: The Stone Giants, also known as Stone Coats, were a race of cannibalistic giants whose skin was as hard as stone, making them nearly invulnerable to weapons. They were the mortal enemies of humans and the constant antagonists in Iroquois hero tales. According to legend, they were exterminated through the cunning and bravery of heroes who discovered that their only weakness was the soft spot on the top of their heads, where a special stone or arrow could pierce their otherwise impenetrable hides. Tales include the Stone Giant's wife, who helped humans, and the story of a young warrior who challenged a Stone Giant in personal combat. Their threat was so great that it required the intervention of the Thunderers to finally drive them away.
source: Bruchac, Joseph - Stone Giants & Flying Heads; Smith - Myths of the Iroquois; native-languages.org

title: The Great Peacemaker (Deganawida / Skennenrahawi)
summary: The Great Peacemaker, known as Deganawida in the sacred language and Skennenrahawi in Mohawk, was a legendary leader who founded the Iroquois Confederacy, the oldest living democracy in North America. Born among the Huron, he crossed Lake Ontario in a stone canoe to bring peace to the warring Iroquois nations. With the orator Hiawatha and the Seneca chief Jigonsaseh (the Peace Queen), he converted the cannibal warrior Hiawatha and confronted the evil wizard Tadodaho at Onondaga, whose twisted hair was filled with snakes. They transformed Tadodaho and made him the first fire-keeper of the Confederacy. The Great Law (Kaianere'kó:wa) established the principles of peace, equity, and the power of the clan mothers.
source: Wikipedia - Great Law of Peace; native-languages.org Great Peacemaker; Fenton/Simpson versions

title: Hiawatha and the Invention of Wampum
summary: When Hiawatha's three daughters were killed by the evil Tadodaho, he was consumed by grief and wandered the wilderness. The Great Peacemaker found him and taught him the Condolence Ceremony to ease his grief. As part of this ceremony, Hiawatha invented wampum — strings of purple and white shell beads — to record the laws and treaties of the growing Confederacy. He strung the white beads to represent peace and the purple to represent the grave matters of the law. The strings of wampum became the constitutional record of the Haudenosaunee, serving as mnemonic devices to help storytellers recall the exact words of the Great Law. The Hiawatha Belt, with its central symbols, depicts the five nations united under the Great Tree of Peace.
source: Smith - Myths of the Iroquois; Wikipedia - Great Law of Peace; native-languages.org Peacemaker

title: Origin of the Pleiades (The Dancing Children)
summary: Long ago, seven young Iroquois children loved to dance in the woods. Every evening they would gather in a clearing, singing and dancing even as others told them to stop. They refused to go to sleep or do their chores, so absorbed were they in their joyous dancing. The Creator, watching from the sky, decided to preserve their joy forever. He caused the ground beneath them to rise up, lifting them higher and higher into the sky. Their families called for them to come back, but they continued dancing as they ascended. The children became the constellation of the Pleiades, the seven stars that dance across the winter sky. In the Onondaga version, the sixth star is dimmer because one child looked back and began to fall before the others caught her.
source: native-languages.org Onondaga Legends; sacred-texts.com

title: The Girl Who Was Not Satisfied
summary: This moral tale from the Iroquois tradition tells of a young girl who was never content with what she had, always wanting more and better things. She rejected the simple life of her village and the young men who courted her. One day she met a handsome stranger who was actually a great serpent in disguise. She went away with him, only to discover too late his true nature. The story teaches the importance of appreciating what one has and warns against the dangers of pride and dissatisfaction. It also reinforces the Iroquois value of community over individual desire, as the girl's isolation from her people made her vulnerable to the serpent's deceptions.
source: Wikipedia - Iroquois mythology; Bruchac, Joseph - Stone Giants & Flying Heads

title: The Thunderers (Hadiwennoda:dye's)
summary: The Thunderers are powerful storm spirits who live in the sky and cause thunder and lightning. They are described as giant warriors who hunt and kill the great horned serpents and other monsters that threaten humanity. In the Seneca tradition, they are led by the great chief Hino. Their gaze can bring death to mortal men, yet they are generally benevolent and play a protective role in Iroquois legends. They are the eternal enemies of the Great Horned Serpent Oniare and maintain the balance of the world by ensuring no monster grows too powerful. The Thunderers are honored with tobacco offerings, and their rumbling voices are heard whenever they pursue evil spirits across the sky.
source: native-languages.org; Smith - Myths of the Iroquois

title: The Great Horned Serpent (Oniare)
summary: Oniare is a dragon-like horned serpent of the Great Lakes region, deeply feared by the Iroquois and neighboring tribes for its habit of capsizing canoes and devouring people. It lives in the deepest parts of lakes and rivers, controlling the waters and causing storms. The serpent has a single horn or a crest of antlers and can grow to enormous size. It is the archetypal enemy of the Thunderers, who hunt it across the sky. In one legend, a great horned serpent appeared on Lake Ontario, killing many with its poisonous breath before the old women of the tribe prayed to the Thunderers to destroy it. The Thunderers hurled lightning bolts until the monster retreated to the depths, never to trouble the people again.
source: native-languages.org; Legends, Traditions and Laws of the Iroquois; sacred-texts.com

title: The Stone Giant's Wife
summary: This legend tells of a human woman who was taken captive by a Stone Giant. Against all expectations, she survived in the giant's lodge by learning his habits and discovering the secret weakness of his kind — the soft spot on the crown of the head. She eventually escaped and returned to her people with the critical knowledge that would allow them to defend themselves against the stone-coated monsters. The story reflects the Iroquois belief that even the most terrifying adversaries have vulnerabilities, and that courage and observation can overcome seemingly insurmountable threats. It was one of the most popular tales told around winter fires, and versions appear in the early ethnographic collections of the Bureau of American Ethnology.
source: Smith - Myths of the Iroquois; Bruchac - Stone Giants & Flying Heads

title: The Stone Giant's Challenge
summary: A heroic Iroquois warrior once issued a challenge to a fearsome Stone Giant who had been terrorizing villages. The giant accepted and the two met in battle. The warrior discovered that ordinary weapons could not pierce the giant's stone skin. Using his wits, he lured the giant onto treacherous ground where a rockslide buried him, then climbed to the giant's head and found the vulnerable soft spot. This story was recorded in multiple versions by anthropologists in the 19th century, including by Erminnie Smith among the Onondaga and by Arthur C. Parker among the Seneca. It belongs to a cycle of legends about the extermination of the Stone Giants, which the Iroquois believed once ruled the land before humans were created.
source: Smith - Myths of the Iroquois; Parker, Arthur C. - Seneca Myths and Folk Tales (1923)

title: Hi-nun and the Niagara Legend
summary: The Seneca tell of Hi-nun, the Thunderer, who fought a great battle with a monstrous serpent living in the Niagara River. The serpent had grown so large that it blocked the flow of the river, causing devastating floods. Hi-nun descended from the sky with his thunderbolt and struck the serpent, whose thrashing body carved out the shape of Niagara Falls as it died. The mist that rises from the falls is said to be the serpent's dying breath. The Spirit of the West Wind was honored thereafter by the Seneca for his role in the battle, and the thunder of the falls is Hi-nun's voice warning away other monsters. This legend explains both the origin of the falls and the reverence paid to the thunder beings.
source: Smith - Myths of the Iroquois (Gutenberg); Seneca oral tradition

title: The Great Head and the Old Woman
summary: Cusick's story of the dispersion of the Great Heads recounts how an old Onondaga woman was sitting alone in her wigwam one evening, parching acorns over the fire, when a monstrous Flying Head appeared at her door. The head was enormous, with blazing eyes and long hair draping like wings. The old woman, showing no fear, continued calmly eating her roasted acorns. The Flying Head watched her crack the hard shells and eat what appeared to be red-hot coals from the fire. Terrified of a being who could eat fire itself, the Flying Head fled and none of its kind were ever seen in that region again. The story demonstrates the Iroquois value of courage in the face of supernatural terror.
source: Smith - Myths of the Iroquois (Cusick's account); native-languages.org Flying Head

title: Wenebojo Creates the Earth (After the Great Flood)
summary: In the Algonquian creation story, the trickster-transformer Wenebojo (Nanabozho) caused a great flood through his battle with the underwater serpents who killed his brother. After the waters covered everything, Wenebojo floated on a log with the surviving animals. He asked the loon, then the otter, then the muskrat to dive to the bottom of the flood waters and bring up a pawful of earth. One by one they tried; each failed until the humble muskrat dove so deep he nearly died, but surfaced with a tiny bit of mud in his paw. From this mud, Wenebojo created a new earth, placing it on the back of a great turtle. The turtle grew and grew, becoming Turtle Island, the North American continent. The muskrat's sacrifice is remembered as proof that even the smallest can achieve the greatest.
source: native-languages.org Nanabozho; Johnston, Basil - The Mishomis Book; Leland - Algonquin Legends

title: Wenebojo and the Dancing Geese
summary: Wenebojo, hungry and lazy, saw a flock of geese dancing on the shore. He convinced them to teach him their dance, saying he had seen a more beautiful dance in his dreams. The geese agreed, and Wenebojo joined them, singing and dancing with his eyes closed as he had been instructed. As they danced, Wenebojo began to wring the necks of the geese one by one, still keeping his eyes shut. A young goose opened its eyes and saw what was happening, crying out a warning. The remaining geese flew away. Wenebojo was left with a pile of dead geese, but his treachery was discovered and he faced the consequences. This trickster tale teaches both humor and the lesson that dishonesty will eventually be exposed.
source: native-languages.org Nanabozho; Ojibwe oral tradition

title: Nanabozho and the Theft of Fire
summary: In the beginning, fire belonged only to the spirits and the people shivered in the cold. Nanabozho, the culture hero, decided to steal fire for humanity. He traveled to the lodge of the Old Man who guarded the only flame, guarded by a fierce serpent. Nanabozho transformed himself into a rabbit to sneak past the serpent. He grabbed a burning brand and fled, with the Old Man and the serpent in pursuit. As he ran, the fire scorched the trees around him, which is why the birch tree still bears black marks on its white bark to this day. Nanabozho passed the fire to the animals who relayed it to the people. Ever since, the people have had fire for warmth and cooking, and the birch tree's scars are a reminder of Nanabozho's gift.
source: native-languages.org Chippewa Legends; Ojibwa myths; Johnston - Mishomis Book

title: The Origin of Maple Sugar (Wenebojo and the Maple Trees)
summary: Long ago, when Wenebojo was wandering the earth, he discovered that the maple trees produced thick, sweet syrup that flowed freely from their trunks. The people had only to collect it and drink their fill. Wenebojo, being lazy and greedy, drank so much that he became sick. Angrily believing the trees had tricked him, he diluted the syrup by throwing water into the trees so that only a thin sap remained. Then he took his spear and stabbed the birch tree full of holes (the marks are visible on birch bark to this day), declaring that in the future people would have to work hard to get maple sugar — gathering wood, boiling the sap for many hours, and it would take forty barrels of sap to make one barrel of sugar. Thus Wenebojo made life harder for his people through his own fault.
source: Ojibwe People's Dictionary (Neganabenazekwa); native-languages.org Chippewa Legends

title: How Fisher Went to the Skyland (The Big Dipper)
summary: Long ago, the giant lynx Mishipeshu had imprisoned all the summer birds in a cave beneath the earth, plunging the world into endless winter. The animals held a council and decided to free the birds. Fisher, the swiftest of them, led a band of animals to the cave. While the others distracted Mishipeshu, Fisher slipped inside and released the birds. Summer returned, but the enraged Mishipeshu pursued Fisher across the sky. As Fisher ran, the spirits lifted him higher and higher into the heavens, where he was transformed into the constellation we call the Big Dipper (which the Ojibwe call Ojiig, meaning Fisher). Every year, when the constellation appears in the northern sky, it signals the return of spring. The other animals who helped were also placed in the sky as stars.
source: native-languages.org Ojibwe Legends; Johnston - Mishomis Book

title: Nanabozho and the Great Serpent
summary: The Great Horned Serpent Mishiginebig, ruler of the underwater world, had been devouring the people who lived near the Great Lakes. Nanabozho, the culture hero, vowed to destroy the monster. He transformed into a tree stump on the shore and waited. When the serpent surfaced, its antlered head rising like an island from the water, Nanabozho struck with his magical club. A terrible battle ensued, churning the waters of the lake into a furious storm. Nanabozho finally killed the serpent, but the dying monster's body caused a great flood that nearly destroyed the world. This flood led to the recreation of the earth from mud brought up by the muskrat. The story teaches the danger of disrupting the balance between the upper and lower worlds.
source: native-languages.org; Conway - Northern Mythology; Johnston - Mishomis Book

title: Kishelamakank's Dream of Creation
summary: In the beginning, before there was anything, there existed only an endless void of darkness. Within this emptiness dwelled Kishelamakank (He Who Creates Us By Thought), the Great Spirit. While in the void, the Creator fell into a deep sleep and dreamed of the world as it would become — mountains, forests, animals, and people. Upon awakening, He brought His dream into reality. He created helper spirits: the Grandfathers of the East, North, and West, and the Grandmother of the South. Together, they shaped the earth. From a sacred tree, the first man emerged from the roots. When the tree bent and touched the ground, the first woman sprang forth. This story affirms the Lenape understanding that all creation began not with violence but with a divine dream.
source: Brinton, Daniel G. - The Lenape and Their Legends (Gutenberg); ordoluporumpma.org; native-languages.org

title: Turtle Island: The Lenape Earth Diver
summary: In the Lenape creation account, before land existed, all the animals lived in deep water. They grew weary of swimming and wanted dry land. One by one the animals dove to retrieve earth from the bottom — the otter, the beaver — but each failed, gasping for air. Finally the humble muskrat took his turn. He was under the water so long the others thought he had drowned. At last he surfaced, near death, with a tiny clump of mud in his paw. He Who Creates Us By Thought summoned Turtle to the surface and placed the mud on Turtle's back. The Creator caused the mud to grow and spread across Turtle's shell, forming Turtle Island, the North American continent. From a tree growing on this new land sprouted the first man and woman, the ancestors of all people.
source: Nanticoke Lenape Museum resources; Brinton - The Lenape and Their Legends; native-languages.org

title: Rainbow Crow (Mànàka'has)
summary: In the time before fire, the world was cold and dark. The animals suffered, unable to keep warm or cook their food. Kishelamakank, the Creator, took pity on them and placed a great fire at the top of a high mountain, but only the birds could reach it. The crow, who in those days had a beautiful rainbow-colored song and iridescent feathers, volunteered to fetch the fire. Crow flew to the summit and seized a burning coal in his beak. The flames scorched his colorful feathers, turning them black, and the smoke choked his beautiful voice into a harsh caw. Despite the pain, crow carried the fire down to the people. The Creator honored crow's sacrifice by declaring that his black feathers would forever shimmer with the rainbow's ghost, visible when the sun catches them at the right angle — a reminder of his noble deed.
source: native-languages.org Lenape Legends; Delaware oral tradition

title: Nanapush and the Sacred Pipe
summary: After Kishelamakank created the world, the human tribes fell into conflict over a magical bear tooth, leading to wars and divisions between families. The Creator saw that the people had lost their way and sent the spirit Nanapush to restore harmony. Nanapush gathered the warring peoples together at a council fire. He introduced the sacred pipe ceremony, teaching them to smoke tobacco as a sign of peace. He declared that no pipe could be lit in anger, and that to smoke together was to call the Creator as a witness to one's peaceful intentions. The people accepted Nanapush's teachings and laid down their weapons. From that day forward, the Lenape used the sacred pipe to seal agreements, welcome strangers, and pray directly to Kishelamakank, a practice that continues to this day.
source: ordoluporumpma.org; native-languages.org Lenape creation

title: The Big House Ceremony (Xinkwikaon)
summary: The Big House Ceremony is the paramount spiritual tradition of the Lenape people. According to oral tradition, the ceremony was given to the ancestors long ago when the people lived in the East. It is held in a special longhouse built to face the east, with carved faces on the center posts representing the Creator and the spirits. The ceremony lasts for twelve nights, during which the people pray, sing, and dance to fulfill their obligations to the spiritual forces of the universe. Participants use turtle-shell rattles and carved drumsticks, and each night a new fire is kindled with a fire-drill. The ceremony expresses gratitude to Kishelamakank, the four directions, and all of creation. The tradition declined during the forced removals but was revived through the dedication of elders who preserved the sacred songs and the original words.
source: Speck, Frank G. - A Study of the Delaware Indian Big House Ceremony; Harrington - Religion and Ceremonies of the Lenape

title: Nanabozho and the Creation of the World (Ojibwe)
summary: The Ojibwe creation story tells how Nanabozho, the son of Epingishmook (the West Wind) and a human mother, was born with great spiritual powers. After his mother died, he was raised by his grandmother Nokomis. When the underwater panthers and horned serpents killed his beloved wolf-brother, Nanabozho pursued them and in his rage caused a great flood that covered the earth. The waters rose until even the tallest mountains were submerged. Nanabozho and Nokomis floated on a log with the surviving animals. One by one, the animals tried to dive for earth — the loon, the otter, the beaver — until the muskrat finally succeeded, surfacing nearly dead with a pawful of mud. From this, Nanabozho created the new world on the back of the Great Turtle. He then filled it with plants, animals, and the first people.
source: Johnston, Basil - The Mishomis Book; native-languages.org Chippewa Legends; Ojibwe oral tradition

title: The Windigo Baby
summary: A newly married couple had a baby boy who seemed normal until one day, while still in his cradleboard, he spoke to his parents saying he would visit the Sky Spirit. That night, the baby vanished from his cradleboard. The parents found tiny tracks leading to the lake, then enormous footprints beyond. The child had transformed into a Windigo, a cannibalistic ice monster. The creature walked across the lake toward the Sky Spirit's home, which was protected by fifty Thunderers who threw lightning bolts at approaching threats. When the Windigo approached, the Thunderers fought it with lightning, finally striking it dead. It fell like a great tree. When the people chopped it up, they found it was made of ice, and in the center was a tiny infant six inches long — the original baby — with a hole in its head where the lightning had struck.
source: Milwaukee Public Museum - Ojibwe Oral Tradition; native-languages.org Chippewa Legends

title: The Little Girl Who Defeated the Windigo
summary: A Windigo as tall as a white pine tree approached a village, its approach signaled by a kettle swinging over the fire and trees cracking from the bitter cold. The villagers were terrified, but a wise old grandmother's granddaughter, a tiny girl, asked for two peeled sumac sticks as long as her arms. She told her grandmother to melt a kettle of tallow. When the Windigo came over the hill, the little girl ran out to meet it with her two dogs. She grew bigger and bigger with each step until she was as tall as the Windigo herself. She struck the Windigo with the sumac sticks, which had turned to copper, crushing its skull. Then she drank the hot tallow and shrank back to her normal size. The Windigo, when chopped up, was made of ice with a human body at its core. The village celebrated the little girl as their savior.
source: Milwaukee Public Museum - Ojibwe Oral Tradition; native-languages.org

title: Mishipeshu, the Underwater Panther
summary: Mishipeshu, the Great Lynx, is one of the most powerful water beings in Ojibwe mythology, ruling the depths of Lake Superior from his home on Michipicoten Island. He has the head and paws of a giant cat but is covered in scales with dagger-like spikes along his back and tail. He is the master of all water creatures, including snakes, and can cause storms that capsize canoes and drown the unwary. Mishipeshu is the eternal opponent of the Thunderbirds who rule the skies. In one story, four Ojibwe warriors traveled to Mishipeshu's island home to take copper, but his eerie voice surrounded them and they barely escaped with their lives. Travelers traditionally made offerings of tobacco before crossing large lakes to appease the underwater panther and ensure safe passage.
source: Wikipedia - Mishipeshu; native-languages.org; Kohl - Kitchi-Gami

title: Wunzh and the Origin of Indian Corn (Mon-Daw-Min)
summary: A young boy named Wunzh went into the forest to fast and pray for guidance, as was the custom of his people. After many days of fasting, a spirit appeared to him dressed in green and yellow, with feathers on his head. The spirit challenged Wunzh to wrestle him. Though weak from fasting, Wunzh fought with all his strength. The spirit returned every day, and each day Wunzh grew stronger. Finally, the spirit revealed himself as Mon-Daw-Min, the gift of the Creator. He told Wunzh to kill him, bury his body in the ground, and tend the grave. Wunzh obeyed, and from the spirit's body sprouted the first corn plant. The kernels were his teeth, the silk his hair, and the stalk his body. Wunzh brought this sacred knowledge to his people, who have cultivated corn ever since.
source: native-languages.org Chippewa Legends; Schoolcraft, Henry Rowe - Algic Researches

title: The Dream Fast: The Boy Who Became a Robin
summary: An Ojibwe boy underwent his vision fast alone in the wilderness, seeking a guardian spirit as was traditional for young people. He prayed and fasted for many days without eating, asking the Creator for guidance. On the final day of his fast, a spirit appeared to him in a vision and promised that he would become a messenger between the people and the spirit world. When he returned to his village, his grandfather saw the change in him and understood that the fast had been successful. Later, the boy was transformed into a robin — the first robin — with a red breast that represents the fire and passion of his vision. From that day forward, the robin's song each morning reminds the people that the spirit world is close and that those who seek visions with a pure heart will be answered.
source: native-languages.org Chippewa Legends; Johnston - Mishomis Book

title: The Girls Who Wished to Marry Stars
summary: Two Ojibwe girls slept outside one summer night, gazing at the stars. They began to wish out loud — one wished to marry the brightest star, the other wished to marry the Morning Star. To their astonishment, the stars descended and took them to the sky world. At first, everything was wonderful beyond imagination, but the girls soon grew homesick. They missed their families and the familiar earth. Their star husbands warned them never to dig in a certain spot, but curiosity overcame them and they dug, creating a hole through which they could see the earth below. They wove a rope of basswood bark and tried to climb down, but the rope broke, and they fell. The first girl turned into a shooting star, forever falling between worlds. The second girl landed safely and returned to her people, but she was never quite the same, always gazing at the stars.
source: Ojibwe oral tradition; native-languages.org Chippewa Legends

title: The Huron Creation: Aataentsic and the Earth Diver
summary: In the beginning, the Wyandot people lived in the Sky Land. The shaman told the people to dig around the roots of the wild apple tree by the chief's lodge. As they dug, they broke through the floor of the Sky Land, and the tree and the chief's daughter Aataentsic fell through. Below was only water. Swans swimming on the water heard a thunderclap — the first thunder ever heard — and looked up to see the woman falling. They swam together so she could land on their backs. The animals held a council called by the Great Turtle. One by one the divers went down — Otter, Muskrat, Beaver — seeking earth from the tree's roots. Each drowned. Finally, Old Toad volunteered. The animals laughed at her smallness, but she dove and surfaced near death with grains of earth in her mouth. The Small Turtle spread the earth around the Big Turtle's shell, where it grew until it became the island of the world.
source: Barbeau, Marius - Huron and Wyandot Mythology (1915); sacred-texts.com; Wikisource Wyandot creation

title: Ioskeha and Tawescaron: The Twin Brothers
summary: After Aataentsic fell to the new earth, she gave birth to twin daughters. One of these daughters, Tekawerahkwa, became the mother of twin sons — Ioskeha and Tawescaron. The twins were opposites in every way. Ioskeha, the older twin, was the benefactor of the Huron people. He created the sun from his mother's body, brought light to the world, slew monsters, gave the people corn and tobacco, and taught them how to live. Tawescaron, the younger twin, was his antagonist. He created monsters, darkness, and winter, and constantly tried to undo his brother's good works. Ioskeha represented light, creation, and life, and was associated with the sun. Tawescaron represented darkness, destruction, and death, and was associated with the night and winter. Despite their opposition, both were necessary for the balance of the world.
source: Barbeau - Huron and Wyandot Mythology; native-languages.org Wyandot Legends

title: The Race of Good and Evil
summary: After the world was made, Good and Evil existed as two brothers. Evil constantly spoiled Good's work, making stony places and poisonous fruits. Good decided to destroy Evil. He proposed a race, and each brother had to reveal his greatest fear. Evil feared buck's horns; Good feared braided Indian grass. Evil filled Good's path with braided grass, which Good chewed for strength when he tired. Good filled Evil's path with deer antlers. Evil struggled badly, tripping and falling. When Evil begged to stop, Good refused. Toward evening, Evil collapsed. Good took buck's horns and killed him. But Evil's spirit returned and spoke with their grandmother, who loved Evil. Evil declared he would go to the northwest land where those who follow him never return, condemning death upon the world. From that day, Good ruled the living and Evil ruled the dead, and the cycle of life and death was established forever.
source: Judson, Katherine - Myths and Legends of the Mississippi Valley (1914); native-languages.org Wyandot

title: Hinnon, the Thunder Spirit
summary: The Huron believe in the Thunders, a group of powerful storm spirits who live in the sky and look like a cross between men and giant turkeys. Their leader is Hinnon, whom they call Grandfather. Hinnon and his warriors pursue and destroy the great horned serpents and other monsters that threaten the world. When lightning flashes, it is Hinnon throwing his thunderbolt; when thunder rolls, it is his voice. The Huron make offerings of tobacco to Hinnon before long journeys and during storms. In the Huron creation myth, the first thunderclap occurred when Aataentsic fell through the Sky Land, marking the beginning of the new world below. The Thunderers also gave the people tobacco and taught them the proper ceremonies to honor the Great Spirit.
source: Barbeau - Huron and Wyandot Mythology; native-languages.org Huron/Wyandot

title: Why Leaves Change Color in Autumn
summary: According to Wyandot legend, the changing colors of autumn leaves are the result of a great battle between the summer spirits and the winter spirits. Every autumn, the giant who rules the North Wind comes down from his cold kingdom and fights with the spirit of summer. During the battle, the leaves are bruised and battered, turning yellow, red, and orange from the struggle. The frost giants paint the remaining leaves with ice colors before stripping them from the trees entirely. Another version says that when the Thunders retreat south for the winter, their departing tears fall on the leaves, staining them red and gold. The fallen leaves cover the ground as a blanket to protect Mother Earth during her winter sleep. When spring returns and the Thunders come back north, new green leaves sprout, and the cycle begins again.
source: native-languages.org Wyandot Legends; Barbeau - Huron and Wyandot Mythology

title: Onditichiae: The Thunder Beings
summary: The Onditichiae are the Thunder beings of the Wyandot and related Tobacco Indians. They are a race of giant sky beings who control the storms. According to tradition, they were created to serve as the eternal enemies of all water monsters. Whenever a great serpent or underwater panther becomes too dangerous, the Onditichiae descend from the sky and hunt it down with lightning. Their leader Hinnon assigns each Thunder being a specific territory to protect. The noise of battle between Thunders and serpents is what people hear as summer thunderstorms. The Wyandot believe that if the Onditichiae ever stopped their vigilance, the water monsters would overrun the earth. Offerings of tobacco smoke are made to honor and strengthen the Onditichiae in their ceaseless war against chaos.
source: Barbeau - Huron and Wyandot Mythology; native-languages.org Tobacco Indian myths

title: The Mi'kmaq Creation Story (Seven Levels of Creation)
summary: The Mi'kmaq creation story unfolds across seven sacred levels. First there was the Great Spirit Kji Niskam, who existed before all things. From the first spark of creation came Grandfather Sun and Grandmother Moon. The third level is Mother Earth, who bears the spirits of the ancestors. On the fourth level, the first man Glooscap was created when lightning struck the earth three times — the first strike gave him form, the second gave him seven sacred parts to his head, and the third freed him to walk. Glooscap lay on the earth for 365 nights with his head to the rising sun. On the fifth level, his grandmother Nogami was born from dewdrops on a rock. On the sixth level, his nephew Nataoa-nsen was born from the foam of the sea, created by Whirlwind. On the seventh level, Glooscap's mother appeared from dew collected on a leaf. Seven families were created from the sparks of the Great Fire, and the Mi'kmaq are one of these seven original families.
source: Stephen Augustine - Mi'kmaq Creation Story; History Museum of Canada; native-languages.org

title: Glooscap and the Evil Frog Monster
summary: In the days of the Mi'kmaq ancestors, a monstrous frog spirit had swallowed all the water in the world. Rivers dried up, lakes vanished, and people and animals were dying of thirst. The great being Glooscap took pity on his people. He tracked the enormous frog to its lair and engaged it in a titanic struggle. He struck the frog with such force that it coughed up all the water it had swallowed. Water gushed forth, refilling the rivers and lakes. Some of the animals present were so relieved and joyful that they leaped into the water and were transformed into fish and other aquatic creatures. The lesson of this story is that even a small creature (the frog should not be underestimated in its power to do harm, but that goodness and determination will ultimately prevail. The foam of Glooscap's battle can still be seen in the whitecaps of the Bay of Fundy.
source: Wikipedia - Glooscap; native-languages.org Wabanaki; Hill, Kay - Glooscap and His Magic

title: Glooscap and the Great Beaver: The Five Islands
summary: Glooscap once transformed himself into a giant beaver to create a safe haven for his people. Swimming in the Bay of Fundy, he slapped his enormous tail against the water with such tremendous force that it churned up the earth from the seabed, creating the Five Islands of Nova Scotia. When Glooscap slept, Nova Scotia was his bed and Prince Edward Island his pillow. In another tradition, when Glooscap finished painting all the splendor of the world, he dipped his brush into a blend of all the colors left over and created Abegweit, meaning Cradled on the Waves, his favorite island — Prince Edward Island. The Annapolis Valley was also shaped by Glooscap's hand. These landscape features are constant reminders to the Mi'kmaq of their culture hero's presence and his love for the land and its people.
source: Wikipedia - Glooscap; Rand, Silas Tertius - Micmac legends

title: Oochigeas and the Invisible Boy (The Mi'kmaq Cinderella)
summary: A great chief had a son who was invisible. Only a woman who spoke the truth could see him. Many vain young women tried and failed, always lying that they could see the Invisible Boy. In a nearby village lived Oochigeas, the youngest daughter, whose face and hands were scarred from tending fires because her cruel elder sisters forced her to do all the hard work. Her ragged clothes were made of bark. She decided to try her luck and walked to the chief's lodge in tatters. The Invisible Boy's sister asked her the question: Can you see my brother? Oochigeas answered truthfully that she could not. Then the sister gave her fine clothes and combed her hair. Oochigeas approached again, and this time she saw the Invisible Boy — a handsome young man sitting at the edge of the sky. They married, and her kindness and truthfulness were rewarded, while her cruel sisters were punished.
source: native-languages.org Mi'kmaq Legends; Rand - Micmac legends

title: Mooin, the Bear's Child
summary: A young Mi'kmaq boy wandered into the forest and became lost. A great she-bear found him and, instead of harming him, adopted him as her own cub. The boy grew up among the bear people, learning their ways and understanding the language of animals. He became strong and brave, but he always remembered that he was human. When he was grown, the bear people told him it was time to return to his own kind. Before he left, the bears taught him the songs and ceremonies they used to honor the Great Spirit, and gave him permission to hunt them for food — but only if he always showed respect and gratitude. The boy returned to the Mi'kmaq people as a great hunter and medicine man, teaching them the ceremony for honoring the spirits of the animals they killed. This is the origin of the Mi'kmaq hunting ceremonies and the Bear Clan tradition.
source: native-languages.org Mi'kmaq Legends

title: The Girl Chenoo
summary: A beautiful Mi'kmaq woman was lost in the frozen north and suffered terrible hunger. To survive, she ate human flesh and was transformed into a Chenoo — a cannibalistic ice monster with a heart of ice and an insatiable hunger for human beings. Her family searched for her but when they found her, she had become a monstrous giant with claws and fangs, her heart visible through her translucent chest. The family's oldest grandmother did not abandon her. She sang the old healing songs and performed the sacred ceremonies to warm her granddaughter's frozen heart. Through love and prayer, the grandmother melted the ice around the Chenoo's heart and restored her to human form. The story teaches the Mi'kmaq understanding that even those who become monsters through tragedy can be healed through family love, community, and the power of traditional ceremonies.
source: native-languages.org Mi'kmaq Legends; Leland - Algonquin Legends

title: Gluskabe and the Wind Eagle
summary: Gluskabe (Glooscap) was annoyed by the constant wind that made life difficult for his people. He traveled to the great mountain where the Wind Eagle — a colossal bird whose wingbeats created all the wind in the world — was perched. Gluskabe climbed the mountain and captured the Wind Eagle, tying its wings together so they could not move. Immediately the wind stopped. At first, everything was calm and pleasant, but soon the air grew hot and still. The water became stagnant, the people could not breathe, and the world began to suffocate. Gluskabe realized that the wind was necessary for life. He returned to the mountaintop and untied the Wind Eagle's wings — just a little — allowing a gentle breeze to flow. He learned that while too much wind is destructive, no wind at all is even worse. The Wind Eagle's wings have been tied ever since, which is why we have breezes but not constant gales.
source: native-languages.org Wabanaki Legends; Abenaki oral tradition

title: Tabaldak Creates Glooscap and Malsumis
summary: In Abenaki tradition, the Great Spirit Tabaldak first created the world and all the people. When he finished making humans, he wiped the dust from his hands, and from this divine dust were born twin brothers — Glooscap and Malsumis. Glooscap was given the power to create a good world, full of kind animals, sweet fruits, and gentle rivers. Malsumis, the wolf, was his opposite, creating only evil — dangerous animals, thorns, and crooked rivers that flooded the land. From birth, Malsumis resented his brother and plotted to destroy him. Glooscap discovered that his brother could only be killed by a blow from a fern root. Malsumis tricked the animals into revealing this secret and attacked Glooscap, but the good twin was saved by Grandmother Woodchuck. Glooscap eventually defeated Malsumis and turned him into stone, where he remains, the embodiment of all that is harmful in nature.
source: Wikipedia - Glooscap; native-languages.org Abenaki Legends

title: The Origin of Corn: First Mother's Sacrifice
summary: In the time of the first people, the Abenaki were hungry. Gluskabe's grandmother Nokomes taught the people to survive on wild plants, but food was scarce. First Mother, the first woman created by Gichi Niwaskw and Gluskabe, saw her children starving. She told her husband to kill her and drag her body across a clearing. Weeping, he obeyed. Where her body passed, corn sprang up — tall green stalks bearing golden ears. Where her heart lay, beans grew to climb the corn. Where her feet lay, squash spread across the ground. First Mother had given her life so her children would never starve. The Abenaki honor her sacrifice in every planting season, and the Three Sisters — corn, beans, and squash — are always planted together, as First Mother intended, supporting each other just as the people must support each other.
source: native-languages.org Abenaki Legends; Penobscot oral tradition

title: Gluskonba and the Four Wishes
summary: Gluskonba (Glooscap) offered four Abenaki men a single wish each. The first man wished to be powerful and rich, and was turned into a great pine tree — tall and strong, but rooted in one place forever. The second man wished to be feared by everyone, and was turned into a bear — powerful but alone. The third man wished to live forever, and was turned into a rock — eternal but without feeling or movement. The fourth man listened to the fate of the others and asked only for the wisdom to accept whatever Gluskonba gave him. Gluskonba blessed this man with a long, happy life, many children, and the love of his community. The first three had received what they asked for, but found their wishes brought misery instead of happiness. The story teaches that true wisdom lies not in seeking power or wealth, but in accepting the gifts of the Creator with gratitude.
source: native-languages.org Abenaki Legends; Bruchac, Joseph - Gluskabe Stories

title: Pamola, the Snow Bird Spirit of Mount Katahdin
summary: Pamola (also called Bemola or Pomola) is a powerful snow bird spirit who dwells on the summit of Mount Katahdin, the highest mountain in Maine and the most sacred peak of the Penobscot and Abenaki people. Pamola has the body of a man and the head and wings of a giant eagle and makes the cold weather by beating his enormous wings. He fiercely guards the mountain against trespassers, sending blizzards to drive away those who dare approach his domain. According to legend, a Penobscot woman once married Pamola and lived with him on the mountain, but she eventually grew homesick and returned to her village, bringing with her the knowledge of how to survive the mountain's winter. The Pamola legend serves as a warning to respect the sacred power of Mount Katahdin and the spirits that dwell there.
source: native-languages.org Penobscot Legends; Penobscot oral tradition

title: Gluskabe and Dzeedzeez (The Baby Who Outmatched the Hero)
summary: Gluskabe, the great culture hero, was resting in his lodge after his many labors when he heard a baby crying. The baby was Dzeedzeez (Wasis in Passamaquoddy), whose mother had left him alone while she gathered firewood. Gluskabe, confident in his powers, decided to quiet the baby himself. He tried using his magic, but the baby only cried louder. He sang his most powerful healing songs, but Dzeedzeez screamed. He tried every trick he knew — shapeshifting, illusions, promises of gifts — and Dzeedzeez simply stared at him and continued wailing. Finally, the great Gluskabe, who had defeated monsters and giants and even his evil twin brother, was utterly defeated by a baby. He left the lodge humbled, realizing that some challenges cannot be overcome by strength or magic. The story teaches that wisdom sometimes comes from recognizing one's own limitations.
source: native-languages.org Wabanaki Legends; Leland - Algonquin Legends

title: How Gluskabe Stole Tobacco
summary: In the early days, tobacco was guarded jealously by Grasshopper (or a giant), who kept it hidden from humanity. The people prayed for tobacco to use in their ceremonies, as it was the sacred plant that carried their prayers to the Creator. Gluskabe heard their pleas and devised a plan. He transformed himself into a small, harmless creature — sometimes a rabbit, sometimes a mouse — and snuck into Grasshopper's lodge while he was away. He found the tobacco bundled in a pouch and escaped with it. Grasshopper pursued but Gluskabe was too swift. He distributed the tobacco among the Wabanaki tribes, teaching them how to plant and harvest it and how to offer it in sacred pipes. The smoke rising from the pipes carries the people's prayers directly to Gichi Niwaskw, the Great Spirit, and Gluskabe is honored for this gift that connects the people with the divine.
source: native-languages.org Wabanaki Legends

title: How Glooscap Found the Summer
summary: In the long-ago time, there was no summer — only endless winter. The earth was covered in snow and ice, the people were freezing, and the animals suffered. Glooscap decided to find the spirit of summer and bring it back. He traveled to the far north where Winter, a powerful giant, held Summer captive in his ice lodge. Glooscap challenged Winter to a contest of power. They told stories for many days, and as Winter talked, frost grew thicker on the walls. But Glooscap's stories were of warmth, green leaves, and flowing water. He so charmed the spirit of Summer that she awakened and began to melt Winter's ice. Winter retreated north, and Summer followed Glooscap back to the people. Ever since, Summer and Winter alternate — Winter rules for half the year, but when his power wanes, Summer returns and the world thaws. The Mi'kmaq celebrate this cycle as the eternal rhythm of life.
source: native-languages.org Mi'kmaq Legends; Rand - Micmac legends

title: Ableegumooch: How Rabbit Lost His Tail
summary: Ableegumooch the Rabbit was once the most vain of all animals, boasting constantly about his beautiful long tail. He teased Otter for having only a small, stubby tail. Annoyed, Otter challenged Rabbit to a fishing contest. Rabbit, who knew nothing about fishing but would never admit it, agreed. Otter caught fish after fish by diving into the river, while Rabbit sat on the bank with a line, catching nothing. In desperation, Rabbit tied his line to his tail and cast it far into the water, but instead of catching a fish, a giant turtle grabbed his tail and pulled. Rabbit escaped but his magnificent tail was snapped off, leaving only the short stub rabbits have to this day. Otter laughed at Rabbit's vanity, and Rabbit learned that pride comes before a fall. Glooscap, watching from his lodge, declared that Rabbit's new tail would remind all creatures of the dangers of vanity.
source: native-languages.org Mi'kmaq Legends; Hill, Kay - Glooscap and His Magic

title: Gluskabe and the Monster Frog
summary: A devastating drought struck the land of the Wabanaki. Rivers dried to dust, the lakes shrank, and the people were dying. A giant frog monster had swallowed all the water and held it captive in its bloated body. The people called to Gluskabe for help. Gluskabe found the giant frog sunning itself on a rock, swollen with all the water of the world. He approached carefully and, using his magic, made the frog laugh. As the frog's great mouth opened, a flood of water poured forth, refilling the rivers and lakes. Some of the animals that had been trapped in the frog's belly leaped out transformed — those who had been four-legged became fish, and those who had been birds became waterfowl. Gluskabe then shrunk the frog down to its present size and declared that from then on, frogs would live near water but never again swallow more than they needed.
source: native-languages.org Wabanaki Legends; Penobscot oral tradition

title: The Flying Canoe (Passamaquoddy)
summary: Three Passamaquoddy brothers, all skilled canoe-builders, competed to see who could build the finest canoe. The eldest two built large, sturdy vessels decorated with porcupine quills and painted designs. But the youngest brother, instead of building an ordinary canoe, consulted the spirits and built a magical canoe that could fly through the air as easily as it could glide across water. When the day of judgment came, the two older brothers mocked the youngest's strange craft. But when the youngest sang a special song, his canoe rose into the air and circled the village. The chief was so impressed that he gave his daughter to the youngest brother in marriage. The story celebrates innovation and the wisdom of seeking spiritual guidance rather than merely following tradition, a theme common to Wabanaki folktales.
source: native-languages.org Passamaquoddy Legends; Maliseet-Passamaquoddy oral tradition

title: The Origin of the Thunderbird (Wabanaki)
summary: In the Wabanaki tradition, the Thunderbird was once a great chief who loved his people so much that after his death, the Great Spirit transformed him into a giant bird whose wings make the thunder and whose eyes flash lightning. He was given dominion over the sky and tasked with hunting the great horned serpent Weewillmekq (Jipijka'm), who lurked in lakes and devoured people. Whenever the horned serpent rose to attack, the Thunderbird descended from the clouds and struck it with lightning. The Thunderbird had a nest on the highest peak of the mountains, and its eggs were made of solid lightning. The Wabanaki honored the Thunderbird with offerings of tobacco, asking it to bring rain for the crops and to protect them from the underworld monsters.
source: native-languages.org Wabanaki Legends; Rand - Micmac legends

title: The Owl Husband (Passamaquoddy)
summary: A beautiful young Passamaquoddy woman was visited each night by a mysterious man who came to her lodge after dark. He was kind and gentle, and she grew to love him, though she had never seen his face clearly. Her parents warned her to discover his identity. One night, she placed ashes near the doorway. When the man left, his footprints showed the marks of an owl's claws. The next night, she hid and watched, and saw her husband transform into a great horned owl and fly away. Heartbroken but determined, she followed him into the forest. The owl tried to keep her as his wife in his tree-home, but she longed for her human family. Finally, the owl released her, but she was never the same — she had seen the spirit world and could not fully return to human life. The story teaches that love between different kinds of beings cannot overcome the fundamental boundaries of nature.
source: native-languages.org Passamaquoddy Legends

title: Kanati and Selu: The Origin of Game and Corn (Cherokee)
summary: Kanati (The Lucky Hunter) and Selu (Corn) were the first Cherokee man and woman. Kanati would hunt by moving a rock at the entrance of a cave, releasing an animal and killing it. Their son befriended a Wild Boy born from the blood of slaughtered game. The boys secretly followed Kanati and discovered the cave; they opened it, allowing all animals to escape, which is why humans must hunt for food today. Selu would produce corn by rubbing her stomach, and beans by rubbing her sides. When the boys discovered her secret, Selu knew she would die. She instructed them to drag her body seven times around a cleared circle, then seven times inside it, and corn would grow by morning. The boys did not complete the ritual properly, which is why corn only grows in certain places. The story establishes the Cherokee relationship with hunting, agriculture, and the origin of food scarcity.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; Mooney, Myths of the Cherokee

title: The Creation of Earth and the Great Buzzard (Cherokee)
summary: In the beginning, all was water, and animals lived in the sky world above the stone vault. The water beetle Dâyuni'sï volunteered to explore below and brought up soft mud from the ocean floor, which grew into the earth. The earth was initially soft and flat. The Great Buzzard flew down to survey the land, and his wings striking the ground created the mountains and valleys of Cherokee territory. When the animals saw what the Buzzard had done, they feared he would make the earth too rough, so they called him back, leaving the Cherokee mountains steep and the valleys deep. The sun was set on a track to travel across the sky each day. This foundational myth explains the geography of the Southern Appalachian region and establishes the Cherokee cosmic order.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; Mooney, Myths of the Cherokee

title: Uktena: The Horned Serpent (Cherokee)
summary: Uktena is a massive horned serpent covered in shining scales, with a single blazing crystal in its forehead that grants supernatural vision. The first Uktena was once a man who attempted to kill the sun. He failed and was transformed into the monstrous serpent as punishment. Uktena dwells in deep rivers, lakes, and under mountains. Its gaze alone can paralyze or kill. Only the bravest medicine men and heroes dare to hunt Uktena, seeking the Ulûñsû'tî, the diamond-like crystal from its forehead, which grants great power to whoever possesses it. The most famous story involves a hunter who stalked an Uktena to its lair and, after a great battle, retrieved the crystal. The stone brought power but also danger, and was eventually hidden away in a sacred place.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; native-languages.org Uktena

title: The Nunnehi: Cherokee Spirit People (Cherokee)
summary: The Nunnehi, meaning 'The Travelers' or 'Those Who Live Anywhere,' are a race of invisible spirit people who dwell in the mountains and forests of Cherokee country. They are friendly toward humans and often appear as regal Cherokee warriors. The Nunnehi are said to be great dancers and musicians; travelers sometimes hear their singing and drumming coming from inside hollow mountains. They have saved Cherokee war parties from defeat by magically enlarging their numbers or by confusing enemy forces. The Nunnehi are known to invite favored humans into their underground dwellings, where time passes differently — a visitor may spend what seems like a single night only to return home and find that years have passed. They are generally benevolent but withdraw from contact if treated with disrespect.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; native-languages.org Nunnehi

title: Yunwi Tsunsdi: The Little People (Cherokee)
summary: Yunwi Tsunsdi are the Cherokee Little People — small, child-sized nature spirits who live in caves, under rocks, and in the dense forest. They are usually invisible but sometimes reveal themselves to humans they trust. They possess powerful magic and are said to have taught medicine to certain shamans. The Little People can be helpful to those who respect them, leading lost hunters to safety or granting blessings. However, they are easily angered by disrespect and punish those who bother them excessively with confusion or misfortune. They are known to borrow tools from villagers, always returning them with small gifts of thanks. The Yunwi Tsunsdi are not to be spoken of lightly; those who encounter them rarely discuss the experience directly.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; native-languages.org Yunwi Tsunsdi

title: Spearfinger (U'tlun'ta): The Stone Witch (Cherokee)
summary: Spearfinger, also known as Nûñ'yunu'ï or 'Stone-dress,' is a fearsome Cherokee monster and witch who lives along the eastern side of Tennessee and western North Carolina. She has a long, sharp forefinger on her right hand like a spear or obsidian knife, which she uses to cut open her victims. She wears a dress made of stone that makes her nearly invulnerable. Spearfinger feeds on human livers, especially those of children. Her mouth is perpetually stained with blood. She can mimic the voices of loved ones to lure victims close. According to legend, she was eventually defeated by a group of Cherokee warriors who discovered that her only vulnerable spot was a small point on her palm.
