They shot arrows into her palm, and she fell from the mountain where she lived.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; Sacred Texts - U'tlun'ta

title: Raven Mocker (Kâ'lanû Ahkyeli'skï) (Cherokee)
summary: Kâ'lanû Ahkyeli'skï, the Raven Mocker, is a malevolent Cherokee spirit that preys on the dying. These witches are said to be ordinary-looking people during the day who transform at night into raven-like spirits. They gather around the home of a dying person and torment them, stealing their remaining years of life for themselves. The Raven Mocker flies with a sound like rushing wind and can only be seen by medicine men. After a person dies, the Raven Mockers eat the heart of the victim. The only defense against a Raven Mocker is a medicine man who knows the proper rituals to drive them away. Raven Mockers are visible only to medicine men, who can identify them even in their human form by their sleepless, bloodshot eyes and their habit of appearing exhausted from their nighttime flights.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs

title: The Thunder Beings (Aniyvdaqualosgi) (Cherokee)
summary: The Cherokee believe in the Thunder Beings, known as Aniyvdaqualosgi or Ani-Yuntikwalaski. The Great Thunder and his two sons, the Thunder Boys, live in the land of the west above the sky vault. They dress in lightning and rainbows. Unlike many tribes where thunder spirits take bird form, Cherokee Thunderers are human in form — powerful and dangerous but generally friendly to humans. The priests pray to the Thunder beings for rain and blessings. Some lesser thunder beings live closer to the Earth's surface in cliffs, mountains, and waterfalls, and these are more mischievous, sometimes causing harm. Thunder is said to be the sound of the Great Thunder's wings or his voice rolling across the sky. Lightning is the fire from his eyes.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; Mooney, Myths of the Cherokee

title: Grandmother Spider Steals the Fire (Cherokee)
summary: In the beginning, the only fire belonged to the Thunders in the sky world. The animals held a council to decide who would bring fire to the people. Many brave animals volunteered but failed: Raven tried to carry an ember but his feathers turned black; Screech Owl's eyes were burned red; Hooting Owl's eyes were singed white. Finally, the tiny Water Spider, Dilsdohdi, volunteered. She spun a bowl-shaped web, placed it on her back, and crossed the water to the sky world. She placed a small ember in her web-basket and carried it safely back across the water to the people. This is why the Water Spider has black downy hair with red stripes — the smoke and fire marked her. Fire became the sacred gift that transformed human life.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; Mooney, Myths of the Cherokee

title: The Origin of Disease and Medicine (Cherokee)
summary: Long ago, all plants, animals, and humans lived together in harmony, and animals were larger and stronger. As the human population grew, they began killing animals without respect. The animals held councils to decide how to protect themselves. The Deer council decreed that any hunter who killed a deer without asking forgiveness would develop rheumatism. The Birds, Insects, and smaller animals created diseases to afflict humans. But the Plants heard of the animals' plans. Since plants had always been friends with humans, they vowed that for every disease the animals created, they would provide a cure. This is why every plant has medicinal purpose, and why medicine men must learn from the spirits of plants which herbs to use. The story establishes the Cherokee understanding of balance between humans, animals, and plants.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; native-languages.org

title: Stone Coat (Nûñ'yunu'ï): The Rock Giants (Cherokee)
summary: Stone Coat, also called Nûñ'yunu'ï, is a race of giant beings covered in stone-like skin that makes them nearly invulnerable. They dwell in the remote mountains and caves of Cherokee territory. These rock giants are cannibalistic and prey upon humans who venture too far into their domain. They are incredibly strong, capable of throwing boulders and tearing down trees. Stone Coat is also associated with Spearfinger, who wears a stone dress. The giants' only weakness is their vulnerability to certain spiritual practices. Cherokee medicine men knew rituals to confuse or drive away Stone Coats. The legend may have been inspired by encounters with the fossilized remains of prehistoric megafauna in the Appalachian region, and it serves as a warning against venturing into dangerous, unknown territory.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; native-languages.org Stoneclad

title: Tlanuwa: The Giant Raptor (Cherokee)
summary: Tlanuwa is a giant mythological bird of prey with feathers made of impenetrable metal. This massive raptor could carry off humans and even small deer in its talons. Its feathers were said to be sharp as knives and could be launched as projectiles. The Tlanuwa lived in the highest mountain peaks and remote cliffs. In Cherokee legend, the Tlanuwa sometimes battled the Uktena serpent, their ancient enemy. The birds were also known to fight against humans who invaded their territory. The Tlanuwa appears in stories from many Southeastern tribes including the Creek and Natchez, suggesting it was a widespread figure in the regional mythology. Some accounts describe it as a benevolent protector spirit of the Cherokee people.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; native-languages.org Tlanuwa

title: The First Strawberries (Cherokee)
summary: The first man and the first woman quarreled, and the woman left their home in anger. The man, sad and remorseful, tried to bring her back. The Sun, taking pity on the man, created huckleberries along the path to tempt the woman to stop, but she paid them no mind. Next the Sun created blackberries, but she ignored them too. Finally, the Sun created strawberries — the first ever. When the woman saw the ripe, red berries, she stopped to taste one. The sweetness reminded her of happier times, and she began to gather them. As she looked back down the path, she saw her husband approaching, and she forgave him. They reconciled and returned home together. This Cherokee story explains the origin of strawberries and teaches the power of sweetness and forgiveness in mending relationships.
source: native-languages.org Cherokee Legends

title: Choctaw Creation: The Emergence from Nanih Waiya (Choctaw)
summary: In the beginning, there was a great mound called Nanih Waiya. The Creator fashioned the first people from the earth within this mound. These first Choctaw emerged from a long, dark cave that led from the center of the mound into daylight. After emerging, they scattered across the land to populate the earth. When a great flood arose, the Choctaw people fled by canoe to an island, guided by a dove. They prospered there but eventually overpopulated the island. They traveled back to the coast of Turtle Island and journeyed for hundreds of years, eventually reaching the Yucatan and finally settling in what is now Mississippi. They rebuilt their sacred Nanih Waiya and found a home for the bones of their ancestors that they had carried throughout their long migration.
source: Wikipedia - Choctaw mythology; Swanton, Source Material Choctaw Indians

title: Chata and Chicksah: The Separation of the Choctaw and Chickasaw (Choctaw/Chickasaw)
summary: Two brothers named Chata and Chicksah led their people from the west, following a sacred pole. Each night they planted the pole in the ground, and each morning they traveled in the direction it leaned. After an immensely long journey, they finally reached a place where the pole stood perfectly upright. There, they laid to rest the bones of their ancestors, which they had carried in buffalo-hide sacks from the original land in the west. The great earthwork mound of Nanih Waiya grew from that burial. Afterward, the land could not support all the people. Chicksah took half the people and departed north, becoming the Chickasaw tribe. Chata and the others remained near Nanih Waiya and became the Choctaw. This shared origin story explains the close relationship between the two tribes.
source: Wikipedia - Choctaw mythology

title: The Little People (Bohpoli/Kowi Anukasha) (Choctaw)
summary: The Choctaw believe in little people called Bohpoli ('the Thrower') or Kowi Anukasha ('Forest Dweller'). These are small beings about two feet tall who dwell alone in the thick, dark woods. They are mischievous but not malicious, often playfully throwing sticks and stones at travelers or hitting pine trees to create noise. All unexplained sounds in the forest are attributed to Bohpoli. They are never seen by common people, only by prophets and shamans. The little people are said to assist medicine men in creating their remedies. Sometimes Bohpoli would 'steal' children and take them into the woods to teach them about herbs and medicines, then return them to their homes. These children would grow up to become the tribe's doctors and healers.
source: Wikipedia - Choctaw mythology; native-languages.org Kowi Anukasha

title: Nalusa Falaya: The Shadow Being (Choctaw)
summary: Nalusa Falaya, meaning 'Long Black Being,' is a terrifying shadow creature from Choctaw mythology. It resembles a man but with a shriveled face, very small eyes, long pointed ears, and an elongated nose. Nalusa Falaya is known to frighten hunters and can transfer its power to do harm. It prefers to approach people by sliding on its stomach like a snake. The creature has many children who, when quite young, possess the peculiar ability to remove their internal organs at night, becoming small, luminous bodies seen along the borders of marshes. Nalusa Falaya represents the danger of allowing negative thoughts to take hold and serves as a warning to maintain courage and a pure heart.
source: Wikipedia - Choctaw mythology; native-languages.org Nalusa Falaya

title: Nalusa Chito: The Soul Eater (Choctaw)
summary: Nalusa Chito, also known as Impa Shilup ('Soul Eater'), is a great black shadow being of Choctaw legend. If individuals allow evil thoughts, depression, or negativity to enter their minds, Nalusa Chito will creep inside them and devour their souls. Many Choctaw people will not speak its name aloud, for fear of summoning the spirit. The being represents the internal battle against despair and moral weakness. The legend teaches the importance of maintaining a strong, positive spirit and guarding one's thoughts against darkness. Nalusa Chito cannot harm those who keep their hearts and minds pure. Traditional healers perform rituals to protect vulnerable individuals from this soul-eating presence.
source: Wikipedia - Choctaw mythology; Swanton, Source Material Choctaw Indians

title: Kashehotapalo (Choctaw)
summary: Kashehotapalo is a creature from Choctaw mythology that is neither man nor beast. It has the legs and hooves of a deer but the body of a man. Its head and face are small and shriveled, and it is said that anyone who looks at it will be visited by evil. Kashehotapalo delights in frightening hunters who venture near the swamps it inhabits. It sneaks up behind them and lets out a loud call that sounds like a woman's scream before quickly fleeing into the darkness. Though it does not physically harm people, it takes great pleasure in their terror. Its name comes from 'kasheho' (woman) and 'tapalo' (call). The creature serves as a guardian of the wild places, warning against entering certain swamps and forests.
source: Wikipedia - Choctaw mythology

title: Sinti Lapitta: The Horned Serpent (Choctaw)
summary: Sinti Lapitta is a great horned serpent from Choctaw mythology, a water monster with immense power. Described as a giant snake with a head like a panther or with two horns on its head, it dwells in deep rivers, lakes, and water holes. Unlike the malevolent horned serpents of some tribes, Sinti Lapitta was sometimes known to visit unusually wise young men, bestowing knowledge and power upon them. The serpent was both feared and respected, embodying the power of the water realm. It served as a symbol of the underworld and was associated with the chaotic forces that must be balanced against the order of the sky. Stories of Sinti Lapitta warn against disturbing sacred waters.
source: Wikipedia - Choctaw mythology

title: Heloha and Melatha: Thunder and Lightning Birds (Choctaw)
summary: In Choctaw mythology, Heloha (Thunder) and Melatha (Lightning) are two enormous birds responsible for the dramatic thunderstorms that sweep across the Mississippi region. Heloha, the female, lays her giant eggs high in the clouds, and the eggs rumble and roll around atop the clouds, creating the sound of thunder. Her mate, Melatha, is incredibly fast despite his huge size. As he streaks across the sky, he leaves a trail of sparks that become lightning. Together they create the powerful storms that bring rain to the land. The Choctaw respected these bird spirits and understood thunder and lightning as natural phenomena caused by living creatures, not abstract forces.
source: Wikipedia - Choctaw mythology

title: Okwa Naholo: White Water People (Choctaw)
summary: Okwa Naholo (or Oka Nahullo), meaning 'White people of the water,' are supernatural beings from Choctaw mythology who dwell in deep pools, rivers, and springs. They have light-colored skin like the underside of trout, which makes them almost invisible in the water. These powerful water spirits sometimes capture human beings, converting them into beings like themselves. Children were warned not to swim alone in deep water for fear of the Okwa Naholo dragging them under. They represent the dangerous yet beautiful aspects of the water realm, and their stories serve both to teach respect for the dangers of deep water and to explain mysterious disappearances near rivers and lakes.
source: Wikipedia - Choctaw mythology

title: Finkingo: The Stickball Game (Choctaw)
summary: The Choctaw game of stickball, known as Finkingo or kabocha, is featured in many Choctaw legends. According to tradition, the game was given to the Choctaw by the spirits to resolve conflicts without warfare. Stickball is played with two small rackets and a ball, and it is extremely fast and violent. The game was often played between villages, with hundreds of players on each side, goals miles apart, and games lasting from sunrise to sunset. One legend tells of a young warrior who won the hand of a chief's daughter by scoring the winning goal in a legendary match. Another story describes how stickball settled a territorial dispute between the Choctaw and a neighboring tribe, avoiding a bloody war. The game remains central to Choctaw culture today.
source: Wikipedia - Choctaw mythology; Choctaw Nation

title: The Hunter of the Sun (Choctaw)
summary: None of the Choctaw prophets or chief leaders could answer what happened to the Sun when it disappeared each night. A young man embarked on a mission to discover this mystery. He left his family, community, and country to dedicate his life to tracking the Sun's path. Many years passed — the young men became old, and the old men died — but the people continued to speak of him. Finally, he returned as an old man with the answer he had sought. He had journeyed to the great ocean and discovered that the sun sets and rises from the water. After sharing this profound knowledge with his people, the old man died, his life's purpose fulfilled. The story represents the Choctaw value of dedication to knowledge and truth.
source: Wikipedia - Choctaw mythology; Mould, Choctaw Tales

title: Origin of Poison (Choctaw)
summary: Long ago, a poison vine grew in a pool where Choctaw people bathed. Everything that touched the vine would die. The vine loved the Choctaw people and did not want to hurt them, but it could not warn them when its poison would infect the water. It decided to rid itself of the poison and called the chiefs of the snakes, bees, and wasps, who had all been trampled by humans. The chiefs agreed to share the vine's poison among themselves as a defense. The bees took a small amount and promised they would die after stinging, showing they were not human enemies. The wasps took their portion and promised to buzz a warning before attacking. The snakes took the rest and promised to rattle before striking. From then on, only fools ignored these warnings.
source: Wikipedia - Choctaw mythology; Lankford, Native American Legends

title: The Tashka and Walo: Brothers Who Followed the Sun (Choctaw)
summary: Two brothers named Tashka and Walo followed the sun every day from childhood to adulthood. One day, the sun rested over a great expanse of water, and the boys swam into it, emerging in the home of the sun. The sun's wife, the moon, and the stars all lived there. The sun asked why they had followed him for so many years, and they replied they only wished to see where he died each evening. The sun agreed to send them home but warned them not to speak for four days after returning, or they would surely die. A giant buzzard flew them home. Their frightened mother made them speak, and they told of their journey, knowing they would now die. After telling all they knew, they died and entered the spirit world.
source: Wikipedia - Choctaw mythology; Mould, Choctaw Tales

title: Hoklonote: The Shape-Shifting Spirit (Choctaw)
summary: Hoklonote is a malevolent Choctaw spirit who can assume any shape it desires. This bad spirit is believed to be able to read people's thoughts, making it nearly impossible to hide from. Hoklonote takes different forms to deceive and harm humans — appearing as a loved one, an animal, or even an inanimate object. The spirit's ability to change shape and read minds made it one of the most feared beings in Choctaw mythology. Shamans performed protective rituals to ward off Hoklonote, and people were taught to be suspicious of unexpected occurrences or unusual animal behavior. The story teaches that evil can take many disguises and that spiritual awareness is necessary for protection.
source: Wikipedia - Choctaw mythology

title: Kwanokasha: The Spirit Who Grants Powers (Choctaw)
summary: Kwanokasha is a powerful Choctaw spirit associated with caves and rock shelters, who appears to young children during vision quests. According to legend, Kwanokasha takes children who wander into certain caves into the spirit world, where they are tested. The spirit offers them three choices: a knife (representing the power to heal through surgery), herbs (representing the power to heal through medicine), or a pebble (representing the power to heal through prayer and ritual). The child's choice determines what kind of medicine person they will become. Those who choose nothing may be cursed with sickness or bad luck. The Kwanokasha legend underscores the Choctaw belief in the sacred nature of caves as gateways to the spirit world and the importance of choosing one's path wisely.
source: native-languages.org Choctaw Legends; Choctaw oral tradition

title: Muscogee Creation: Emergence from the Lower World (Creek)
summary: In Muscogee Creek belief, the cosmos is divided into three worlds: the Upper World (home of Hesaketvmesē, the Maker of Breath, along with the Sun, Moon, and Thunder), the Middle World (where humans live), and the Lower World (inhabited by the Tie Snake and the Horned Serpent). Human beings first emerged from beneath the earth in the West and journeyed eastward. The Creator, Hesaketvmesē, also called Epohfvnkv ('the One Above'), spoke to and guided the Muscogee on their journey. The Middle World exists as the balance between the order of the Upper World and the chaos of the Lower World. This tripartite cosmology emphasized balance and harmony, with humans playing a crucial role in maintaining equilibrium between the opposing forces.
source: Wikipedia - Muscogee mythology; Grantham, Creation Myths and Legends of the Creek Indians

title: The Tie Snake and the Horned Serpent (Creek)
summary: The Tie Snake and the Horned Serpent are powerful underwater serpents in Muscogee Creek mythology. The Tie Snake has immense strength and can drag humans underwater to drown. The Horned Serpent is even larger, with antlers or horns, and represents the chaotic forces of the Lower World. According to legend, these creatures were once humans who ate prohibited food, causing them to transform into snakes overnight. A hunter who broke a taboo could become a Tie Snake, signaling the importance of respecting the cosmic balance between the three worlds. These serpents serve as guardians of the boundary between the Middle World and the Lower World. Their stories warn against violating sacred prohibitions and emphasize the consequences of disrupting the natural order.
source: Wikipedia - Muscogee mythology; native-languages.org Tie Snakes

title: Corn Woman (Creek)
summary: Corn Woman is a central figure in Muscogee Creek mythology. Her origin story varies across traditions: in one version, corn fell from a woman's feet after she rubbed them together; in another, corn poured from her thigh after she scratched it. Many legends end with Corn Woman sacrificing herself to allow corn crops to grow, symbolizing how life comes from death. Corn Woman is closely associated with female fertility and the agricultural cycle. The Green Corn Ceremony, the most important Muscogee ritual, honors her gift and marks the beginning of the new year. The story emphasizes the sacred nature of agriculture and the interconnectedness of women's creative power and the growth of corn, the tribe's staple food.
source: Wikipedia - Muscogee mythology; Grantham, Creation Myths of the Creek Indians

title: Master of Breath (Hesaketvmesē) (Creek)
summary: Hesaketvmesē, also called Epohfvnkv, is the supreme being in Muscogee Creek religion. Known as the 'Maker of Breath' or 'the One Above who holds life's breath,' Hesaketvmesē is a solar deity who spoke to and guided the Muscogee people on their journey from the west. The name emphasizes the belief that breath and life are one — Hesaketvmesē gives breath to all living things and can take it away. The Muscogee associated the Creator with the sun, which was central to their ceremonial life. Fire was considered the most striking representation of the sun on earth and was believed to have intelligence and to be in constant communication with Hesaketvmesē. The sacred fire in the square ground was maintained as a direct connection to the Creator.
source: Wikipedia - Muscogee mythology; Martin, Sacred Revolt: The Muskogees' Struggle

title: The Creek Flood Myth and the Dog's Sacrifice (Creek)
summary: The Muscogee Creek tell a flood story in which the Great Flood covered the entire earth. As the waters rose, the people prayed to the Creator for deliverance. A great dog appeared and offered to sacrifice himself by lying across a creek to dam the water. The dog's noble self-sacrifice allowed the people to cross to higher ground. In gratitude and mourning, the Creator set the dog's spirit in the sky, where it became a constellation. To this day, the Creek honor dogs for their loyalty and self-sacrifice. The myth shares elements with flood stories from many other Native American tribes but is distinctive in its focus on the selfless act of an animal rather than a human or divine hero, reflecting the Creek belief in the spiritual nobility of animals.
source: native-languages.org Creek Flood Myth

title: Kolowa: The Hairy Ogre (Creek)
summary: Kolowa is a fearsome, hairy, man-eating ogre from Muscogee Creek mythology. Described as being covered in thick hair and possessing enormous strength, Kolowa lives in the deep forest and preys on humans who stray too far from their villages. Some modern Creek storytellers translate the name as 'gorilla.' Kolowa is particularly dangerous because it can imitate human voices to lure victims into the woods. Parents told stories of Kolowa to keep children from wandering off alone. The creature is one of several 'Man-Eater' figures in Creek mythology, along with Isti Papa, the Great Lion. Warriors and hunters who confronted Kolowa had to rely on their courage, cleverness, and spiritual protection to survive the encounter.
source: native-languages.org Creek Legends

title: Lodge-Boy and Thrown-Away (Creek)
summary: Lodge-Boy (or By-The-Door) and Thrown-Away are mythical twin heroes in Muscogee Creek (and Natchez) tradition. The twins were born after their pregnant mother was killed by a monster. One twin was found inside the lodge and the other was thrown away, giving them their names. They grew up with extraordinary strength and magical abilities. The twins are portrayed as rowdy monster-slayers who cause plenty of trouble during their adventures. They travel across the land defeating dangerous creatures, including giant serpents, man-eating monsters, and evil spirits. Each twin possesses complementary skills — one is physically powerful while the other is clever and resourceful. Their adventures teach the value of brotherhood, courage, and balance between strength and wisdom.
source: native-languages.org Creek Legends; Sacred Texts

title: How Rabbit Brought Fire to the People (Creek)
summary: In this Muscogee Creek legend, the animals possessed fire but kept it hidden from humans. Rabbit, the trickster figure Chufi, decided to steal fire for the people. Rabbit crept to where the animals were guarding the fire and tricked them. He challenged them to a game and, while they were distracted, grabbed a burning ember. The animals chased Rabbit, but he was too quick and clever, passing the fire to the humans. This is why Rabbit is honored by the Creek people. The story differs from the Cherokee fire-bringing myth (which features Water Spider) but shares the theme of a small, clever creature overcoming larger, more powerful beings to bring the gift of fire to humanity. Rabbit's trickster nature is celebrated as ultimately benefitting the people.
source: native-languages.org Creek Legends

title: Seminole Creation: Breathmaker and the Earth (Seminole)
summary: The Seminole creation story begins with Breathmaker, the great Creator god, who existed before anything else. Breathmaker created the earth and all living things. In the Seminole version of the Muskogean creation tradition, the earth was formed from mud brought up from the primordial waters, similar to the Cherokee story. Breathmaker then created humans, animals, and plants, and taught the Seminole how to live properly. He gave them the Green Corn Ceremony and established the clans. Breathmaker also created the sun, moon, and stars to govern time and seasons. The Seminole believe that Breathmaker remains involved in the world and can be communicated with through prayer and ceremony. The creation story establishes the sacred relationship between the Seminole people and their land in Florida.
source: native-languages.org Seminole Legends; indigenouspeople.net

title: Stikini: The Owl Witches (Seminole)
summary: Stikini are dreaded owl-witches from Seminole folklore. During the day, Stikini appear as ordinary humans, but at night they transform into monstrous owls with supernatural powers. They are known to prey on humans, particularly those who are alone in the forest at night. A Stikini can be identified by its bloodshot eyes and unusual behavior. The owl-witches are associated with witchcraft and dark magic, and their appearance is considered an omen of death or misfortune. Seminole medicine men have the power to identify Stikini and perform rituals to protect against them. The legend serves as a warning against practicing evil magic and emphasizes the importance of staying within the protection of the community, especially at night.
source: native-languages.org Seminole Legends

title: Long-Ears: The Wolf Monster (Seminole)
summary: Long-Ears is a wolf-like monster from Seminole folklore. This enormous, predatory creature is distinguished by its unusually long ears and its insatiable hunger for human flesh. Long-Ears dwells in the remote swamps and hammocks of Florida, emerging to hunt when food is scarce. The monster is said to be incredibly fast and cunning, able to track prey over long distances. Seminole hunters told stories of encounters with Long-Ears to young warriors as a test of courage. The creature also appears in stories as a cautionary figure, warning against venturing too deep into unknown territory unprepared. Like many Seminole monsters, Long-Ears represents the dangers of the wild Florida landscape that the Seminole learned to navigate and respect.
source: native-languages.org Seminole Legends

title: The Milky Way (Seminole/Hitchiti)
summary: This Seminole and Hitchiti story explains the origin of the Milky Way. Long ago, a father was angry with his son and banished him from the village. The son, heartbroken, wandered the earth. A great dog took pity on the boy and decided to help him escape his sorrow. The dog ran through the sky, scattering sparkling dust from its fur across the heavens, creating a luminous path. This path — the Milky Way — would forever remind people of the dog's compassion. Another version tells that the Milky Way is the path of souls traveling to the spirit world. The story reflects the Seminole reverence for animals and the belief that the stars and sky are filled with the stories of compassionate beings who helped humanity.
source: native-languages.org Seminole Legends; indigenouspeople.net

title: Origin of the Seminole Clans (Seminole)
summary: According to Seminole tradition, the clans were established at the time of creation. The Seminole have several matrilineal clans, including the Panther, Bear, Deer, Bird, Wind, and Otter clans. The legend tells that Breathmaker created the animals and asked them to protect the human people. Each animal agreed, and in return, the people would carry the animal's name as a clan. Clan membership determines marriage rules — a person must marry outside their clan — and establishes social obligations and relationships. The clans also have specific roles in ceremonies, including the Green Corn Dance. Clan identity is passed through the mother's line, and children belong to their mother's clan. The clan system remains a fundamental organizing principle of Seminole society today.
source: Seminole Tribe of Florida; native-languages.org

title: Little Giver: The Corn Spirit (Seminole)
summary: Little Giver is the Seminole corn spirit who brought agriculture to the people. According to tradition, Little Giver appeared as a small, generous being who taught the Seminole how to plant, cultivate, and harvest corn. The spirit showed them the proper way to prepare the fields, the right time to plant, and the rituals to ensure a bountiful harvest. Little Giver is closely associated with the Green Corn Ceremony, the most important Seminole ritual, which marks the ripening of the corn and the beginning of the new year. Offerings are made to Little Giver during the ceremony to ensure continued blessings. The spirit embodies the principle of generosity — the name 'Little Giver' reminds the Seminole that even small gifts given freely are precious.
source: native-languages.org Seminole Legends

title: Chickasaw Creation and the Sacred Pole (Chickasaw)
summary: The Chickasaw creation story tells of their emergence as a people guided by a sacred pole. According to tradition, the Chickasaw and Choctaw were originally one people who migrated from the west. They carried a sacred pole (sometimes described as a dogwood pole) which they planted each night. Each morning, they would travel in the direction the pole leaned. After a long journey, the pole stood perfectly upright at the site of Nanih Waiya mound in Mississippi. There they divided: the Chickasaw, led by Chicksah, traveled north, while the Choctaw, led by Chata, remained. The Chickasaw settled in northern Mississippi, western Tennessee, and northwestern Alabama. Before arriving at Nanih Waiya, they had emerged into this world from a hole in the ground at a place where the sun rises.
source: Wikipedia - Chickasaw; native-languages.org Chickasaw Legends

title: Ababinili and the Creation of Humans (Chickasaw)
summary: Ababinili (also spelled Aba' Binni'li'), meaning 'One Who Sits Above' or 'Dwells Above,' is the Creator god of the Chickasaw people. According to legend, Ababinili created the first humans from the clay of the earth and breathed life into them. He taught the people how to live, hunt, farm, and conduct ceremonies. Ababinili established the sacred fire as a symbol of his presence and instructed the people to keep it burning always. He created the sun, moon, and stars to give light and mark the passage of time. Ababinili also appointed the thunder and lightning to bring rain. The Chickasaw believe that Ababinili watches over all creation and that humans must live in harmony with nature to maintain his favor.
source: native-languages.org Chickasaw Legends; firstpeople.us

title: The Big White Dog and the Sacred Pole (Chickasaw)
summary: In this Chickasaw origin story, a great white dog led the people on their migration and guided them with the sacred pole. The white dog was sent by Ababinili to protect the Chickasaw people during their long journey from the west. Each day, the dog would lead the way, and at night, the sacred pole was planted in the ground. The direction the pole leaned would indicate the path for the next day's travel. After many years, the pole finally stood upright at the site where the Chickasaw would settle. The great white dog then disappeared, its mission complete. The white dog is honored in Chickasaw tradition as a symbol of faithfulness, protection, and divine guidance. The story emphasizes the Chickasaw belief that animals are spiritual beings sent to assist humans.
source: firstpeople.us; Chickasaw Nation

title: Sint-Holo: The Horned Serpent (Chickasaw)
summary: Sint-Holo is a dragon-like horned serpent from Chickasaw mythology. This powerful water monster dwells in deep rivers, lakes, and underground springs. Sint-Holo has a serpentine body covered with scales, a horned or antlered head, and possesses supernatural powers. Unlike some malevolent serpents in neighboring tribes' mythology, Sint-Holo is sometimes ambivalent or even protective of the Chickasaw people. The serpent is associated with the underground water realm and represents the forces of nature that must be respected. Those who encounter Sint-Holo must show proper respect or face dire consequences. The horned serpent symbol appears in Chickasaw art and ceremonial objects and is one of the most widespread mythological figures across Southeastern Native American cultures.
source: native-languages.org Chickasaw Legends

title: Ghost of the White Deer (Chickasaw)
summary: The Ghost of the White Deer is a Chickasaw legend explaining why the white deer is considered sacred. A young hunter once encountered a pure white deer in the forest. The deer spoke to him, revealing itself as a spirit sent by Ababinili to test the hunter's character. The hunter chose not to kill the deer, showing mercy and respect for the spirit world. Because of his compassion, the hunter was blessed with success in future hunts and prosperity for his family. From that day, the Chickasaw have regarded the white deer as a sacred being that should never be hunted. Killing a white deer brings bad luck and spiritual contamination. The story teaches that mercy and respect for all living things bring blessings, while greed and disrespect have spiritual consequences.
source: native-languages.org Chickasaw Legends

title: Chickasaw Flood Myth (Chickasaw)
summary: The Chickasaw flood myth tells of a time when the earth was covered by a great deluge. As the waters rose, the people climbed to the highest ground, but the water continued to rise. Ababinili, the Creator, instructed a man and a woman to build a raft and take on board pairs of all the animals. The flood covered even the tallest mountains for many days. When the waters finally receded, the man and woman found themselves at Nanih Waiya, the sacred mound. From them, the Chickasaw people were reborn. The flood myth shares elements with the Choctaw flood legend, reflecting their shared origins. The story emphasizes renewal and divine mercy — the Creator preserved a remnant of life to begin anew.
source: Chickasaw Nation; native-languages.org

title: The Rabbit Trickster (Chickasaw)
summary: Chukfi (or Chokfi), the Rabbit, is the trickster figure in Chickasaw folklore. Rabbit is clever, mischievous, and always getting into trouble, but he also uses his wits to benefit the people. In one story, Rabbit outwits the wolves by pretending to be dead and then escaping when they are not looking. In another, he tricks the larger animals into letting him share their food. Rabbit's adventures explain many natural phenomena, such as why Rabbit has a short tail, why the possum's tail is bare, and why the deer has antlers. Though Rabbit is often selfish and deceitful, his cleverness is admired, and his stories teach important lessons about the consequences of pride, greed, and dishonesty. Listeners are encouraged to laugh at Rabbit's antics while learning from his mistakes.
source: native-languages.org Chickasaw Legends

title: Lofa: The Stinking Ogre (Chickasaw)
summary: Lofa is a hideous, foul-smelling ogre from Chickasaw mythology, similar to the Choctaw Shampe. Lofa is a bigfoot-like creature that dwells in the remote swamps and forests of Chickasaw territory. It is covered in shaggy hair and emits an unbearable stench that can be smelled from a great distance. The creature's odor is so powerful that it can knock a person unconscious. Lofa preys on travelers who become lost in its territory. However, the creature is slow and clumsy, and an alert person can escape by running against the wind. Lofa stories served as warnings to children and hunters about the dangers of the wilderness and the importance of staying oriented and traveling in groups.
source: native-languages.org Chickasaw Legends

title: The Legend of the Squirrel Stick (Chickasaw)
summary: This Chickasaw legend tells of a young man who wished to marry a chief's daughter. The chief set an impossible task: the young man must catch a squirrel alive using only a specially carved stick — not a bow, trap, or snare. The young man prayed to Ababinili for guidance and spent days practicing with his stick in the forest. His persistence and faith were rewarded when a squirrel came to him without fear, and he caught it gently. The chief, impressed by the young man's patience, skill, and spiritual devotion, allowed the marriage. The squirrel stick became a symbol of the proper way to achieve one's goals: through preparation, faith, and respect for nature rather than through force or deceit.
source: native-languages.org Chickasaw Legends; Chickasaw Nation

title: Natchez Creation: The People of the Sun (Natchez)
summary: The Natchez creation tradition centers on the Great Sun, the divine ruler who descended from the sun itself. According to legend, the first Great Sun was born of a woman who was impregnated by a ray of sunlight. This established the Sun lineage, which was matrilineal — the Great Sun was always the son of the Female Sun, and her daughter would be the mother of the next Great Sun. The Natchez believed they were the Children of the Sun, and their paramount chief was considered a living deity. The Natchez also have a flood myth in which the earth was covered by water, and a dog's sacrifice helped save the people. The sun, as the source of life and the symbol of divine authority, was central to all Natchez religion and governance.
source: Wikipedia - Natchez people; native-languages.org Natchez Legends

title: The Natchez Sacred Fire (Natchez)
summary: The sacred fire was the heart of Natchez religion and governance. A perpetual fire was kept burning in the temple atop the main platform mound at the Grand Village of the Natchez. The fire was guarded by temple priests and was never allowed to go out. Only the Great Sun and his family could enter the inner sanctum where the fire burned. The fire represented the sun on earth and the continuing presence of the divine among the Natchez people. When the temple fire was ritually extinguished and rekindled each year, it marked the most important ceremonial occasion. The story of 'Polluting the Fire' warns of a time when someone defiled the sacred flame by bringing unclean objects near it, angering the spirits and causing misfortune to fall upon the people until the fire was ritually purified.
source: native-languages.org Natchez Legends; Arkansas Archeology

title: Corn Woman and the Origin of Corn (Natchez)
summary: The Natchez legend of Corn Woman (also known as Corn-Woman's Son) tells how agriculture came to the people. According to the story, Corn Woman gave birth to a son and from her body came the first corn. In one version, corn poured from her thigh when she scratched it. In another, she sacrificed herself so that corn would grow from her burial place. Her son became the first Great Sun, the divine ruler of the Natchez. The story establishes the connection between female fertility, sacrifice, and agricultural abundance. Corn was the staple crop of the Natchez, and its cultivation was surrounded by ritual and ceremony. The legend teaches that food is a gift that comes through sacrifice and that the people must honor that gift through proper ceremonies.
source: native-languages.org Natchez Legends; Sacred Texts - Corn Woman

title: Lodge-Boy and Thrown-Away (Natchez)
summary: Lodge-Boy and Thrown-Away are twin culture heroes in Natchez mythology. The twins were born when their pregnant mother was killed by a monster — one was found inside the lodge (Lodge-Boy) and the other was found where he had been thrown away (Thrown-Away). They grew up in a single day, blessed with supernatural strength and abilities. The twins embarked on a series of adventures: they killed the monster that had murdered their mother, battled giant serpents, defeated man-eating ogres, and cleared the land of dangerous beings to make it safe for the Natchez people. They also brought important knowledge to the people, including hunting techniques and ceremonial practices. The twins are complementary — one uses brute strength while the other uses cunning — representing the balance needed to overcome life's challenges.
source: native-languages.org Natchez Legends; Sacred Texts

title: Olobit (Ukteni): The Great Serpent (Natchez)
summary: Olobit (also called Olobis or Intsiyacdoo'cgu, 'sharp-breasted snake') is a monstrous serpent from Natchez folklore. Similar to the Cherokee Uktena, Olobit is a giant serpent with supernatural powers and a deadly gaze. It also has a sharp, breast-like protrusion on its chest that can impale victims. Olobit dwells in deep water or remote forests and is extremely dangerous to encounter. The serpent is associated with the underworld and the chaotic forces that the Natchez believed existed beneath the ordered world of humans. Shamans performed rituals to protect against Olobit's influence. The Olobit legend reflects the widespread Southeastern belief in horned or crested water serpents that guard the boundary between the human world and the spirit realm.
source: native-languages.org Natchez Legends; Sacred Texts

title: Adventure With a Tie-Snake (Natchez)
summary: The Tie-Snake (u'lax daxgi'ilu in Natchez) is a water spirit common to Southeastern Native American folklore. Though the size and shape of an ordinary snake, Tie-Snakes possess immense strength and can coil around a human with crushing force. In the Natchez story, a man encountered a Tie-Snake while fishing. The snake rose from the water and tried to drag him under. The man fought bravely, remembering the teachings of his elders, and managed to cut the snake in half with his knife. The two halves of the Tie-Snake transformed into eels and slithered away into the water. The story teaches that knowledge and courage can overcome even supernatural threats, and it explains the origin of eels. Tie-Snake legends are found across the Southeast, from the Cherokee to the Creek to the Natchez.
source: native-languages.org Natchez Legends; Sacred Texts

title: The Natchez Great Sun and Tattooed Serpent (Natchez)
summary: The Natchez were ruled by two paramount chiefs: the Great Sun, who had supreme authority over civil and religious affairs, and the Tattooed Serpent, who oversaw matters of war, peace, and diplomacy. Both were hereditary positions within the Sun lineage, the royal clan. The Great Sun was considered a living deity, a direct descendant of the sun itself, and was carried on a litter so that his feet never touched the ground. When the Tattooed Serpent died in 1725, the funeral was a spectacular and solemn event. Two of his wives, one of his sisters, his first warrior, his doctor, his head servant and the servant's wife, his nurse, and a craftsman of war clubs all chose to die with him through ritual suicide, believing they would accompany him to the spirit world. The practice reflected the absolute authority of the Sun dynasty and the Natchez belief in an afterlife where rank and status persisted.
source: Wikipedia - Natchez people; DuVal, Native Ground

title: The Tlanuwa Bird (Natchez)
summary: The Tlanuwa is a giant mythological bird of prey known to both the Natchez and Cherokee. This enormous raptor has feathers made of impenetrable metal that it can launch like arrows at enemies. The Tlanuwa was large enough to carry away humans and even deer. It nested in the highest cliffs and mountains. The Natchez tell stories of the Tlanuwa battling the great water serpents such as the Olobit and the Tie-Snake, representing the conflict between the sky and the underworld. Heroes in Natchez legends sometimes sought the feathers of the Tlanuwa for their protective properties. The bird was feared but also respected as a powerful spirit being. The name Tlanuwa is Cherokee — the original Natchez name for this creature was never recorded, though the stories survived.
source: native-languages.org Natchez Legends

title: Raven Steals the Sun, Moon, and Stars (Haida)
summary: In the time before light, Raven (Nang Kilslas) was tired of living in darkness. He discovered an old chief who kept all the light of the universe hidden in a nested set of five boxes. Raven transformed himself into a hemlock needle and dropped into the river where the chief's daughter came to drink. She swallowed the needle and became pregnant, giving birth to Raven-child. The old chief adored his grandchild. Raven cried incessantly until the chief allowed him to open the boxes one by one. First the stars escaped, then the moon, then the northern lights. Finally Raven grabbed the sun in his beak and flew through the smoke hole, releasing light into the world forever. This core Haida myth explains the origin of all celestial light and establishes Raven as both a trickster and culture hero who brought essential gifts to humanity despite his selfish motives.
source: Haida oral tradition; John Reed Swanton, Haida Texts and Myths; The Earth Stories Collection

title: The Creation of Haida Gwaii
summary: Before the days of the grandfathers, there was nothing but water except a single reef where the supernatural beings lived crowded together. Raven flew about looking for a place to land but could alight nowhere. He flew up to the Sky Land, where he tricked a chief's family by taking the form of a baby. He caused mischief until a stone woman exposed him. The chief sang a magic song and Raven was dropped, falling onto the great waters in his cradle. A grebe appeared and summoned him to the underwater house of Sea-Gull Man. There, Raven was given two rocks: a small speckled one that became Haida Gwaii and a larger black one that became the mainland continent. Raven was instructed to spiral down from the sky, carrying the rocks, and when he reached the islands he made humans from the earth. This creation narrative establishes the sacred origin of Haida Gwaii and the Haida people's deep connection to the land.
source: Haida oral tradition narrated by GwaaGanad (Diane Brown) and James Young; Wikisource Myths and Legends of British North America

title: Raven and the Moon Woman (Haida)
summary: Raven became the son of Moon Woman and cried incessantly, saying 'Boo-hoo, moon!' His mother, exhausted by his noise, stopped up all the holes in the house and untied the strings of a box containing the moon, letting Raven play with it. When she left, Raven turned into a raven and flew about the house with the moon in his beak. He then cried for the smoke hole to be opened, and when his mother obliged, he flew through it with the moon under his wing. He perched on a house top and called like a raven. Raven flew to the Nass River where fishermen were taking olachen in total darkness. He bargained with them, offering light in exchange for their spruce needles. When they mocked him, he let them see a sliver of the moon, and they gave him many olachen. He then flew to a high mountain, broke the moon in half, threw one half into the sky for the night and the other for the day, and scattered fragments as stars.
source: Haida oral tradition; Wikisource Myths and Legends of British North America

title: Foam Woman and Ice Woman (Haida)
summary: Among the SGaana Kiidads (Supernatural Beings) of Haida tradition are Kalga Jaad (Ice Woman) and GaayhllGa Jaad (Flood-Tide-Woman). These female supernatural beings are part of the Raven Traveling narratives, also known as Xuuya Kaagang.ngas, which tell of Raven traveling the earth and transforming it to make it suitable for humans. Kalga Jaad controls ice and winter forces, while GaayhllGa Jaad governs the tides and the boundary between land and sea. These beings embody the Haida understanding of the interconnected relationship between land and sea. They are among the many supernatural entities that Raven encountered during his travels across Haida Gwaii, and their stories teach respect for natural forces and the balance required for human survival on the islands.
source: Terri-Lynn Williams-Davidson, Magical Beings of Haida Gwaii; Haida oral tradition

title: Cedar Woman (Ts'uu Jaas) and the Cedar Tree (Haida)
summary: In Haida worldview, the Cedar Tree is considered an Ancient Sister called Ts'uu Jaasas. She is a SGaana Kiidads (Supernatural Being) and a physical manifestation of the Haida people's interconnected relationship with the land and sea. The cedar tree is regarded as every woman's older sister, known as Ts'uu K'waayGa. This supernatural being taught the Haida people how to use cedar for clothing, shelter, canoes, and baskets. The cedar tree's importance in Haida culture cannot be overstated: it provided the material for the great houses, the seagoing canoes, the bentwood boxes, and the ceremonial regalia. The Haida legal framework, called the Ts'uu JaasGalang HlGaajuu (Older Cedar Sister) Framework, derives from the teachings of this supernatural being, emphasizing the interconnectedness of all life and the responsibility humans have to care for the natural world.
source: Haida oral tradition and legal scholarship; Terri-Lynn Williams-Davidson

title: The Bear Mother (Haida)
summary: In this classic Haida narrative, a woman who mocked the bears was taken by a bear who made her his wife. She was led into the bear's den deep in the forest, where she lived among the bear people and bore children who were half-human, half-bear. Her human brothers eventually tracked her down and killed the bears in a great hunt, but the woman herself had been transformed by her time with the bears. This story, common across many Northwest Coast tribes including the Tlingit version known as Kaats' and the Bear, serves to explain the special relationship between humans and bears. The bear woman's children became powerful ancestors, and her story validates the bear as a crest figure for certain clans. It also teaches respect for all animal people and warns against the dangers of disrespecting the natural world.
source: Haida oral tradition; Robert Bringhurst, A Story as Sharp as a Knife

title: The Sea Wolf (Wasgo) (Haida)
summary: Wasgo the Sea Wolf is a powerful supernatural being in Haida mythology, a creature that combines the features of a wolf and a whale. He possesses the strength of both land and sea animals and serves as an important crest figure for Haida clans. In the narratives, Wasgo descends from a supernatural lineage and mediates between the ocean and forest worlds. He appears in Haida art as a composite creature with a wolf's head and body but with the fins and tail of a killer whale, often depicted with exaggerated features indicating supernatural power. Wasgo stories teach about the fluid boundaries between animal species in the Haida worldview and the power that comes from understanding both the land and sea. The sea wolf is considered one of the most potent supernatural helpers a person could receive.
source: Haida oral tradition; Haida art and crest narratives

title: Raven Steals the Light (Tlingit - Yeil)
summary: In the Tlingit version of this foundational myth, Yeil (Raven) was born as the son of Kit-ka'ositiyi-qâ and was instructed and trained in every way to make a world. At that time there was no light in the world, but Raven knew that far up the Nass River was a large house where a chief kept light just for himself. Raven turned himself into a pine needle and dropped into the drinking cup of the chief's daughter. She swallowed it and gave birth to Raven as a human baby. The grandfather loved the child and eventually gave him the bundles containing the stars. Raven scattered them across the sky. He cried for the moon and pushed it through the smoke hole. Finally he cried for the box of daylight, turned into his true raven form, and flew out with the daylight under his wing. The Tlingit version emphasizes Raven's dual nature as creator and trickster, and explains not only the origin of light but also the creation of human beings from leaves after the great flood.
source: John R. Swanton, Tlingit Myths and Texts (1909); Tlingit oral tradition

title: Raven and the Fog Woman (Tlingit)
summary: Raven wanted to marry and went to the chief called Fog-Over-The-Salmon, who had a young daughter. The chief agreed but made Raven promise to treat her with respect. They married and lived contentedly through summer and fall, but winter brought famine. Raven's wife began making a basket, and despite Raven's testy comments, she completed it. The next morning she washed her hands in the basket and salmon appeared, the first salmon ever created. Every day she did this and their house was soon full of drying salmon. But Raven forgot his good fortune and quarreled with his wife daily. In a rage, he hit her with a piece of dried salmon. She fled toward the water, and when Raven tried to catch her, his hands passed through her body as if through mist. All the salmon followed her into the sea. She disappeared into the fog, for she was Fog Woman herself. Raven went to his father-in-law to beg for her return, but Chief Fog-Over-The-Salmon refused, saying Raven had broken his promise.
source: John R. Swanton, Tlingit Myths and Texts (1909); Tlingit oral tradition adapted by University of Alaska Fairbanks

title: The Land Otter Son (Kushtaka) (Tlingit)
summary: After their son was lost at sea, a grieving Tlingit couple living in poverty heard whistling outside their home at night. The wife called out, begging her son to come in. Without being seen entering, a figure appeared seated opposite them with his hands over his face. He only whistled by drawing in his breath. Toward midnight, the kushtaka (land-otter-man) revealed himself and brought them devilfish. The next morning he rowed his father to the halibut grounds with supernatural speed, catching fish effortlessly. He brought in a seal for them. The family decided to move to Sitka, and the land-otter son paddled with them, but as they rounded a point, his mother saw only his shadow — his body had disappeared. The kushtaka had returned to the forest, his true home. This story illustrates the Tlingit belief that those lost at sea become kushtaka, half-human, half-otter beings who can return to help their families but can never fully rejoin human society.
source: Smithsonian Institution Bureau of American Ethnology, Tlingit Myths and Texts (1908)

title: Raven Pulls the Salmon (Tlingit)
summary: After Raven brought light to the world and created rivers and lakes, there were still no salmon in the coastal waters. The salmon, herrings, and ooligans were kept in a magical house in the middle of the Pacific Ocean, where they spawned without ever coming to shore. Only rich chiefs could paddle out to catch them, leaving the poor hungry. Raven heard of Xanaax.atwaayaa, a mythical being who possessed an octopus-tentacle staff with supernatural powers. Raven tricked the being into trading the staff by demonstrating a magical bow and arrow (actually a magpie) that seemed to kill a mountain goat from impossible distances. With the staff, Raven went to the entrance of the Alsek River and latched it to the salmon house. He struggled mightily, and his nephew Crow urged him to sing a song about Xanaax.atwaayaa. When Raven sang, he easily pulled all the salmon into the bays and rivers. The tracks of Raven's struggle can still be seen at the Alsek River entrance.
source: Tlingit legend as told by Henry Davis, Sheldon Jackson College; University of Alaska Fairbanks curriculum

title: Raven and the Beaver (Tlingit)
summary: Near a bay near Kôts!ê'L! there was a seawater pond where a beaver lived. Raven very much wanted to catch and kill this beaver, so he dug two trenches to drain the lake at low tide. After the water ran out and the beaver was visible at the bottom, Raven lowered a hook and pulled the beaver up. After killing the beaver, Raven also killed a big whale and got his people to tow it to the place where the beaver had lived. He commanded four large canoes full of people to tow the whale up the rapids through one of the canals he had made. After they labored for many days and grew tired, Raven told them to take it easy. Finally, even Raven grew tired and said, 'Turn into stone.' All did so, and to this day you can see a large island shaped like a whale with a string of four smaller islands extending from it. This Tlingit etiological myth explains a distinctive geological formation near Sitka.
source: John R. Swanton, Tlingit Myths and Texts (1909)

title: Raven Turns Himself into a Woman (Tlingit)
summary: Having lost his wife Fog Woman, Raven decided to marry again so people would look after him. Rather than finding a good wife, he turned himself into a woman and married a man from the Killerwhale people. As a wife, Raven was lazy and deceitful — she stole from the Killerwhale people. Eventually she decided to kill her husband and all the Killerwhales in the village. After this destruction, the story continues with Raven visiting Fish Hawk, promising to be a good partner and share work and food. But Raven never did any work; he ate and ate while Fish Hawk provided everything. Whenever Fish Hawk grew angry, Raven flattered him, praising his wealth and generosity. Finally Fish Hawk refused to feed Raven any longer and left him to find his own food. This comic story showcases Raven's laziness, deceit, and clever manipulation, using flattery to exploit others, while also serving as a moral lesson about the consequences of trusting flatterers.
source: John R. Swanton, Tlingit Myths and Texts (1909); adapted by University of Alaska Fairbanks

title: Kaats' and the Bear Woman (Tlingit)
summary: The Tlingit hunter Kaats' was hunting in the mountains near Glacier Bay when he encountered a bear who removed her skin and became a beautiful woman. He married her, and they had children who were half-human, half-bear. His bear wife provided for him and taught him about the forest, but Kaats' eventually grew homesick for his human village. When he tried to leave with their children, the bear wife pursued him. She called upon her bear relatives, and a great confrontation ensued. Kaats' escaped with the help of his human relatives, but the bear wife reclaimed her children and returned to the forest. The descendants of these children became the Bear Clan among the Tlingit. This story explains clan origins and the special relationship between the Tlingit Bear Clan and bears, who are considered ancestral relatives rather than mere animals. It also teaches profound lessons about cross-cultural marriage, identity, and belonging.
source: Tlingit oral tradition; Alaska Native Knowledge Network

title: D'Sonoqua (Dzunukwa) and the Children (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: D'Sonoqua, also known as Dzunukwa or Tsonokwa, is the Wild Woman of the Woods, a giant cannibal ogress in Kwakwaka'wakw tradition. She is black in color with messy hair, pendulous breasts, pursed red lips, and carries a huge basket on her back. She wanders the forests calling 'Hu! Hu!' - a sound that children are told is the wind blowing through cedar trees. She captures children who misbehave or wander too far from home, stuffing them into her basket and carrying them to her remote house in the woods to eat them. However, D'Sonoqua is dim-witted with poor eyesight, and children frequently outwit her. In many versions, a clever child tricks her into sitting on a hot fire or into falling into a pit, killing her and taking her treasures. From the ashes of one burned D'Sonoqua came the first mosquitoes. Despite her fearsome reputation, she is also associated with wealth and is venerated as an ancestor of the Namgis clan through her son Tsilwalagame.
source: Kwakwaka'wakw oral tradition; Franz Boas, Kwakiutl Ethnography

title: Thunderbird and Whale (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: The epic struggle between Thunderbird (Tseiqami) and Whale is one of the most important myths of the Pacific Northwest, found across Kwakwaka'wakw and Nuu-chah-nulth traditions. Thunderbird is a massive supernatural bird whose wingbeats cause thunder and whose eyes flash lightning. He hunts whales for food, plunging from the sky to seize them in his talons. In the Kwakwaka'wakw version recorded by Franz Boas, the battle takes place between Thunderbird and the leader of the animals, who retaliates against Thunderbird for carrying away one of his sons. The leader raises an army carried in an artificial whale. In the great battle at the village, Thunderbird's four children (named One-Whale-Carrier among them) are drowned, and Thunderbird himself is killed, survived only by a nine-month-old infant in the cradle. Some versions tell of Thunderbird dropping the whale to create thunder, or of the whale escaping and causing earthquakes and tidal waves. Geologists believe this myth may describe the 1700 Cascadia earthquake and tsunami.
source: Franz Boas, Kwakiutl Tales: New Series (1935); Ella E. Clark, Indian Legends of the Pacific Northwest

title: The Hamatsa Cannibal Spirit (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: Baxbaxwalanuksiwe' (Man-Eater at the North End of the World) is the most important supernatural being in Kwakwaka'wakw winter ceremonies. This cannibal spirit has mouths all over his body and can transform into various man-eating birds. His attendants include the Hamatsa bird-masks: Gwaxwgwakwalanuksiwe' (a man-eating raven), Galuxwadzuwus (Crooked-Beak of Heaven, a bird with a long, crooked beak), and Huxhukw (a supernatural crane-like bird who cracks skulls to suck out brains). The Hamatsa ceremony, the most prestigious of the T'seka (Red Cedar Bark Ceremonies), involves a four-day complex dance where initiates become possessed by Baxbaxwalanuksiwe'. The initiate goes into the woods for months, then returns to the village in a wild cannibalistic frenzy, calling 'Hap! Hap! (Eat! Eat!)'. He is gradually tamed by healers and society members through songs, dances, and rituals, finally emerging as a calm, respected member of Hamatsa society. The ceremony enacts the binding of cannibal forces for human benefit.
source: Kwakwaka'wakw oral tradition; U'mista Cultural Centre; Franz Boas

title: Sisiutl the Double-Headed Serpent (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: The Sisiutl is a fearsome three-headed sea serpent whose glance can turn an adversary into stone. Typically depicted as a double-headed serpent with a central humanoid face representing Baxbaxwalanuksiwe' or Komokwa, it has horned heads, protruding tongues, and spiral noses that indicate supernatural power. The Sisiutl is closely associated with shamans, who mediate between natural and supernatural worlds, and warriors believed that its blood made them invincible in battle. It appears as a protective figure on clan house beams, war canoes, and ceremonial regalia. In some myths, the Sisiutl transforms into an invincible war canoe or a magic belt that protects the wearer against all dangers. The beach mineral mica was believed to be the scales of the Sisiutl and was incorporated into ceremonial traditions. The Sisiutl plays a dual role as both a terrifying monster and a powerful helper to ancestors who earned its favor.
source: Kwakwaka'wakw oral tradition; Franz Boas; Wikipedia

title: Komokwa the Undersea Chief (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: Komokwa (also known as Qomoqua or Copper-Maker) is the powerful chief of the undersea world in Kwakwaka'wakw mythology. He lives in a magnificent copper house beneath the ocean, where he controls all the wealth of the sea, including seals, sea lions, salmon, and the precious copper that gives him his name. Komokwa appears as a figure with a large, bulbous head covered in feathers or hair, often depicted with a wide mouth and protruding tongue. He sometimes takes the form of a sea monster or appears riding a Sisiutl canoe. Humans who visit his underwater kingdom must undergo dangerous trials. In some stories, a person who drowns is taken to Komokwa's house and may return with great wealth and supernatural power. Komokwa is both feared and respected; he is a generous giver of wealth to those who treat him properly but also a dangerous being who can drown anyone who disrespects the sea.
source: Franz Boas, Kwakiutl Ethnography; Kwakwaka'wakw oral tradition

title: The Cannibal Woman Society (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: Long ago when totemic beings walked the earth, there was Dzunukwa (Cannibal Woman), a gigantic female being with great wealth and powerful magic who ate people. The other beings became alarmed and decided only Raven could stop her. Raven flew to the North End of the World and recruited a flock of fearsome Cannibal Birds, becoming their leader. He returned with his flock to the great forest where Dzunukwa lived, and they danced and sang her praises. When the dance ended, Dzunukwa found herself bound by powerful magic and could no longer eat flesh — the only things she could eat were nuggets of native copper. This story establishes the origin of the Hamatsa (Cannibal Woman) Society, the most important secret society in Kwakwaka'wakw culture. The society's winter ceremonies reenact the capturing and taming of the cannibal spirit, transforming its destructive power into constructive social and spiritual energy for the community.
source: Kwakwaka'wakw oral tradition; Charles M. Nelson

title: Bukwus the Wild Man of the Woods (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: Bukwus is the male counterpart of D'Sonoqua, the Wild Man of the Woods in Kwakwaka'wakw mythology. He is a hairy, giant being who lives in the deep forests and is associated with the spirits of those who died by starvation or exposure. Bukwus roams the woods calling out in a hollow voice, trying to lure humans into the forest with promises of food. He is particularly dangerous because he appears as a starving, pitiful figure who begs for help, but anyone who follows him becomes lost forever and joins his ghostly company. Unlike D'Sonoqua, who is more easily outwitted, Bukwus is treacherous and deceptive. However, like D'Sonoqua, he is also associated with great wealth and can bestow supernatural powers upon those who manage to survive an encounter with him. Bukwus appears in both the T'seka (Red Cedar Bark Ceremonies) and the Tła'sala (Peace Dances), where dancers wear masks depicting his gaunt, haunted features.
source: Franz Boas, Kwakiutl Ethnography; Kwakwaka'wakw oral tradition

title: Changer and the Creation of the World (Coast Salish)
summary: In Coast Salish tradition, dukʷibəɬ the Changer (also known as Xe'las or Xelas) walked the earth when the world was not yet as it is today. Sometimes he appeared as a brilliant light from the East, sometimes as a young man, sometimes as several young men, and sometimes as an invisible old person who knew everything. The Changer went everywhere, preparing the world for the First People who were yet to come. He transformed inappropriate things into useful ones: he turned rocks that beat people over the head into hammers, sticks that struck people into fish spears, and dangerous beings into animals, birds, and landscape features. The Changer also sent a flood to cleanse the earth when things went wrong. All living things were destroyed except one woman and one dog, who fled to the top of Tacobud (Mount Rainier). From them, the next race of people was born. The Changer's work established the Coast Salish understanding of the world as a place shaped by a powerful transformer who continues to watch over the land and its people.
source: Coast Salish oral tradition; Hibulb Cultural Center; Franz Boas; Brian Thom, Transformer Stories

title: Crane and Changer (Coast Salish)
summary: As told by Snoqualmie Charlie, this story recounts how dukʷibəɬ the Changer came upon a man with very long legs standing by the shore, getting hit over the head by rocks. The Changer asked what he was doing, and the man explained he was trying to get food but the rocks kept hurting him. The Changer took the rocks, declared they would be hammers for the people, and knocked them together, transforming them into useful tools. Then the long-legged man picked up a stick to use as a fish spear, but it too hit him over the head. The Changer took the stick, broke it in two, and declared they would be fish spears. He gave the transformed items to the man and walked away, continuing his journey of preparing the world. The long-legged man was transformed into the Crane, who to this day stands on long legs at the water's edge, using his beak as a fish spear. This is one of many stories showing how the Changer shaped the world by transforming flawed ancestors into the animals and birds we know today.
source: Hibulb Cultural Center; Snoqualmie Charlie oral tradition

title: Qals (Mink) and the Transformations (Coast Salish)
summary: Qals (Mink) is a major trickster figure in Coast Salish mythology, particularly in the Fraser River region. In the longest and most important story told by George StsE'lis to Franz Boas, Qals shapes the world through his encounters with various flawed ancestors. Mink tricked a chief's daughter into sleeping with him, producing a human boy named T'qulä'tca, who became the ancestor of the Ts'uwä'l people. Qals encountered numerous beings who needed correction: he transformed a toothed-vagina woman named LEqyiles who bit off Mink's hand into stone on the Harrison River; he changed a one-legged man into stone at StsE'lis; and he transformed whole villages of people who opposed him into animals, rocks, and landmarks. The Qals cycle demonstrates the Coast Salish concept of transformation as a world-ordering force. Everything in the landscape has a story behind it — every rock, every mountain, every bend in the river — recording where Qals walked and changed the world.
source: Franz Boas texts from StsE'lis; Brian Thom, Transformer Stories

title: The Two-Men-Who-Changed-Things (Coast Salish)
summary: The Coast Salish people of the Olympic Peninsula tell of Ho-ho-e-ap-bess, the Two-Men-Who-Changed-Things, who were the brothers of the Sun and the Moon. When the world was very young, there were no people, birds, or animals — only grass and sand and creatures that were neither animal nor human. The Two-Men-Who-Changed-Things came to earth to make it ready for a new race of people. They called all the creatures to them and transformed them: a thief became Seal, with shortened arms and tied legs so he had to catch his own fish; a creature with a long fishing spear and a white cape became Great Blue Heron, his spear becoming his bill and his cape becoming white neck feathers; a huge appetite couple became Raven and Crow, given strong beaks to tear food; Bluejay's uncooperative son became Mink, living on land and eating fish from the shore. They also created the yew tree to provide tough wood for bows. This cycle explains the origins of all Pacific Northwest animals through transformation.
source: Coast Salish oral tradition; Ella E. Clark, Indian Legends of the Pacific Northwest

title: Raven Steals the Sun (Nuu-chah-nulth)
summary: In the Nuu-chah-nulth version of this foundational myth, there was a time before sunlight when light was kept securely in a box guarded by a great chief sometimes called the Old Fisherman. Raven wanted this light and attempted various methods to release it. He transformed himself into a floating leaf that ended up in the teacup of the great chief's daughter. She accidentally ate the leaf and in due time gave birth to Son of Raven, who was actually Raven reborn. The child was doted upon by the chief, his grandfather. Son of Raven begged to see the box of light, and after persistent pleading, the chief relented and opened the box, releasing the light. Son of Raven immediately transformed back into Raven, seized the light, and flew high into the sky. He placed the light in the sky, creating the sun that shines down on all. This myth establishes Raven as the Nuu-chah-nulth's primary culture hero and explains the origin of day and night.
source: Nuu-chah-nulth oral tradition; The Canadian Encyclopedia

title: Raven Annoys Octopus (Nuu-chah-nulth)
summary: One morning as the tide went out, the old people came to sit and watch the ocean. They saw a woman walking along the beach with her hair long and strung into eight braids — she was Octopus, carrying a digging stick to look for clams. She sat down on a rock and began to dig. Soon another person came along the beach, tall with glossy black hair — it was Raven. The old people predicted he would bother Octopus, and they knew it was not a good idea. Raven walked right down to the rock where Octopus sat and began bothering her. He teased and pestered her while she tried to dig for clams. The story serves as a humorous cautionary tale about respecting others' boundaries and not interfering with people who are working. Raven's persistent meddling backfires, teaching him (and the listener) that pestering others leads to unpleasant consequences. The Nuu-chah-nulth version emphasizes Raven's trickster nature and his tendency to interfere in others' affairs.
source: Nuu-chah-nulth oral tradition; native-languages.org

title: Kwatyat and Tihtipihin Rescue Their Mother (Nuu-chah-nulth)
summary: Two Nuu-chah-nulth brothers, Tihtipihin and Kwatyat, witnessed a great monster swallow their mother whole. Without hesitation, they willingly allowed themselves to be swallowed by the same monster so they could rescue her. Inside the monster's stomach, they found their mother alive along with other swallowed victims. Using their wits and supernatural strength, the brothers cut through the monster's stomach from the inside, freeing themselves, their mother, and all the other captives. They escaped safely as the monster perished. This story of filial devotion and brotherly courage is one of the most important Nuu-chah-nulth hero tales. Kwatyat, the younger brother, is particularly significant as he is recognized as a transformer figure in Nuu-chah-nulth mythology who later helped shape the world. The story emphasizes family loyalty, bravery in the face of overwhelming danger, and the power of cooperation between siblings.
source: Nuu-chah-nulth oral tradition; Wikipedia

title: Andaokut Rescues the Children (Nuu-chah-nulth)
summary: A woman whose children had been stolen by Malahas, a malicious forest goddess, wept uncontrollably. From her mucus or tears, a child was born — Andaokut. This miraculous birth child grew with extraordinary speed and possessed supernatural abilities. Andaokut set out to rescue the stolen children, tracking Malahas to her remote forest lair. Using courage and cleverness, he confronted Malahas and defeated her, freeing all the children she had captured. He killed Malahas and returned the children safely to their families. This story from Nuu-chah-nulth tradition exemplifies the theme of miraculous birth and the triumph of good over evil. Andaokut represents hope born from despair, showing that even from a mother's tears, salvation can emerge. The defeat of Malahas demonstrates that even the most powerful evil beings can be overcome by determination and courage.
source: Nuu-chah-nulth oral tradition; Wikipedia

title: Matlose the Hobgoblin (Nuu-chah-nulth)
summary: Matlose is the most feared hobgoblin spirit of the Nuu-chah-nulth people, described as a monstrous Caliban-like creature. His head resembles something that might have been human but is not, and his uncouth bulk is horrid with black bristles. His monstrous teeth and curved claws are like those of a bear, capable of rending prey into morsels with a single stroke. Most terrifying of all is his voice — whoever hears Matlose's terrible voice falls as if smitten, paralyzed by fear. He lurks in the deep forests and remote mountains of Vancouver Island, preying on anyone who ventures too far from the safety of the village. Shamans and warriors who successfully confront Matlose gain immense supernatural power. The Matlose legend serves as both a cautionary tale about the dangers of the wilderness and a testament to the power of the spirit world that surrounds the Nuu-chah-nulth people.
source: Nuu-chah-nulth oral tradition; native-languages.org

title: The Theft of Light (Tsimshian - Weeget)
summary: On Raven's first journey, Weeget (Giant/Raven) flew inland toward the east carrying a stone his father the chief had given him. When exhausted, he dropped the stone on the sea, where it became a large rock. At that time there was always darkness — no daylight existed. Weeget put on his raven skin and scattered salmon and trout roe into every river and creek, declaring that all waters should have fish. He then flew up through a hole in the sky to reach heaven. Near the chief of heaven's house, he found a spring where the chief's daughter drank. He turned into a floating object, was swallowed, and was born as a human child. He stole the light (called maa) and escaped back to earth. When the Frog people at the Nass River mocked him while fishing for olachen and refused his requests, Weeget broke the maa, releasing daylight. The north wind drove the Frogs away, and they froze onto a rock where they remain as stone to this day. Thus the Tsimshian explain the origin of daylight.
source: Franz Boas, Tsimshian Mythology; Tsimshian oral tradition

title: The Story of Asdiwal (Tsimshian)
summary: Asdiwal is one of the most important Tsimshian hero narratives, recorded in multiple versions by Franz Boas. The story begins with a mother and daughter dying of starvation during a winter famine in the Skeena River region.
