A supernatural being named Hatsenas (a bird of good omen) appears, provides food, and fathers Asdiwal with the younger woman. Hatsenas gives his son magical objects including a never-missing bow, invisible-making hat, and inexhaustible food basket. Asdiwal's adventures include hunting a magical bear up to the sky, where the bear transforms into Evening-Star, daughter of the Sun. He marries her after passing deadly trials set by the Sun. Returning to earth, Asdiwal marries a Tsimshian chief's daughter and becomes wealthy through potlatches. He competes with his brothers-in-law in sea-lion hunting but is abandoned on a reef during a storm. His father saves him. Eventually Asdiwal goes mountain hunting without his snowshoes, gets lost, and is turned to stone with his dog and lance — a transformation that can still be seen on a mountain at Ginadâos Lake.
source: Franz Boas, Tsimshian Texts (1895, 1902, 1912); Tsimshian oral tradition recorded by Henry Tate

title: Raven Becomes Voracious (Tsimshian)
summary: This Tsimshian myth explains how Raven acquired his insatiable appetite and voracious nature. Before this story, Raven was not known for his hunger. Through a series of events involving trickery, greed, and supernatural encounters, Raven's appetite grew beyond all bounds. He began devouring everything in sight, from salmon to berries, from seal meat to olachen grease. His hunger became so legendary that it defined his character. The story serves as an etiological myth explaining why ravens and crows are scavengers who eat constantly and are always hungry. It also carries a moral lesson about the dangers of greed and insatiable desire. Raven's voraciousness is both a humorous trait — he is constantly scheming to get food — and a serious warning about what happens when consumption becomes uncontrolled. The story is part of the larger Tsimshian Raven cycle that portrays Txamsem as both a world-creating culture hero and a deeply flawed, comic figure.
source: Franz Boas, Tsimshian Mythology; Tsimshian oral tradition

title: Wolf Clan and the Salmon (Tsimshian)
summary: This Tsimshian legend tells of a volcanic eruption caused by disrespect for the salmon. The Wolf Clan, one of the four major Tsimshian clans, had specific protocols for harvesting and honoring the first salmon of the season. One year, a member of the Wolf Clan failed to follow the proper rituals — he did not welcome the salmon chief properly, did not prepare the first salmon with the required ceremony, and showed disrespect to the salmon people. In response, the supernatural forces beneath the mountains grew angry. A great volcanic eruption occurred, devastating the land and reminding all Tsimshian people of the sacred obligation to honor the salmon. The story reinforces the fundamental Northwest Coast principle that humans and salmon are in a reciprocal relationship: humans must treat salmon with respect or face catastrophic consequences. It also explains volcanic activity in the Nass and Skeena River regions through the lens of spiritual cause and effect.
source: Tsimshian oral tradition; native-languages.org

title: The Porcupine and the Beaver (Tsimshian)
summary: This Tsimshian myth from the William Beynon collection tells of a time when animals spoke like human beings and possessed great powers to change the weather. Porcupine and Beaver, two animal-people, had a contest of abilities that determined their roles in the world. Porcupine demonstrated his cleverness and defensive capabilities, while Beaver showed his industrious nature and engineering skills. The story carries important teachings about using one's natural gifts wisely and respecting the different abilities of others. It is also an etiological tale explaining the physical characteristics and behaviors of both porcupines and beavers. Porcupine's quills and Beaver's tail receive supernatural explanations in the narrative. Recorded by William Beynon, a Tsimshian ethnographer who worked with Franz Boas, this story represents one of the many animal-person tales that convey moral teachings while explaining the natural world.
source: William Beynon, Tsimshian Stories; Columbia University Library

title: The Four Carpenters and the Creation of the World (Nuxalk)
summary: When the world was first created, the Creator Alhkw'ntam had help from Four Carpenters called the Masmasalanicw, who were like human beings with wings — angels. They carved the first ancestors from the cosmic tree Tcncniyaaq (the Milky Way). There were 45 original families, each with their own room in the House of Smayusta (the Creator's house in the upper world). The ancestors were given cwmnwas (spirit) placed in the bone of the back of their neck, and slhup'tsta (breath). They were given names, food, tools, a code of ethics, and protocols, all placed within a nuslhiixwta (treasure box). Hanging on the walls were animal, bird, and sea creature cloaks. Each ancestor chose a cloak — grizzly bear, raven, eagle, killer whale, wolf, mountain goat, heron — and danced with it. The Creator threw his sun blanket over himself and transformed into the sun. The ancestors did the same, transforming into their chosen animals. Guided by the eyelashes of the sun, they floated down to earth and removed their cloaks, which returned to heaven. This is the Nuxalk creation story, explaining the origin of all Nuxalk clans and their crests.
source: Nuxalk oral tradition; Nuxalk Nation official website

title: Raven Brings Light (Nuxalk - Qwaxw)
summary: In the beginning, when the world was covered in darkness, the people were in constant sadness and prayed to the Creator Alhkw'ntam for light. Alhkw'ntam held the sun in a box in his longhouse in Nusmata (the land above) and let no one see it. The Four Carpenters who made the world were sitting around a fire when the eldest, Yulm, grabbed a piece of charcoal and shook it in his hands. When he opened his hands, it was a bird. The second Carpenter made its wings, the third made its eyes, and the youngest gave it life. They asked the bird to say its name and it flew into the sky crying 'Qwaxw, qwaxw, qwaxw!' — they named it Qwaxw, Raven. The people begged Raven to get the light. He flew to Nusmata and observed Alhkw'ntam's granddaughter Skimina. He transformed into eagle down, then a hemlock needle, but she blew him away. Finally he muddied her drinking water, and she drank him in. She became pregnant and Raven was born as her child. Raven cried for the box holding the light until Alhkw'ntam gave in. Raven broke the globe against the wall, and light leaked through the smoke vent to become the sun, stars, and moon. The people were no longer sad.
source: Nuxalk oral tradition; Nuxalk Nation official website; Franz Boas, The Mythology of the Bella Coola Indians

title: The Story of Ista (Nuxalk)
summary: Ista is the name of the First Woman who descended to Nuxalknalus (King Island) in Nuxalk tradition. She came down to earth and decorated her crest on a blanket with abalone shell and danced up the coast. Ista was greatly admired for how she chose to show her connection to the Creator. Her story is part of the Nuxalk understanding of how the first ancestors established territories and rights to the land. Traditional family names are handed down and validated at potlatches. The Ista story also includes the tale of Patsallht (First Born), who thought he was the only one on earth. He was given a medicine box containing a 'mukw' of shame, stubbornness, and jealousy. He met the Sisawk people (Society of Chiefs) but was ashamed he was not a member. Later, he met a beautiful woman wearing a red blanket who proposed marriage. She was a grizzly bear woman who built him a home from hemlock. When she left him, First Born refused to leave the house. People followed his puppy to find him and convinced him to feast with them by offering to paint their faces black. The Ista people were ultimately wiped out by a cannibal raiding party from far away.
source: Nuxalk oral tradition; Skwucwlikwana (Lucy Mack); Nuxalk Nation

title: The Sun Tests His Son-in-Law (Nuxalk)
summary: In this Nuxalk myth, the daughter of the Sun married a human man known as Salmon Boy. The Sun was not pleased with his daughter's choice and subjected his new son-in-law to a series of deadly tests. The Sun took Salmon Boy to a mountain of unparalleled steepness where mountain goats climbed, commanding him to kill one. Salmon Boy succeeded with the help of his wife. Next, the Sun required him to draw water from a spring whose walls closed in on anyone who approached. Again his wife helped him. The Sun tried to bake him in a supernaturally hot sweat lodge, burn him on hot stones, and crush him with a falling tree. Each time Salmon Boy survived through his wife's guidance and his own growing supernatural power. Finally recognizing his son-in-law's worth, the Sun accepted him and bestowed upon him the gift of bringing salmon to the people. Salmon Boy returned to earth as a powerful being who taught the Nuxalk people how to properly catch, prepare, and honor the salmon, establishing the sacred relationship between the people and the salmon runs.
source: Franz Boas, The Mythology of the Bella Coola Indians; Nuxalk oral tradition

title: The Child and the Cannibal (Nuxalk)
summary: This Nuxalk legend tells of a magical boy who defeats a man-eating monster that terrorized the people. A cannibal giant (Boqs) had been stealing children from a Nuxalk village, carrying them off to his remote mountain lair to devour. The people were helpless against the monster's supernatural strength and ferocity. A young boy, born with extraordinary powers and protected by supernatural helpers, volunteered to confront the cannibal. Using cleverness, courage, and his spirit-given abilities, he tracked the monster to its den. He outwitted the slow-witted giant, leading it into traps and finally killing it with the help of the forest spirits. He freed all the captured children and returned them safely to the village. The boy became a great chief and his descendants established one of the Nuxalk clans. This is part of a broader Nuxalk tradition of hero-child stories where young protagonists overcome powerful supernatural adversaries, demonstrating that age and size are less important than courage and spiritual power.
source: Nuxalk oral tradition; native-languages.org; Franz Boas

title: Origin of the Chinook People (Chinook)
summary: When Old Man South Wind was traveling north, he met an Old Woman who was a giant. Hungry and tired, he asked for food. She gave him a net and told him to catch fish. South Wind caught a little whale and was about to cut it crossways when the old giantress warned him to slit it down the back instead. He ignored her and cut crossways. The whale transformed into a huge bird so big that when it flew into the air it hid the sun and the noise of its wings shook the earth — it was Thunderbird. Thunderbird flew north and landed on Saddleback Mountain near the Columbia River, where it laid a nest full of eggs. The old giantress followed and found the nest. She broke an egg, found it not good, and threw it down the mountainside. Before it reached the valley, it became an Indian. She broke more eggs and each one became an Indian. This is how the Chinook people were created. The story also explains why Indians never cut the first salmon crossways — they always slit it down the back, following the lesson of South Wind's mistake.
source: James Swan (1857); Chinook oral tradition; native-languages.org

title: Blue Jay Visits Ghost Town (Chinook - Ioi and Blue Jay)
summary: Blue Jay's sister Ioi was bought by ghosts as a wife and disappeared to the land of the dead. After a year, Blue Jay went searching for her. He asked all the trees and birds where people go when they die. He journeyed to the ghost town, which appeared to him as a place of bones and decay, but the ghosts saw it as beautiful. Ioi warned Blue Jay not to laugh at the ghosts or behave disrespectfully, but Blue Jay could not control himself. He kicked skulls, laughed at the skeletons, and shouted at the ghost people who were only skeletons to his eyes. When the ghosts went to butcher a whale, Blue Jay saw only a beached skeleton. When they brought in firewood, he saw only bark. Ioi kept explaining that his eyes saw differently because he was alive — the ghosts saw everything as beautiful. He tried to join the ghost dances, but when the ghosts danced gracefully, Blue Jay stood on his head. He died a second time and remained in the ghost world forever. This haunting story explores the boundary between life and death and the dangers of lacking respect for the supernatural world.
source: Chinook Texts, Smithsonian Institution; Chinook oral tradition from Charles Cultee

title: Robin and Blue-Jay (Chinook)
summary: Blue Jay and Robin, two brothers, were hungry. Blue Jay called a large fish (the sleeper) to come ashore through trickery and speared it. When they returned home, Robin asked for the tail, breast, and head, making Blue Jay angry. He said that the Q!têê'nse (a supernatural monster) would eat the fish instead. Robin cried and left. Blue Jay softened and called his brother back, giving him all the choice parts. Later, Blue Jay dreamed that people were sending for him to cure a sick person. Blue Jay and Robin journeyed to cure a duck who was choking, receiving payment of a lake's worth of roots. Next they cured a wolf girl who was choking on an elk's kneecap, receiving two canoes full of meat and grease. But Blue Jay's arrogance and greed in demanding excessive payment led to consequences. The story demonstrates the Chinook trickster cycle's typical structure: Blue Jay is boastful and selfish but ultimately provides for his family through his supernatural curing abilities, while long-suffering Robin represents patience and humility.
source: Franz Boas, Chinook Texts (1894); Chinook oral tradition from Charles Cultee

title: Blue Jay Finds a Wife (Chinook)
summary: Ioi decided it was high time for Blue Jay to quit his playful trickery and settle down with a wife. She told him he must select a wife from the land of the dead, from the Supernatural People. Ioi recommended that Blue Jay choose an old woman and suggested the recently deceased wife of a chief. Blue Jay went to the ghost town and courted the ghost woman. True to his nature, he behaved foolishly, misinterpreting the customs of the dead and acting disrespectfully. He made inappropriate jokes and did not understand that the ghosts perceived reality differently than the living. Despite Ioi's warnings, Blue Jay's behavior caused chaos in the ghost village. The story highlights Blue Jay's inability to mature and learn from experience, making him a comic but ultimately tragic figure. It is also one of several Chinook stories that explore the relationship between the living and the dead, suggesting that the boundary between worlds must be approached with humility and respect.
source: Chinook oral tradition; native-languages.org

title: Coyote and the Creation of the Tribes (Chinook/Chelan)
summary: Long ago, the Great Chief Above made the world, then the animals and birds. After twelve moons, the animal people gathered. Bluejay, Meadowlark, and Coyote complained about their names, but the Creator declared that names would stay as given. The Creator told Coyote he would be the head of all creatures and given power to keep peace. As the Creator foretold, things became bad — mountains swallowed creatures, winds blew them away. Coyote stopped the mountains and winds, rescued the creatures, and made laws for natural forces. When Coyote's work was done, the Creator told Coyote he would not make the first human. Instead, the four Wolf Brothers took over. The youngest Wolf Brother killed Beaver and cut it into twelve pieces according to the Creator's instructions. The pieces were given to different animal people who carried them to different regions, where each piece became a different Indian tribe: the Nez Perce from the liver, the Spokane, Lake people, Flathead, and others from different organs. The blood became the Blackfeet. This story explains the origin of the twelve tribes of the Columbia Plateau region.
source: Chelan/Chinook oral tradition; greatdreams.com

title: The Nuxalk Origin of the Sun (Nuxalk)
summary: The Nuxalk people tell that before the sun existed, there was only the dim light of copper-colored darkness. The people prayed constantly to Alhkw'ntam, the Creator, for light. In Nusmata, the house of the gods in the upper world, the Creator's granddaughter Skimina lived. The Four Carpenters created Raven (Qwaxw) from a piece of charcoal to answer the people's prayers. Raven, after being born as Skimina's child, cried persistently for the box containing the globe of light. When Alhkw'ntam finally relented, Raven rolled the globe back and forth until he smashed it against the wall. Light poured out through the smoke hole, flooding the world with brightness. The breaking of the globe created the sun for daytime, the moon for night, and the stars scattered across the sky. The people's sadness ended, and they celebrated the gift of light. This myth emphasizes the cooperative work of the carpenters, Raven's role as mediator between heaven and earth, and the Nuxalk belief that the sun is a living being with whom they maintain a spiritual relationship.
source: Nuxalk oral tradition; Nuxalk Nation; Franz Boas

title: The Great Flood and the Origin of the Tides (Tlingit)
summary: Raven made a woman under the earth to have charge of the rise and fall of the tides. One time he wanted to explore everything beneath the ocean and commanded the tide woman to raise the water. She made the tide rise very slowly so the people had time to load their canoes and climb to the mountaintops. As the water lifted the canoes between the mountains, bears and other wild animals swam out to them, and those with dogs had good protection. Some people walled the mountaintops and tied their canoes inside. When the tide began to fall, the people followed it down, but the trees were gone and they had nothing for firewood, so they were destroyed by the cold. When Raven returned, he turned all the remaining people and animals into stone. He then made new human beings from leaves, which is why people die off rapidly in autumn when leaves fall. This Tlingit flood narrative explains both the origin of tides and the seasonal cycle of death, reflecting on human fragility and the consequences of curiosity.
source: John R. Swanton, Tlingit Myths and Texts (1909)

title: Komokwa and the Undersea Kingdom (Haida)
summary: In Haida tradition, the undersea world is ruled by powerful supernatural beings who control the wealth of the sea. The sea chiefs live in houses made of copper and abalone shell, surrounded by supernatural beings including the killer whale people, seal people, and halibut people. Those who drown do not truly die but are taken to live in the undersea kingdom, where they may acquire great supernatural power and wealth. The supernatural killer whales (SGaana) are the most powerful of the undersea beings; they can transform into human form and own houses, territories, and crests. The Haida people take their clan crests from these supernatural beings, copying their likenesses for use on totem poles, blankets, and ceremonial regalia. The relationship between the Haida and the undersea kingdom is reciprocal: humans show respect through proper protocols, and the supernatural beings provide salmon, halibut, seals, and whales for the people's survival.
source: Haida oral tradition; Haida Gwaii Pledge; Terri-Lynn Williams-Davidson

title: The D'Sonoqua Who Became Wealth (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: In one Kwakwaka'wakw narrative, a clever boy was captured by D'Sonoqua and carried in her basket to her forest house. While she prepared to cook him, the boy noticed that D'Sonoqua was dim-witted with poor eyesight. He tricked her into showing him her treasures and then pushed her into her own cooking fire. As she burned, her wealth — copper shields, abalone shells, and blankets — spilled out. The boy took the treasures back to his village and became a great chief. But D'Sonoqua's ashes blew into the air and became mosquitoes, which is why mosquitoes bite humans — they carry D'Sonoqua's eternal hunger. This story also has versions where D'Sonoqua cannot truly die; she regenerates from any wound, and the only way to stop her is to burn her completely and scatter the ashes. Her association with wealth makes her a complex figure: she is a terrifying cannibal monster but also a source of great prosperity for those brave and clever enough to defeat her. At potlatches, a chief wearing the D'Sonoqua mask signals the end of the ceremony.
source: Kwakwaka'wakw oral tradition; Audrey Hawthorn, Kwakiutl Art; U'mista Cultural Centre

title: The Transformer Who Became the Mountains (Coast Salish)
summary: The Coast Salish tell of a Transformer (Xe'las) who walked through the land making the world right for the coming humans. Near Lake Chelan, he encountered people who had become corrupted by greed and selfishness. He transformed them into stone, their canoes becoming islands in the lake. Near the Fraser River, he found a woman with a vagina full of teeth who emasculated men — he turned her into a rock formation that still guards the river. In the San Juan Islands, he found two sisters fighting over a man and turned them into a pair of islands called The Sisters. The Transformer also created Mount Rainier (Tacobud) as a refuge during the great flood, and the mountain still bears the scars of the waters. Every rock, island, and mountain in Coast Salish territory has a story of transformation, creating a landscape that serves as a permanent record of moral teachings. The land itself is a library of stories that instruct people in proper behavior and remind them of the consequences of their actions.
source: Coast Salish oral tradition; Hibulb Cultural Center; Brian Thom

title: The Bluejay Cycle (Coast Salish)
summary: Bluejay is a prominent trickster figure in Coast Salish mythology, particularly among the Chemainus, Cowichan, and other Salish communities. He is generally a benevolent being who helps people, but he is also extremely foolish and careless, and stories about him are often humorous or slapstick. In one classic story, Bluejay boasted that he could steal the sun, but his lies got him into trouble when the people took him seriously and he had to deliver on his impossible promise. In another, he tried to imitate the skills of other animals and birds, with disastrously funny results. Bluejay often interacts with his long-suffering sister Ioi, who tries to keep him out of trouble but fails because Bluejay refuses to listen to good advice. Unlike Raven, who possesses genuine supernatural power, Bluejay's adventures typically stem from his foolishness rather than malice. The Bluejay stories serve as comic relief in the storytelling tradition while also teaching lessons about humility, honesty, and the dangers of boastfulness.
source: Coast Salish oral tradition; Salish Myths and Legends (University of Nebraska Press)

title: Kwatyat the Transformer (Nuu-chah-nulth)
summary: Kwatyat is one of the most important transformer figures in Nuu-chah-nulth mythology. After his adventures with his brother Tihtipihin rescuing their mother from the monster, Kwatyat traveled the land transforming the world and making it ready for human beings. He changed dangerous beings into harmless animals, taught people how to fish and hunt, and established the proper relationship between humans and the supernatural world. Kwatyat also had a trickster aspect — he could be foolish and greedy, and many stories tell of his failed attempts to imitate others or his schemes backfiring. In one story, he tried to catch salmon by building a weir but was too impatient and it collapsed. In another, he tried to fly like an eagle and fell into the ocean. Despite his flaws, Kwatyat's transformations established the fundamental order of Nuu-chah-nulth life. He is sometimes paired with or compared to Raven, sharing the role of culture hero and trickster in a complementary way.
source: Nuu-chah-nulth oral tradition; Wikipedia

title: One Who Walks All Over the Sky (Tsimshian)
summary: This Tsimshian myth explains the origin of the sun, moon, and night sky. After Weeget (Giant/Raven) brought light from heaven, there was still the question of who would carry the sun across the sky each day and what the patterns of stars meant. The One Who Walks All Over the Sky is a supernatural being assigned to carry the sun on his daily journey from east to west. He walks across the dome of the sky, and his path determines the seasons. When he walks high, it is summer; when low, winter. The stars are his footprints or the campfires of other sky beings. This story is part of the larger Tsimshian astronomical tradition that interprets the night sky as a map of supernatural activities. The movements of celestial bodies are guided by the same social and spiritual principles that govern Tsimshian life on earth — chiefs, potlatches, journeys, and encounters with supernatural beings all have their counterparts in the sky world.
source: Franz Boas, Tsimshian Mythology; Tsimshian oral tradition

title: The Bear Who Married a Woman (Tsimshian)
summary: A Tsimshian woman gathering berries in the forest encountered a bear who removed his bearskin and revealed himself as a handsome man. He took her to his village, where the bear people lived just like humans inside their lodges but appeared as bears to outsiders. The woman married him, learned the ways of the bear people, and gave birth to children who were half-human, half-bear. Her husband provided abundantly for her, bringing salmon and berries. She grew content with her bear husband, but when her human brothers came searching for her, she was forced to choose between her two families. The brothers killed the bear husband in a hunt, but the woman begged them not to harm her children. The children grew up with special powers of communication between humans and bears. Their descendants became the Bear Clan. This cross-cultural marriage story, found throughout the Northwest Coast, establishes the bear as a relative rather than an animal, requiring respect and proper protocol in hunting.
source: Tsimshian oral tradition; Franz Boas, Tsimshian Mythology; native-languages.org

title: The Wonderful Tar-Baby
summary: Brer Fox fashions a doll made of tar and turpentine and sets it in the road to trap Brer Rabbit. When Brer Rabbit greets the silent Tar-Baby and receives no reply, he grows angry and strikes it, getting his paw stuck. He strikes with the other paw, then kicks, and finally butts with his head—each limb becoming hopelessly ensnared in the sticky tar. Brer Fox emerges from hiding, delighted at his capture, but Brer Rabbit's clever pleading ultimately allows him to escape when he begs not to be thrown into the briar patch—the one place he was born and bred to thrive.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: Brer Rabbit and the Briar Patch (Bred and Bawn)
summary: After Brer Fox captures Brer Rabbit using the Tar-Baby, he declares his intent to kill and eat him. Brer Rabbit pleads for his life, begging not to be thrown into the briar patch, suggesting instead that he be roasted, hanged, drowned, or skinned. The more Brer Rabbit begs to avoid the briar patch, the more determined Brer Fox becomes to throw him there. Finally, Brer Fox snatches Brer Rabbit by the hind legs and flings him deep into the briar patch. To Brer Fox's astonishment, Brer Rabbit appears on a hillside, picking burrs from his fur, and shouts back: 'Bred en bawn in a brier-patch, Brer Fox—bred en bawn in a brier-patch!' The briar patch, which would tear the skin of any other creature, was Brer Rabbit's ancestral home.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: Brer Rabbit's Laughing Place
summary: Brer Fox catches Brer Rabbit and declares he will finally kill him. Brer Rabbit, always quick-witted, says he does not care about dying but begs to be allowed to visit his 'laughing place' one last time to die happy. Curious, Brer Fox agrees and follows Brer Rabbit into the woods. Brer Rabbit leads him to a hollow tree filled with a swarm of angry bees. Brer Rabbit points to the tree and whispers that his laughing place is inside. When Brer Fox peers into the hollow, the bees swarm out and attack him furiously. While Brer Fox flees in agony, Brer Rabbit laughs himself silly and escapes once again, proving that his most dangerous weapon is his clever tongue.
source: African American oral tradition, collected in Uncle Remus stories

title: Brer Rabbit and the Moon in the Millpond
summary: A classic African American trickster tale in which Brer Rabbit, feeling thirsty, goes to the millpond to drink and sees the moon reflected in the water. Mistaking it for a golden cheese wheel, he tries to retrieve it. Unable to reach it from the bank, he tricks Brer Fox into helping him by claiming there is a valuable treasure in the pond. Brer Fox ties a rope around Brer Rabbit's waist and lowers him down, but Brer Rabbit only stirs up the water, making the reflection disappear. He claims the treasure sank and convinces Brer Fox to jump in after it, leaving Brer Fox cold and humiliated while Brer Rabbit laughs and feasts on the cheese he finds elsewhere.
source: African American oral tradition (variant collected throughout the US South)

title: Brer Rabbit Nibbles Up the Butter
summary: Brer Rabbit, Brer Fox, Brer Possum, and Brer Coon together churn butter and set it aside to share later. Brer Rabbit grows hungry and secretly eats all the butter. When the friends discover the butter is gone, Brer Rabbit blames Brer Possum and suggests they test who is guilty by having each lie down in the sun and see whose belly the butter melts from. Brer Rabbit arranges things so that Brer Possum ends up sitting on the butter smear, and when the sun warms him, the butter appears on his fur. Brer Possum is punished for Brer Rabbit's crime, while Brer Rabbit escapes blame through clever manipulation. The story explains why the possum plays dead when caught—he learned to be wary of being tricked again.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: Old Mr. Rabbit, He's a Good Fisherman
summary: Brer Rabbit, tired and hungry, finds his way to a creek where he sees Brer Fox and Brer Wolf fishing. Rather than asking for food, Brer Rabbit pretends to be a great fisherman himself. He cuts a willow branch, ties a string to it, and sits downstream, pretending to fish while actually eating the berries he finds nearby. When Brer Fox and Brer Wolf catch nothing and see Brer Rabbit with a full belly, they demand to know his secret. Brer Rabbit tells them the best fishing is done with a snare around one's tail—convincing them to tie their tails together and cast out. When a large fish tugs, they both pull, only to catch nothing while their tails are painfully tangled. Brer Rabbit wanders off, well-fed and amused at their gullibility.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: Brer Rabbit Rides Brer Fox
summary: Brer Rabbit, seeking to prove his superiority, convinces Miss Meadows and the girls that he can ride Brer Fox like a horse. Brer Fox overhears this boast and vows revenge. The next day, Brer Rabbit pretends to be sick and asks Brer Fox to carry him to Miss Meadows's house. Once on Brer Fox's back, Brer Rabbit digs in his heels and rides Brer Fox straight to the gathering, where he shows off by declaring: 'This is the way I ride Brer Fox!' Humiliated, Brer Fox shakes him off, but Brer Rabbit escapes into a hollow tree. Brer Fox enlists Brer Wolf to help dig him out, but Brer Rabbit tricks them by claiming enemies are approaching and sends them fleeing. The tale is part of a cycle of African trickster stories also found in the Caribbean and South America.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: Brer Terrapin Outruns Brer Rabbit
summary: Brer Rabbit, proud of his speed, mocks Brer Terrapin for his slow, plodding pace. Brer Terrapin challenges Brer Rabbit to a race. Knowing he cannot win on speed alone, Brer Terrapin stations his wife and several family members at intervals along the race course. At each mile marker, Brer Rabbit calls out 'Brer Terrapin!' and one of the terrapin family calls back, 'Here I am!' After several such exchanges, Brer Rabbit grows exhausted and confused, unable to understand how the slow terrapin keeps ahead of him. When Brer Terrapin crosses the finish line first, Brer Rabbit collapses from exhaustion. The story—a variant of the tortoise and hare theme—is a cornerstone of African American animal folklore, reinforcing that patience and strategy triumph over arrogance.
source: African American oral tradition (Gullah Geechee and Uncle Remus variants)

title: Mr. Fox and the Deceitful Frogs
summary: Brer Fox, having not eaten for days, spots a large bullfrog at the edge of the pond and makes a grab for him. The frog dives deep, and Brer Fox, desperate, decides to drink the pond dry. He drinks until his belly swells enormously, but the pond level barely drops. Meanwhile, the frogs mock him from the safety of the water. The bullfrog surfaces and offers Brer Fox advice: tie a stone to his neck to sink himself, then he can catch all the frogs underwater. Brer Fox, not realizing he is being mocked, considers this seriously. Other animals pass by and laugh at the foolish fox attempting to drink an entire pond. Brer Fox eventually gives up, exhausted and humiliated, while the frogs continue their chorus of croaking laughter. The tale reinforces the cleverness of small creatures against larger predators.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: Miss Cow Falls a Victim to Mr. Rabbit
summary: Brer Rabbit, hungry and with nothing to eat, approaches Miss Cow and asks her for some milk, claiming his children are starving. Miss Cow refuses, saying the milk is for her calf. Brer Rabbit then asks her to come to Miss Meadows's house where there is plenty of grass, but Miss Cow refuses again. Later, Brer Rabbit hides behind a bush and calls out in a deep voice, pretending to be a wild animal threatening to eat Miss Cow. He frightens her into running to Miss Meadows's gate, where he pretends to defend her. Grateful, Miss Cow offers him all the milk he wants. The next day, Brer Rabbit repeats the trick with variations, eventually milking Miss Cow dry. When Miss Cow realizes she has been tricked, Brer Rabbit is long gone.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: How Brer Rabbit Lost His Fine Bushy Tail
summary: In this pourquoi tale explaining why rabbits have short tails, Brer Rabbit once had a long, beautiful, bushy tail that he showed off constantly. Brer Fox grew tired of Brer Rabbit's vanity and devised a plan. During a cold winter, Brer Fox went ice fishing and called out to Brer Rabbit, showing him a string of fish he had caught. Brer Rabbit, greedy for the fish, asked how to catch them himself. Brer Fox told him to cut a hole in the ice, dip his beautiful tail in, and sit there until the fish bit—then pull up the tail with a mighty jerk. Brer Rabbit sat all day until his tail froze solid in the ice. When evening came, he gave a mighty jerk and his tail snapped off clean, leaving only the short stub rabbits have to this day. Brer Fox laughed heartily at the vanity that cost Brer Rabbit his finest feature.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: Brer Rabbit Finds His Match at Last
summary: Brer Rabbit, overconfident after countless successful tricks, encounters a creature he cannot outsmart. In some versions, it is Old Man Tarrypin (the terrapin) who turns the tables on Brer Rabbit; snip in others, it is a wise old woman or a young girl who matches wits with him. Brer Rabbit tries every trick in his repertoire—false friendliness, reverse psychology, flattery, and fear—but nothing works. The creature remains calm, sees through every deception, and ultimately traps Brer Rabbit in a situation where his cleverness fails him. Brer Rabbit must humble himself, admit defeat, and negotiate his release. This tale serves as a caution that even the most cunning trickster may eventually meet his match, reminding listeners that overconfidence is the trickster's own greatest vulnerability.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: John Henry vs. the Steam Drill
summary: John Henry, the legendary steel-driving man, worked as a hammer-swinging laborer blasting tunnels through mountains for the expanding railroad. When a salesman brought a steam-powered drilling machine to the worksite, claiming it could outwork any man, the captain of the crew proposed a contest: man against machine. John Henry accepted the challenge to prove that human strength and dignity could not be replaced by cold iron. With his shaker holding the drill steady, John Henry swung his nine-pound hammer in great arcs, driving steel deeper and faster than the steam drill. For hours the contest raged, the rhythmic ring of John Henry's hammer against the drill echoing through the mountain. When the contest ended, John Henry had drilled fourteen feet while the steam drill had managed only nine. He had beaten the machine.
source: African American folk ballad tradition (first printed, Journal of American Folklore, 1909)

title: The Death of John Henry
summary: After defeating the steam drill, John Henry's heart, strained beyond its limit, gave out. With his hammer still in his hand, the mighty steel-driving man collapsed and died. His fellow workers gathered around as the man who had beaten the machine lay still. The ballad recounts that John Henry had a woman—Polly Ann, Mary Ann, or Julia Ann in various versions—who took up his hammer after his death. John Henry's funeral became a massive gathering where workers from every railroad line came to pay their respects. The ballad spread rapidly across the South, carried by itinerant workers, becoming one of the most recorded folk songs in American history. More than just a story, John Henry's death became a symbol of the human cost of industrialization and the struggle of Black workers against the dehumanizing forces of progress that sought to replace them.
source: African American folk ballad tradition

title: High John the Conqueror and the Devil's Daughter
summary: High John the Conqueror, an African prince sold into slavery who became a legendary trickster, falls in love with the Devil's beautiful daughter. The Devil, enraged, sets John a series of impossible tasks to keep them apart: John must clear sixty acres of forest in half a day, plow the land, plant it with corn, and harvest it all before sunset. The Devil's daughter, who loves John truly, secretly gives him a magical axe that fells trees on its own and a magical plow that turns the soil without effort. John completes every task with time to spare. The Devil then tries to kill John by setting his bed on fire, but John escapes by shape-shifting. John and the Devil's daughter steal the Devil's swiftest horses and flee. The Devil pursues them, but John and his lover transform themselves into various forms—a pond and a duck, a church and a preacher—evading capture at every turn until they escape forever.
source: Zora Neale Hurston, 'High John de Conquer' (The Sanctified Church, 1943); snip Virginia Hamilton, The People Could Fly

title: High John the Conqueror Brings Freedom
summary: According to the lore collected by Zora Neale Hurston, High John the Conqueror was more than a trickster—he was a spirit of liberation who moved invisibly among the enslaved. Born an African prince, John was captured and sold into slavery, but his spirit could never be broken. When the whip fell, John would 'take the spirit of his people out of their bodies' so they could not feel the pain. He could be heard laughing in the slave quarters at night, a sound that reminded the enslaved that freedom was coming. John planted the idea of emancipation in the minds of white people and provoked the Civil War itself. After emancipation, John returned to Africa, but according to Hurston, 'He left his power here' and 'he waits to return when his people call him.' High John's root—a dried tuber carried in a mojo bag—remains a central talisman in Hoodoo practice, symbolizing unbreakable spirit and the certainty of victory over oppression.
source: Zora Neale Hurston, 'High John de Conquer' (American Mercury, 1943)

title: Igbo Landing: The Flying Africans
summary: In May 1803, a group of 75 Igbo people from what is now Nigeria were captured and transported to Georgia. After surviving the Middle Passage, they were loaded onto a small vessel at Savannah and shipped to St. Simons Island to be sold at the slave market. Rather than submit to a life of bondage, the Igbo captives—led by their high-ranking warriors among them—rose up against their captors, took control of the ship, and forced it aground at Dunbar Creek. There, in a coordinated act of mass resistance, they walked deliberately into the water, singing in their native Igbo language, drowning themselves rather than accepting slavery. Some oral accounts say they did not drown at all but transformed into buzzards and flew back across the Atlantic to Africa. The site, Igbo Landing, became sacred ground in African American memory, birthing the enduring 'Flying Africans' mythos—the belief that those who refused to be enslaved could literally fly home to freedom.
source: Historical accounts (1803) + Gullah Geechee oral tradition; snip National Park Service; snip Georgia Encyclopedia

title: The People Could Fly
summary: In this beloved African American folktale collected by Virginia Hamilton, enslaved Africans secretly retained the magical ability to fly—a power brought from their homeland. One day, as an old man named Toby worked in the cotton fields under a cruel overseer's lash, he began to whisper ancient words in a language the other slaves had forgotten. One by one, the enslaved people remembered the magic of flight. Sarah, carrying her baby on her back, rose into the air. Young Jesse and his mother lifted off. The overseer watched in disbelief as worker after worker soared into the sky. Toby himself flew last, calling the magic words until all who remembered were gone. They flew across the ocean back to Africa, leaving their chains behind. But some chose to stay, their wings clipped by the harshness of bondage, and their descendants tell the story still. The tale expresses the deepest longing of the enslaved: that freedom meant not just escape but miraculous, transcendent return to the ancestral home.
source: Collected in The People Could Fly: American Black Folktales (Virginia Hamilton, 1985)

title: The Slave Who Became a Buzzard
summary: A Gullah version of the Flying Africans legend, recorded by the Federal Writers' Project in the late 1930s. Wallace Quarterman, a Gullah man born in 1844 on Sapelo Island, Georgia, recounted that the Igbo people who drowned at Dunbar Creek did not die at all. According to the oral tradition passed down through generations, the Igbo 'rise out of the water and turn into buzzards and fly back to Africa.' In Gullah belief, the buzzard is not a carrion eater but a sacred bird that carries the souls of the dead back across the Atlantic. This story reflects the deep spiritual connection between the Gullah people and their West African ancestors, and the belief that death by water was not an end but a transformation. The buzzard's circling flight over the Georgia coast is seen as the lingering presence of those who refused to be slaves, forever watching and waiting.
source: Federal Writers' Project Slave Narratives (1930s); snip Wallace Quarterman interview

title: Stagolee Shoots Billy Lyons
summary: On Christmas night, 1895, in a St. Louis saloon, the notorious bad-man Stagolee (based on historical figure Lee Shelton) shot William 'Billy' Lyons after a dispute. In the folk tradition, the argument began when Billy Lyons snatched Stagolee's prized Stetson hat from his head and refused to return it. Stagolee warned him: 'I don't care what you do with me, but don't you mess with my Stetson hat.' When Billy laughed and refused, Stagolee drew his .44 pistol. Billy begged for his life, pleading that he had a wife and children, but Stagolee replied, 'I don't care about your wife and kids—you should have thought of them before you touched my Stetson hat.' He shot Billy dead. The song spread like wildfire across America, with each new version making Stagolee more mythic, transforming a common barroom murder into a foundational American outlaw legend—the quintessential 'bad nigger' who answered to no man and no law.
source: Historical record (St. Louis Globe-Democrat, 1895); snip African American folk ballad tradition

title: Stagolee in Hell (Stagolee vs. the Devil)
summary: After Stagolee was hanged for murder, he arrived at the gates of Hell, still wearing his Stetson hat and carrying his .44 pistol. When the Devil tried to send him to his eternal punishment, Stagolee refused to submit. He pulled his pistol and challenged the Devil himself. The Devil, for all his power, found he could not control Stagolee. Stagolee kicked the Devil off his throne, sat down on it himself, and took over the running of Hell. In some versions, he set all the lost souls free; snip in others, he ran Hell more efficiently than the Devil ever did. The Devil eventually begged God to let Stagolee into Heaven, but Heaven refused as well, leaving Stagolee master of his own domain. This tale of a man so bad that Hell itself could not hold him became a cornerstone of African American 'toasts' (narrative poems), celebrating the ultimate rebellious figure who fears neither God nor Devil and answers to absolutely no one.
source: African American toast tradition; snip Julius Lester, Black Folktales (1969); snip folk ballad variants

title: Stagolee's Funeral
summary: The folk ballad describes Stagolee's funeral as an event unlike any before seen. Thousands of people came from miles around to see the baddest man who ever lived laid to rest. A procession of mourners stretched for blocks—the women weeping, the men silent and respectful. His coffin was reportedly so fine it cost more than most men earned in a year. The ballad describes the funeral train, the flowers, the hymns sung over his grave. Yet even in death, Stagolee remained defiant. The women who heard of his death 'could not stay at home,' rushing to the funeral in droves. Verses describe how 'the women in the West' heard the news and lamented, cementing Stagolee's status as a tragic romantic figure. Stagolee's funeral became a set-piece in African American folk memory: the moment when the community paused to honor a man who, for all his violence, represented a kind of freedom—the freedom to be utterly oneself, without apology or compromise, in a world that demanded constant submission from Black men.
source: African American folk ballad 'Stagger Lee' (multiple collected variants)

title: Jack in the Giants' Newground
summary: Jack, a lazy mountain boy, decides to seek his fortune after his mother grows tired of his idleness. He arrives at a king's house and learns that giants have overrun the king's 'newground' (newly cleared land) and will not let anyone farm it. Anyone who goes against them is eaten. Jack volunteers, armed only with his wits and a bag of tricks. He encounters the giants and, rather than fighting them directly, uses cleverness to turn them against one another. He convinces one giant that another has insulted his wife, leading the giants to fight among themselves. When the dust settles, the giants are either dead or fled, and Jack claims the reward. This tale originates from the European Jack Tale tradition but was carried into Appalachia by Scots-Irish settlers and preserved, particularly within African American communities of the Southern mountains, where Jack became a symbol of the clever underdog who overcomes overwhelming odds through wit rather than strength.
source: The Jack Tales (Richard Chase, 1943); snip Appalachian oral tradition shared by African American storytellers

title: Jack and the Bean Tree
summary: The Appalachian African American version of the classic Jack and the Beanstalk tale. Jack, a poor mountain boy, trades his family's only cow for a handful of seemingly worthless beans. His mother, furious, throws the beans out the window. Overnight, a giant beanstalk grows all the way to the sky. Jack climbs the beanstalk and discovers a world above the clouds—a giant's castle. He meets the giant's wife, who hides him from her husband, who 'smells the blood of a Christian.' Jack steals the giant's gold, a hen that lays golden eggs, and a magical singing harp. When the giant pursues him down the beanstalk, Jack chops it down with an axe, killing the giant. In the Appalachian versions, the setting shifts to mountain terrain, and Jack's language reflects the regional dialect. African American tellers of the tale emphasized themes of economic survival and outwitting a powerful, deadly enemy—resonant with a community navigating a world where power was concentrated in hostile hands.
source: Appalachian oral tradition; snip The Jack Tales (Richard Chase, 1943); snip Affrilachian Tales (Lyn Ford)

title: Jack and the Robbers
summary: Jack leaves home seeking adventure and meets a group of robbers in the forest. Rather than fleeing, Jack cleverly ingratiates himself with them, pretending to be a fierce outlaw. He joins their band and joins them on a raid of a wealthy house. When the robbers send Jack in to check if the house is empty, Jack finds it full of people but convinces the robbers the house is safe by using ventriloquism and mimicry. During the robbery attempt, Jack deliberately makes noise to alert the householders, then blames the commotion on ghosts. The robbers flee in terror, leaving all the loot behind. Jack collects the abandoned treasure and returns home a rich man. The tale highlights Jack's hallmark traits: he is not particularly brave or strong, but he is quick-witted and lucky, always landing on his feet through a combination of clever words and fortunate circumstances. In Affrilachian versions, Jack's voice and humor are distinctly rooted in African American storytelling cadences.
source: The Jack Tales (Richard Chase, 1943); snip Affrilachian Tales (Lyn Ford, 2012)

title: Jack and the Varmints
summary: Jack is tasked with spending a night alone in a haunted cabin in the woods as a test of courage. The cabin is supposedly infested with 'varmints'—supernatural creatures that have driven away everyone who came before. Jack, being too lazy and stubborn to be properly scared, settles into the cabin for the night. As midnight approaches, he hears scratching at the door, howling from the chimney, and strange shapes moving in the shadows. Rather than fleeing, Jack bangs pots, shouts insults, and throws things at the darkness. His sheer orneriness and refusal to be intimidated somehow drives the varmints away. By morning, the cabin is peaceful, and Jack collects the reward. In some versions, the varmints are revealed to be pranksters in costume; snip in others, they are genuine supernatural beings defeated by Jack's audacious spirit. The story resonates in the African American Appalachian tradition as a tale of facing one's fears with humor and determination rather than weapons.
source: The Jack Tales (Richard Chase, 1943); snip Affrilachian oral tradition

title: The Boo Hag
summary: In Gullah Geechee folklore, a young man named Emmet Fisher falls in love with a beautiful but mysterious woman who lives deep in the marsh. Against all warnings, he marries her. One night, Emmet pretends to be asleep and witnesses his bride remove her skin, hang it on a hook, and transform into a skinless red creature with bat-like wings. She flies out through the keyhole to ride her victims—sitting on their chests and stealing their breath while they sleep. Emmet confronts her the next morning, but she denies everything. He sprinkles cayenne pepper and salt around the room, and when she sheds her skin again that night, she cannot put it back on—the pepper burns her raw flesh. She flees into the night, forever skinless. The Boo Hag legend explains why Gullah people paint their doors and windows 'haint blue,' hang brooms by the entrance, and keep salt and pepper mixtures in corners: all ways to confuse, repel, or trap these night-visiting entities that drain the life force of the living.
source: Gullah Geechee oral tradition; snip Federal Writers' Project Slave Narratives; snip Drums and Shadows (1940)

title: The Plat-Eye
summary: The Plat-Eye is a malevolent, shapeshifting spirit in Gullah Geechee folklore, believed to be the restless ghost of a person who was wronged in life and died with unfinished business. The Plat-Eye takes various forms—an animal with one large glowing eye, a ball of light, or a shadowy figure—and haunts the place of its death or burial. It is intensely territorial and will attack anyone who intrudes upon its domain. Unlike the Boo Hag, which enters homes, the Plat-Eye stays outdoors, lurking in swamps, crossroads, and graveyards. To ward off a Plat-Eye, Gullah people carry a small burlap sachet filled with equal parts gunpowder and sulfur, whose strong smell offends the spirit and drives it away. The Plat-Eye tradition reflects African beliefs in ancestral spirits who require proper burial rites to find peace, combined with the harsh reality of the many enslaved people who died unjustly and were buried in unmarked graves along the Sea Islands coast.
source: Gullah Geechee oral tradition; snip Georgia Writers' Project, Drums and Shadows (1940)

title: Jacky-My-Lantern
summary: In this African American version of the will-o'-the-wisp legend collected by Joel Chandler Harris, Jacky-My-Lantern is a wandering spirit who carries a flickering light through the swamps at night, luring travelers from the safety of the road into the treacherous marshland. According to Uncle Remus, Jacky-My-Lantern was once a man who made a deal with the Devil and was cursed to wander the earth with only a lantern made from a hollowed turnip and a coal of fire to light his way, never finding rest. When travelers see the bobbing light and follow it, hoping to reach shelter, they soon find themselves sinking into the bog, unable to find their way back. The only protection against Jacky-My-Lantern is to carry a Bible, wear your coat inside out, or avoid being out at night near the swamp. The legend blends African American folklore with European jack-o'-lantern traditions, creating a distinctively Southern cautionary tale about the dangers of the unknown and the consequences of making deals with dark forces.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881); snip African American oral tradition

title: The Bell Witch
summary: Although the Bell Witch of Tennessee is one of America's most famous supernatural legends, its African American dimensions are often overlooked. Enslaved people on the Bell plantation near Adams, Tennessee, had their own interpretations of the entity that tormented the Bell family from 1817 to 1821. While white accounts describe the witch as the ghost of Kate Batts, African American oral traditions viewed the Bell Witch as a 'haint' connected to the horrors of slavery itself—a spirit born from the suffering of the enslaved on the plantation. Some accounts from formerly enslaved people describe the witch as a Boo Hag-like entity that could shed its skin and fly. The enslaved community used their own protective measures—salt, sulfur, haint blue paint, and conjure bags—to ward off the entity, often with greater success than the Bells' reliance on prayer and Christian ritual. The Bell Witch thus represents a convergence of African and European supernatural traditions on American soil.
source: Tennessee oral tradition; snip Slave narratives referencing Bell Witch; snip Drums and Shadows accounts

title: A Plantation Witch
summary: Collected by Joel Chandler Harris in the Uncle Remus stories, 'A Plantation Witch' tells of supernatural occurrences on the plantation. Uncle Remus recounts that witches on the plantation could transform into animals, particularly haunts and hags that rode people at night. According to the tale, a young enslaved man named 'Toby' was ridden by a hag every night and would wake up exhausted. An old conjure woman told him to sprinkle salt and pepper around his bed before sleeping. When the hag came that night, she could not approach him. In a rage, she revealed herself as a woman from the neighboring plantation who had been jealous of Toby's good fortune. She promised to leave him alone if he never revealed her secret. Toby agreed, and the hag never troubled him again. But from that night on, Toby kept a container of salt and a Bible under his pillow, just in case. The tale illustrates the deeply held African American folk belief in night-hags and the practical, protective measures used against them.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: The Ghost Bride of the Sea Islands
summary: A young Gullah man named Joshua fell in love with a woman named Eloise, the most beautiful woman on the Sea Islands. They were to be married, but on the night before the wedding, Eloise fell ill and died of a sudden fever. Joshua was heartbroken. On the night of what would have been their wedding, Joshua sat alone on the beach, grieving. At midnight, Eloise appeared before him, dressed in white, her eyes glowing faintly. She said she had returned to claim her wedding. Joshua, driven by love, danced with her on the moonlit shore as the waves crashed around them. An old conjure woman saw them and rushed to intervene, throwing salt between Joshua and the ghost bride. The spirit vanished with a wail. The conjure woman explained that the ghost bride had come to take Joshua with her to the spirit world—if he had danced until dawn, his soul would have been hers forever. Joshua painted his door 'haint blue' and never went near the beach at night again. The story is a classic Gullah ghost tale, warning against the power of love to bind the living to the dead.
source: Gullah Geechee oral tradition; snip Drums and Shadows variants

title: The Conjure Doctor Who Outwitted the Master
summary: On a Virginia plantation, the master hired a notorious slave driver to break the spirit of the enslaved people. Whippings and punishments only increased, and the people grew desperate. An old conjure doctor named Uncle Monday was called from a neighboring plantation. Uncle Monday walked through the quarters, speaking to the people and gathering roots and herbs from the forest. He prepared a special mojo bag and buried it at the crossroads near the plantation gate. That night, the slave driver could not sleep; snip he was tormented by nightmares of his victims. The next day, he was thrown from his horse for no reason. Within a week, he fled the plantation, claiming it was haunted. The master, furious, accused Uncle Monday of witchcraft and threatened to have him whipped. Uncle Monday calmly pointed at a tree stump, and it burst into flames. The master never touched him. Uncle Monday taught the people that conjure was their protection, passed from Africa, and that no master could ever truly break what the spirits protected. The tale embodies the power of conjure as a form of psychological and spiritual resistance against the violence of slavery.
source: African American oral tradition; snip Charles W. Chesnutt, The Conjure Woman (1899); snip Zora Neale Hurston collections

title: The Jack Ball: A Conjure Tale
summary: In Hoodoo tradition, a Jack Ball (sometimes called a 'follow-me-boy' or 'trick ball') is one of the most powerful conjure objects. The tale tells of a rootworker who created a Jack Ball from wax, graveyard dust, personal concerns of the target, and the rootworker's own blood, empowered by nine days of prayer and fasting. The Jack Ball was said to be able to 'follow' its target anywhere, bringing good luck to those it favored or misery to those cursed. The most famous Jack Ball story involves a man betrayed by his lover, who went to a conjure woman for justice. She created a Jack Ball that would ensure the unfaithful woman could not rest anywhere, her conscience tormenting her until she returned to make amends. The Jack Ball would roll by itself, knock on doors, and appear in unexpected places. Such stories reinforced the belief in the tangible power of conjure and the importance of honoring one's commitments. The making of a Jack Ball is one of the most secret and solemn rites in Hoodoo practice.
source: African American Hoodoo oral tradition; snip Harry M. Hyatt, Hoodoo-Conjuration-Witchcraft-Rootwork (1970)

title: The Crossroads Spirit
summary: In African American conjure tradition, the crossroads is the most spiritually charged place—the intersection where the physical world meets the spirit world. The story tells of a young man who went to a crossroads at midnight seeking power. He was instructed by a rootworker to bring a black chicken, a bottle of whiskey, and nine silver dimes. At the crossroads, he was to draw an X on the ground, light a black candle, and wait. The spirits would test him with terrors, but he must not run. According to some accounts, a figure would appear—sometimes the Devil, sometimes an old man, sometimes a faceless presence—and offer a deal: power and fortune in exchange for his soul. The crossroads spirit could teach the secrets of conjure, give winning lottery numbers, or make an enemy sick. But the price was always steep. Those who succeeded at the crossroads gained power; snip those who failed lost their minds or their lives. The crossroads story blends African spiritual traditions (where crossroads are home to the trickster god Eshu-Elegba) with the American experience of desperate people seeking power in a world that denied them any.
source: African American Hoodoo tradition; snip Zora Neale Hurston, Mules and Men (1935)

title: The Graveyard Dust Spell
summary: In Hoodoo tradition, graveyard dust is among the most potent and dangerous conjure materials. The tale tells of a woman whose husband was stolen away by a rival through love magic. Desperate, she went to a rootworker who instructed her to go to a graveyard at midnight, find the grave of someone who had been a faithful spouse, and collect dust from the center of the grave. She had to leave a silver dime as payment, walk backward away from the grave, and never look back. The graveyard dust was then mixed with the husband's personal effects and buried under the doorstep. According to the story, the husband returned within three days, unable to explain why he could not stay away. The rootworker warned that graveyard dust could be used for either love or hate, healing or harm, and that using it to harm an innocent would cause the dust to 'turn' on the user. The story teaches that the dead must be treated with respect and that conjure power requires not just knowledge but moral responsibility.
source: African American Hoodoo tradition; snip Harry M. Hyatt collections; snip Zora Neale Hurston

title: The Making of the Mojo Bag
summary: Mojo bags—small red flannel pouches filled with roots, herbs, coins, and personal objects—are central to Hoodoo practice, and their creation is surrounded by elaborate lore. One traditional story tells of a conjure woman named Aunt Polly who made mojo bags for her entire community. People came from miles around seeking her bags for luck in love, protection from harm, success in gambling, or revenge against enemies. The most famous story involves a young man about to gamble with a cruel white landowner who always cheated. Aunt Polly made him a mojo containing John the Conqueror root, a silver dime, and a petition written on brown paper. She told him to keep it in his left pocket and never let it touch the ground. At the gambling table, the young man won every hand. The landowner, enraged, accused him of cheating, but the mojo protected him from harm—the landowner's pistol would not fire. The young man escaped with his winnings and never gambled again. The mojo bag tradition reflects the African American belief that spiritual protection is carried with you, a portable connection to the power of the ancestors and the conjure tradition.
source: African American Hoodoo oral tradition; snip Zora Neale Hurston, Mules and Men (1935)

title: The Sea Island Creation Frog
summary: In Gullah Geechee creation folklore, the frog was one of the first creatures and played a crucial role in shaping the Sea Islands. The story tells that in the beginning, there was only water everywhere. The Creator needed someone to dive to the bottom and bring up mud to make land. All the big animals tried and failed—they could not hold their breath long enough to reach the bottom. Finally, the little green frog volunteered. The big animals laughed at him, but the frog dove down, down, down, past the fish, past the whales, to the very bottom of the ocean. He scooped up a mouthful of mud and kicked his way back up. When he surfaced, the Creator took the mud from the frog's mouth and formed the Sea Islands. As a reward, the frog was allowed to live both in water and on land and to sing the loudest song of any creature at dawn. This pourquoi tale explains both the origin of the Gullah homeland and why frogs sing so loudly at daybreak, connecting the Gullah people's deep sense of place with the natural world around them.
source: Gullah Geechee oral tradition; snip Sea Islands folklore collections

title: Cunjah Maum and the Healing Roots
summary: On a rice plantation along the South Carolina coast, an old Gullah woman named Cunjah Maum was known as the most powerful rootworker in the Lowcountry. When the master's daughter fell ill with a mysterious fever that no doctor could cure, the master reluctantly sent for Cunjah Maum. She came to the big house and asked for three things: a lock of the girl's hair, water from the spring that flowed east, and privacy. Cunjah Maum ground roots and leaves into a paste, mixed it with the spring water, and sang songs in an African language over the mixture. She rubbed it on the girl's forehead and feet and prayed to ancestors she could see standing in the corners of the room. Within three days, the girl recovered completely. The master offered Cunjah Maum money, but she refused—she asked instead that her grandson be taught to read. The master, grateful, agreed. Cunjah Maum's story embodies the Gullah belief in the power of rootwork to heal not just the body but to negotiate justice in a world where Black people had little power. Her refusal of money and demand for literacy represents the deeper value of knowledge over wealth.
source: Gullah Geechee oral tradition; snip Drums and Shadows (Georgia Writers' Project, 1940)

title: Brer Cooter (Terrapin) and the Deer: The Great Race
summary: In this Gullah Geechee variant of the classic trickster race tale, Brer Cooter (the terrapin) and Brer Deer are both courting the same young lady. She declares she will marry whichever one wins a ten-mile race. Brer Deer laughs at the slow-moving terrapin, but Brer Cooter is clever. He stations nine members of his family at each mile marker along the race course, and he himself waits at the start right in front of the young lady's door. At nine o'clock, the race begins. Brer Deer springs forward while Brer Cooter disappears. At the first mile marker, Brer Deer calls out, 'Brer Cooter!' A voice answers from the broomgrass, 'I'm here too!' At every mile post, the same thing happens: Brer Deer calls out, and a terrapin voice answers. By the ninth mile, Brer Deer believes he is about to tie with Brer Cooter. But at the finish line, Brer Cooter is already there, having won the race and the young lady's hand. The tale reinforces that speed alone does not win—strategy, family, and preparation are just as important as raw ability.
source: Gullah Geechee oral tradition; snip collected by Joel Chandler Harris in Uncle Remus introduction (1881)

title: The Quilting Party Ghost
summary: A classic Gullah ghost story set in the Sea Islands of Georgia. A group of women gathered for a quilting party at an old cabin on St. Helena Island. As they worked on the quilt by lantern light, they told stories to pass the night. An old woman named Sarah, whose grandmother had been enslaved on the plantation before the Civil War, told them about the cabin's history. It was said that a young enslaved woman named Liza had died there, waiting for her husband who had been sold away. As Sarah finished the story, the women heard footsteps on the porch. The door creaked open, and a young woman in an old-fashioned dress stood there, looking at the quilting frame. The women froze, recognizing the description Sarah had just given. The ghost smiled sadly, touched the quilt, and vanished. After that night, the women always left a piece of blue fabric—haint blue—in the quilt pattern to protect the cabin. The story reflects the Gullah belief that ghosts are often not malevolent but are ancestral presences who remain connected to the places and activities they loved in life.
source: Gullah Geechee oral tradition; snip Sea Islands folklore collections

title: The Awful Fate of Mr. Wolf
summary: Brer Wolf, determined to catch and eat Brer Rabbit, devises a plan to trap him in his own home. Brer Wolf hides in Brer Rabbit's house, planning to spring out when Brer Rabbit returns. But Brer Rabbit, always cautious, notices fresh tracks around the door and realizes something is wrong. He calls out, pretending to talk to himself: 'I wonder if Brer Wolf is in there. I'll just go ask my friend the cow if there's any fresh wolf tracks around.' Brer Wolf, thinking quickly, ties a string to a rock that he bounces to make it sound like he's not there. But Brer Rabbit sees through the trick and sets the house on fire. Brer Wolf burns to death inside, or in other versions, escapes with his fur singed and vows revenge. The story shows that Brer Rabbit's caution and intelligence allow him not just to escape but to turn the predator's own trap against him. Among African American audiences, the tale resonated as a message to the powerless: vigilance and cleverness can defeat even the most determined enemy.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: Mr. Bear Catches Old Mr. Bull-Frog
summary: Brer Bear, hungry and irritable, decides he will catch the enormous bullfrog that keeps him awake at night with its croaking. He finds the frog at the pond's edge and makes a grab, but the frog leaps into the water. Brer Bear decides to drink the entire pond dry. He drinks until his belly is swollen, but the pond level barely sinks. Then the bullfrog surfaces and teases him, telling him that he will never succeed. Brer Bear, in a rage, plunges his head underwater to catch the frog by force, but only gets his head stuck in the mud. Brer Rabbit and Brer Fox pass by and laugh at the sight of Brer Bear's enormous rump sticking up from the pond. They pull him out, but Brer Bear is so embarrassed that he retreats into the deep woods and never bothers the bullfrog again. The bullfrog is still there, croaking triumphantly. The tale illustrates that brute force and rage are futile against nature's defenses, and that the bully often ends up humiliated by those he sought to dominate.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: Why Mr. Possum Has No Hair on His Tail
summary: A pourquoi tale explaining the possum's hairless tail. Brer Possum once had the most beautiful, bushy tail in the animal kingdom. He was so proud of it that he spent all his time grooming and displaying it, neglecting to gather food for the winter. When winter came, Brer Possum was starving. He went to Brer Rabbit and begged for food. Brer Rabbit, always the trickster, offered him a deal: if Brer Possum would let Brer Rabbit cut off his beautiful tail, Brer Rabbit would give him food for the entire winter. Brer Possum refused at first, but as hunger grew, he reluctantly agreed. Brer Rabbit cut off the bushy tail and gave Brer Possum food. But the tail never grew back, and only a thin, hairless, rat-like tail remained. Brer Possum was so ashamed that he learned to play dead whenever anyone looked at him, so they wouldn't see his shameful tail. To this day, possums have hairless tails and play dead when frightened, eternally reminded of the price of vanity.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: Why the Negro Is Black
summary: An African American origin tale explaining skin color, collected by Joel Chandler Harris. According to Uncle Remus, in the beginning, all people were the same color. The Lord was making people and set them out to bake in the sun, like bread. He planned to bake them different shades—some light, some dark, some in between. The first batch was put in the oven, but the Lord became busy with other matters and left them too long. When he took them out, they were burnt quite black. That was the Negro. The second batch, watched more carefully, came out just right—a nice golden brown. That was the Indian. The third batch, watched too nervously, was pulled out too soon and came out pale and undercooked. That was the white man. The story is told with characteristic humor and is typical of African American 'why' tales that use wit to explain natural phenomena, often with a subtle commentary on racial hierarchy. While using self-deprecating humor, the tale subtly subverts racist ideologies by suggesting racial difference is mere accident of timing in the divine kitchen.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

title: La Llorona — The Weeping Woman
summary: La Llorona is the vengeful ghost of a woman who drowned her children in a river after discovering her husband's infidelity, then drowned herself in grief. She now wanders along rivers and lakes across Mexico and the US Southwest, dressed in white with long black hair, weeping and wailing '¡Ay, mis hijos!' Those who hear her mournful cries are said to suffer misfortune or death. The legend has pre-Columbian roots in the Aztec goddess Cihuacóatl, who appeared before the Spanish conquest weeping for the fate of her people. During the colonial period, the story syncretized with Spanish folklore of white ladies and became associated with La Malinche, Cortés's interpreter who bore his child. Today La Llorona remains one of the most recognized figures in Hispanic folklore, told as a cautionary tale to children about wandering near water alone at night.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/La_Llorona

title: La Llorona de los Ríos de Tejas — The Weeping Woman of the Texas Rivers
summary: In the South Texas borderlands along the Rio Grande, Mexican-American communities tell of La Llorona appearing near irrigation canals and arroyos where children have drowned. Unlike the classic Mexican version, the Texas variant often emphasizes her role as a protector of children rather than a predator — some say she appears to warn mothers of impending danger to their children. In Starr County and Hidalgo County, farm workers have reported hearing her cries mixing with the wind through the mesquite trees at dusk. The legend serves as both a cautionary tale about water safety in the agricultural landscape of the Rio Grande Valley and a symbol of maternal grief that transcends the border.
source: Texas-Mexican border folklore oral traditions; West, John O. 'Mexican-American Folklore' (1988)

title: La Llorona as La Malinche's Curse
summary: One of the most powerful variants of the La Llorona legend connects her directly to La Malinche, the Nahua woman who served as Hernán Cortés's interpreter, advisor, and mother of his son. In this telling, Malinche—cursed by her people for betraying the Aztec Empire—became La Llorona, condemned to wander forever weeping for her mestizo children. This version transforms the ghost story into a foundational myth of Mexican identity, embodying the trauma of conquest, the pain of cultural hybridity, and the complex legacy of the first Mexican mother. Chicana feminists like Gloria Anzaldúa and Sandra Cisneros have reclaimed this figure as a symbol of resistance, survival, and the power of the marginalized feminine voice.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/La_Llorona; Anzaldúa, Gloria 'Borderlands/La Frontera' (1987)

title: El Chupacabra — The Goat-Sucker
summary: The Chupacabra is a legendary cryptid that first appeared in Puerto Rico in 1995, blamed for mysterious livestock killings in which animals were found drained of blood. Descriptions vary: in Puerto Rico and Latin America it is often depicted as a reptilian, alien-like creature with spines along its back, standing 3-4 feet tall and hopping like a kangaroo, while in the US Southwest it is described as a hairless, dog-like animal with a pronounced spinal ridge, fangs, and claws. The legend spread rapidly through Mexico and the border states of Texas, New Mexico, and Arizona during the late 1990s, with hundreds of alleged sightings and attacks. Scientific investigations have consistently identified the creatures as coyotes, dogs, or raccoons suffering from severe sarcoptic mange, which causes hair loss, thickened skin, and a ghastly appearance. The first eyewitness's description was inspired by the alien creature 'Sil' from the 1995 science-fiction film Species. Despite the debunking, El Chupacabra remains deeply embedded in modern Hispanic folklore throughout the Southwest, representing the mysterious and unexplained in rural life.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chupacabra; Radford, Benjamin 'Tracking the Chupacabra' (2011)

title: El Chupacabra in the Texas Borderlands
summary: Throughout the 2000s and 2010s, dozens of alleged Chupacabra carcasses were reported across Texas, particularly in San Antonio, Cuero, Hood County, and Coleman County. Ranchers described finding livestock with puncture wounds and drained of blood, often attributing the attacks to a strange, hairless creature. In Cuero (2007), a hairless carcass named the 'Cuero Chupacabra' gained national attention before DNA tests revealed it was a coyote-wolf hybrid with severe mange. In Hood County (2010), animal control officers shot a creature later identified by Texas A&M as a coyote-dog hybrid. These sightings reflect the blending of ancient Mexican folk belief with modern American cryptozoology, creating a uniquely borderlands legend that continues to evolve with each new discovery.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chupacabra; Texas Parks & Wildlife reports 2004-2014

title: La Lechuza — The Witch-Owl of the Border
summary: La Lechuza is a terrifying figure in Mexican-American folklore, particularly along the Texas-Mexico border. She is described as a witch who transforms into a giant owl — specifically a great horned owl or barn owl — with a wingspan of up to ten feet and the face of an old woman.
