According to legend, a woman who makes a pact with the devil or practices black magic can become a lechuza, flying at night to attack enemies, steal children, or cause illness. In some versions, she appears as an enormous white owl that screeches a woman's cry, luring victims into the darkness. In South Texas and northern Mexico, it is said that if you hear an owl calling your name at night, you must never answer, or the lechuza will carry you away. Ranch hands and farmers tell stories of finding cattle mutilated by her talons. The lechuza is also associated with the nahual tradition, linking it to pre-Columbian shapeshifter beliefs.
source: Mexican-American oral folklore; Texas folklore archives; West, John O. 'Mexican-American Folklore' (1988)

title: El Coco — The Boogeyman of the Spanish-Speaking World
summary: El Coco, also known as El Coco Man or El Hombre del Costal (the Sack Man), is a bogeyman figure used throughout the Spanish-speaking world to frighten children into good behavior. The figure likely has Moorish origins, derived from the Arabic word for 'fear.' In the Mexican-American and Chicano communities of the Southwest, El Coco is described as a dark, shadowy creature with glowing red eyes who hides in closets, under beds, or in dark corners. Parents warn children: 'Duérmete mi niño, duérmete mi sol, si no viene el Coco y te come el dedo gordo del pie' (Sleep my child, sleep my sun, or the Coco will come and eat your big toe). Some versions describe him as a hunchbacked figure who carries a sack to collect misbehaving children. Unlike many other Hispanic folklore figures, El Coco is purely a disciplinary tool, rarely appearing in narrative stories but persisting as a cultural touchstone across generations of Hispanic families in the United States.
source: Hispanic folklore oral traditions; Texas Folklore Society publications

title: El Cucuy — The Monster Under the Bed
summary: El Cucuy is a distinctly Mexican and Mexican-American variant of the bogeyman legend, particularly prevalent in the US Southwest among Chicano families. Unlike the more generic El Coco, El Cucuy is specifically described as a small, hairy, goblin-like creature who lives under children's beds or in dark closets, waiting to grab ankles or drag away children who refuse to sleep. Some accounts describe him as having red or yellow eyes, long claws, and a mischievous grin. The legend serves dual purposes: as a parenting tool to enforce bedtime and as a cultural rite of passage where children learn to face their fears. In recent decades, El Cucuy has been reimagined in Chicano literature and art as a symbol of the fears faced by immigrant children — the unknown terrors of a new country, border crossing, and cultural displacement.
source: Chicano/Mexican-American oral traditions; Anaya, Rudolfo 'The Curse of the ChupaCabra' (2006)

title: La Mano Pachona — The Disembodied Hand of the Border
summary: La Mano Pachona is a chilling legend from northern Mexico and the US Southwest about a disembodied, hairy hand that attacks travelers at night. The hand is described as large, dark, and covered in thick hair, with long fingernails. It is said to wander along lonely roads, scratching at windows and car doors, or reaching up from beneath vehicles to grab unsuspecting victims. Some versions claim the hand belongs to a man who was cursed for his evil deeds, while others say it is the hand of a murdered criminal that continues to seek revenge. In Mexican-American folklore of Texas and California, the story is often told around campfires and at sleepovers, serving as a tale of warning about traveling alone at night. La Mano Pachona represents the fear of the fragmented, disembodied threat — danger that comes not from a whole being but from something partial and inexplicable.
source: Mexican-American folklore; borderlands oral traditions

title: Los Aluxes — The Maya Trickster Spirits
summary: Los Aluxes (singular: Alux) are small, elf-like trickster spirits from Yucatec Maya tradition that survived into the Hispanic folklore of Yucatán and spread through Mexican-American communities. These mischievous beings, standing only knee-high, are said to inhabit ancient Maya ruins, cenotes, and forests. They are known to play pranks on humans — hiding tools, tangling hair, leading travelers astray, and making strange noises at night. However, if respected and offered small gifts (such as corn, honey, or balché), they can become protective guardians of homes and cornfields. In the Yucatán, farmers still build small houses called 'kahtal alux' to invite aluxes to guard their land. Among Mexican-American communities, the belief in aluxes persists especially in areas with Maya heritage, where they are seen as guardians of indigenous traditions and the natural world against modern development.
source: Yucatec Maya folklore; Redfield, Robert 'The Folk Culture of Yucatan' (1941)

title: El Sombrerón — The Goblin with the Big Hat
summary: El Sombrerón is a small, mischievous goblin figure from Guatemalan and southern Mexican folklore who also appears in Mexican-American storytelling. He is described as a tiny man — no taller than a child — wearing an enormous hat, a red tie, and riding a tiny horse or mule. El Sombrerón is known for braiding the manes and tails of horses, donkeys, and mules into intricate patterns that cannot be undone. He also bothers women by serenading them under their windows with a tiny guitar, causing them to fall into a deep melancholy or sleeplessness. His enchantment can only be broken by someone who feeds him or by making the sign of the cross. The legend traveled to the US Southwest with Central American and Mexican immigrants, where it persists as a cautionary tale about vanity and the dangers of nighttime wandering. In some versions, El Sombrerón is said to stuff his victims with food and sugar until they die of sweetness.
source: Guatemalan and Mexican folklore; oral traditions; Texas Folklore Society

title: La Siguanaba — The Horse-Faced Woman
summary: La Siguanaba (or Sihuanaba) is a beautiful woman spirit from Mesoamerican folklore, particularly prevalent in El Salvador, Guatemala, and southern Mexico, with variants told in Mexican-American communities. She appears as a stunningly beautiful woman with long, flowing hair, seen bathing in rivers or washing clothes. She lures men — especially those who are unfaithful or have been drinking — by appearing as their lover or a beautiful stranger. When the man approaches, she transforms, revealing her true face: that of a horse or a skull, driving him mad with terror. Some versions say she then attacks and kills him. The legend has pre-Columbian origins among the Pipil and Maya peoples, where female water spirits were associated with both fertility and danger. In the US Southwest, the story is told as a warning against infidelity, drunkenness, and the dangers of trusting appearances, particularly to young men in Chicano communities.
source: Mesoamerican folklore; Pipil oral tradition; Texas Folklore Society

title: El Duende — The Gnomelike Trickster
summary: El Duende is a small, gnomelike creature from Spanish and Latin American folklore who has a prominent place in Mexican-American oral tradition. Described as a tiny old man or child-like figure wearing a large hat and often carrying a walking stick, El Duende lives in forests, caves, or abandoned buildings. He is known for playing tricks on people — stealing food, hiding objects, and leading travelers in circles through the woods. In some versions, El Duende tries to capture children to keep as his own, especially those who wander away from their parents. In curanderismo, encounters with duendes are believed to cause illness, particularly susto (fright sickness), requiring a limpieza (spiritual cleansing) from a curandero. The duende is also said to have backward feet, making it easy to identify his tracks. Among Hispanic families in the Southwest, stories of El Duende serve to keep children close to home and to explain the mysterious sounds and occurrences of rural life.
source: Spanish and Latin American folklore; Curandero traditions; Texas Folklore Society

title: The Children of La Llorona — The Weeping Children
summary: A poignant variant of the La Llorona legend tells that the spirits of the children she drowned did not pass on but remain as eternal, weeping children along the same waterways. Known sometimes as Los Hijos de la Llorona, these child spirits are said to appear as small, translucent figures playing near rivers and canals at dusk. Unlike their mother, who wails in anguish, the children are sometimes described as playful but sad, trying to lure living children to join them in the water. In some New Mexican versions, the children appear to warn other children away from dangerous waters. This variant transforms the story from one of pure horror into a tragedy of intergenerational trauma, where the sins of the mother echo through the spirits of the innocent. In Chicano literature and art, these children have been reclaimed as symbols of lost innocence and the enduring wounds of colonization.
source: New Mexican and borderlands folklore; oral traditions

title: The Curse of the Dolls — La Isla de las Muñecas
summary: The legend of the Island of the Dolls (La Isla de las Muñecas) in the Xochimilco canals near Mexico City is a modern legend that has spread widely through Mexican-American communities. According to the story, a man named Don Julián Santana found the body of a drowned girl in the canals and, haunted by her spirit, began collecting dolls and hanging them from trees to appease her soul. Over 50 years, thousands of decaying, mutilated dolls accumulated on the island, their blank eyes and torn limbs creating an atmosphere of profound unease. After Don Julián drowned in the same canal in 2001, the legend grew: visitors claim the dolls move their heads, blink, and whisper at night. The legend speaks to themes of death, childhood, and the thin boundary between the natural and supernatural worlds. For Mexican-Americans, the story resonates as a uniquely Mexican contribution to global horror folklore.
source: Mexican urban legend; Xochimilco oral tradition; travel and paranormal accounts

title: Quetzalcóatl — The Feathered Serpent
summary: Quetzalcóatl, the Feathered Serpent, is the most important syncretic deity in Mexican and Mexican-American spiritual folklore. In Aztec tradition, he was the god of wind, knowledge, learning, and the priesthood — credited with creating humanity by descending to Mictlan (the underworld) and mixing his blood with the bones of previous generations. He is also associated with the morning star (Venus) and is the inventor of books and the calendar. The feathered serpent iconography appears throughout Mesoamerica from Teotihuacan (200 BCE) to the Aztec Empire, and his worship was centered at Cholula's great pyramid. In Chicano spirituality, Quetzalcóatl has been reclaimed as a symbol of indigenous identity, cultural resistance, and the profound wisdom of pre-Columbian civilizations. The legend of Quetzalcóatl's prophesied return was later conflated with Hernán Cortés's arrival, a complex historical narrative that continues to shape Mexican-American understanding of conquest and cultural survival.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quetzalcoatl

title: Tonantzin and Our Lady of Guadalupe — The Sacred Syncretism
summary: The story of Our Lady of Guadalupe is the most profound example of indigenous-Hispanic syncretism in the Americas. According to tradition, in December 1531, the Virgin Mary appeared to Juan Diego, an Indigenous Chichimec peasant, on Tepeyac Hill in what is now Mexico City. Speaking to him in Nahuatl, she asked that a church be built on the site. When Juan Diego relayed this to Archbishop Zumárraga, the bishop demanded a sign. The Virgin instructed Juan Diego to gather Castilian roses blooming miraculously on the frozen hilltop, and when he opened his tilma (cloak) before the archbishop, the flowers fell to reveal the image of the Virgin imprinted on the fabric. Crucially, the apparition site was the former temple of Tonantzin, the Aztec mother goddess — making the Virgin of Guadalupe a direct continuation of indigenous earth-mother worship within a Catholic framework. For Chicanos and Mexican-Americans today, the Virgin of Guadalupe transcends religion to become a cultural symbol of Mexican identity, maternal protection, and the resilience of indigenous spirituality within a colonial context. She is venerated as the Empress of the Americas and the Patroness of the Unborn.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Guadalupe; Nican Mopohua (1649); Sahagún, Bernardino de 'Florentine Codex'

title: El Nahual — The Shapeshifting Witch
summary: El Nahual (or Nagual) is a shapeshifting witch or sorcerer in Mesoamerican and Hispanic folklore, with a strong presence in Mexican-American tradition. The belief, originating with the Aztecs and other Nahua peoples, holds that certain individuals have the spiritual power to transform into animals — most commonly jaguars, coyotes, dogs, owls, or turkeys. Each person is born with a tonal (animal spirit companion) linked to their birth date in the Mesoamerican calendar, but only those with special power can actually assume the animal's form. Nagualism was practiced by pre-Columbian shamans and integrated with European witchcraft beliefs during the colonial period. In Mexican-American folklore of the Southwest, nahuales are feared for their ability to cause illness, steal livestock, and harm enemies while in animal form. However, in some Indigenous communities, the nahual is seen as a protector of the people and natural resources. The concept was popularized in English by Carlos Castaneda's books, though these accounts are controversial. Today, belief in nahuales persists alongside curanderismo as part of the living folk religion of the borderlands.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nagual; Nutini, Hugo 'Bloodsucking Witchcraft' (1993)

title: Santa Muerte — Holy Death
summary: Santa Muerte (Holy Death or Saint Death) is a female folk saint and personification of death venerated primarily in Mexico and the US Southwest, with an estimated 12 million followers worldwide. She is depicted as a female skeleton wearing a long robe and carrying a scythe and a globe. Her origins blend pre-Columbian death deities such as the Aztec goddess Mictecacihuatl (Lady of the Dead) with the Spanish Grim Reaper (la Parca). Devotees pray to Santa Muerte for protection, healing, financial prosperity, love, and safe passage to the afterlife. Her statues are dressed in different colored robes depending on the petition: white for purification, red for love, gold for money, black for protection against enemies, green for justice. Though condemned by the Catholic Church, Santa Muerte's following exploded in the early 2000s and spread rapidly to the United States through Mexican immigration. Her shrines are found in botanicas and homes throughout Los Angeles, Houston, Chicago, New York, and other cities with large Mexican-American populations. She has particular appeal among those living on society's margins — taxi drivers, sex workers, police, soldiers, and prisoners — who face death's uncertainty daily.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santa_Muerte; Chesnut, R. Andrew 'Devoted to Death: Santa Muerte, the Skeleton Saint' (2018)

title: Don Pedrito Jaramillo — The Healer of Los Olmos
summary: Don Pedro Jaramillo (1829–1907) is the most famous curandero (traditional healer) and folk saint of South Texas. Born to Purépecha parents in Guadalajara, Mexico, he received his healing calling after a vision during a three-day treatment for a nose ailment with mud and water. He moved to the Los Olmos Creek area near Falfurrias, Texas, where for decades he treated thousands of people without charging, living on donations. His cures often involved water — bathing, drinking, or applying water in specific quantities for specific durations — and the number nine played a prominent role in his prescriptions. He was known for his clairvoyance, able to detect if someone was lying about their illness. At the height of his fame, families traveled from as far as New York City to seek his help. His shrine at his burial site in Falfurrias remains a pilgrimage destination, and the Texas State Historical Marker dedicated to him in 1971 was the first bilingual marker in the state. Don Pedrito embodies the tradition of curanderismo that continues to serve Hispanic communities in the borderlands, blending Indigenous healing knowledge with Catholic faith.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Don_Pedro_Jaramillo; Seman, Jennifer 'Borderlands Curanderos' (2021)

title: El Niño Fidencio — The Child Healer of Espinazo
summary: El Niño Fidencio (José de Jesús Fidencio Sintora, 1898–1938) is a Mexican folk saint and curandero whose cult center is in Espinazo, Nuevo León, and whose devotion extends into Mexican-American communities in Texas and California. Orphaned at a young age, Fidencio began performing healings at age 14 and gained fame for his extraordinary cures, which included surgery performed without instruments, trance-channeling of spirits, and the use of mud baths and herbs. He was known for his childlike demeanor — playing with toys, speaking in a high voice, and having his followers call him 'Niño' (child). His healing sessions often included theatrical elements: he would dress as a woman, a doctor, or a bullfighter. After his death in 1938, his devotees established the Fidencista Christian Church. Pilgrimages to Espinazo on his feast day (March 19) draw tens of thousands. In the US, Fidencio altars are maintained in Chicano communities, and his tradition of 'cajones' (trance sessions) continues among espiritista and curanderismo practitioners. His story represents the fusion of folk Catholicism with Indigenous healing traditions that characterizes borderlands spirituality.
source: Mexican folk sainthood traditions; Torres, Eliseo 'Healing with Herbs and Rituals' (2006); Texas Folklore Society

title: La Curandera of the Llano — The Healing Woman of the Plains
summary: Across the Hispanic villages of New Mexico and Colorado, the figure of La Curandera — the wise healing woman — is both a historical reality and a legendary archetype. These women were the primary healthcare providers in isolated communities, using knowledge of medicinal herbs passed down through generations, prayer, and ritual cleansing (limpias). Legendary curanderas are remembered for miraculous cures: healing blindness with herbal poultices, setting broken bones by touch alone, and diagnosing illness by reading eggs or by feeling the patient's aura. One of the most famous is Doña María from Las Trampas, New Mexico, who was said to have healed a dying man by rubbing him with a live chicken and a raw egg while reciting Catholic prayers. Another, La Loba from the Llano Estacado, was known as a 'she-wolf' healer who could communicate with animals and knew the medicinal use of every desert plant. These legends preserve the vital role of curanderas as repositories of Indigenous and Spanish botanical knowledge, spiritual counselors, and community matriarchs. The curandera tradition remains alive today in Hispanic communities across the Southwest, where it coexists with modern medicine.
source: New Mexican Hispano folklore; Torres, Eliseo 'Healing with Herbs and Rituals' (2006); Weigle, Marta 'New Mexican Folklore'

title: Aztlán — The Mythical Homeland of the Aztecs
summary: Aztlán is the mythical ancestral homeland of the Aztec (Mexica) peoples, a legendary island paradise from which they began their long migration southward to central Mexico around 1064 CE. According to the Codices Aubin, Boturini, and Azcatitlan, the Mexica left Aztlán guided by their god Huitzilopochtli, who forbade them from calling themselves Azteca and commanded they be known as Mexica. The 200-year journey brought them to Lake Texcoco where they saw the prophesied sign: an eagle on a cactus holding a serpent. There they built Tenochtitlan, which became the capital of the Aztec Empire. In the 1960s, the Chicano Movement reclaimed Aztlán as a symbol of cultural identity and territorial heritage. The 1969 'Plan Espiritual de Aztlán,' drafted at the National Chicano Youth Liberation Conference in Denver, declared the US Southwest territories conquered in the Mexican-American War as 'Aztlán' — the ancestral homeland of the Chicano people. For Chicanos, Aztlán represents not just a physical territory but a spiritual concept of belonging, indigenous sovereignty, and the right to cultural self-determination. It remains a powerful symbol in Chicano art, literature, and political activism.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aztl%C3%A1n; Anaya, Rudolfo 'Aztlán: Essays on the Chicano Homeland' (2017)

title: El Pacto con el Diablo — Faustian Legends of the Borderlands
summary: Pact-with-the-devil legends are widespread in Hispanic folklore of the US Southwest and northern Mexico, reflecting the deep Catholic influence on borderlands culture. These stories typically involve a desperate or ambitious person who sells their soul to the devil in exchange for wealth, power, love, or musical talent. One famous variant tells of a musician at a border cantina who makes a pact with a mysterious stranger to play the guitar like nobody else — only to find his shadow disappears and his soul is claimed at midnight. In another, a farmer in the Rio Grande Valley trades his soul for a well that never runs dry during drought, a deal that eventually costs him his entire family. The most famous Faustian figure in Hispanic folklore is Juan de Dios, who reportedly signed a contract with the devil in blood to become the greatest charro (horseman) in Jalisco. These legends serve as morality tales about the dangers of pride, greed, and the pursuit of worldly success at the expense of spiritual salvation. They also reflect the harsh economic realities of the borderlands, where poverty often tempted people toward desperate bargains.
source: Mexican and Mexican-American folklore; borderlands oral traditions; Texas Folklore Society

title: The Lost Treasure of Montezuma — Aztec Gold in the Southwest
summary: The legend of Montezuma's hidden treasure is one of the most persistent treasure myths in the US Southwest, blending Aztec history with Spanish colonial folklore and Mexican-American oral tradition. The story claims that as Hernán Cortés approached Tenochtitlan in 1519, the Aztec emperor Moctezuma II ordered his vast treasure of gold, silver, and jewels to be hidden to prevent it from falling into Spanish hands. According to various versions, the treasure was transported northward by a chain of Aztec runners and hidden in a cave somewhere in the American Southwest — possibly in New Mexico's Sandia Mountains, the Superstition Mountains of Arizona, or the Sierra Madre Occidental. Spanish conquistadors spent centuries searching for it, and later Mexican and Anglo treasure hunters continued the quest. The legend intertwines with the Seven Cities of Cíbola myth and has inspired countless expeditions, some ending in death or madness. For Mexican-Americans, the legend speaks to the theft of indigenous wealth and the hope that what was lost might one day be recovered. The story remains part of Southwestern folklore, with occasional 'discoveries' reported in local news.
source: Southwestern US folklore; Spanish colonial history; Texas Folklore Society publications

title: Las Gorras Blancas — The Night Riders of the San Luis Valley
summary: Las Gorras Blancas (The White Caps) were a secret society of Mexican-American vigilantes who rode at night in northern New Mexico and southern Colorado between 1889 and 1891, terrorizing Anglo landowners and railroad companies who were stealing Hispanic land grants. Clad in white caps and masks, they cut down fences, burned barns, and destroyed railroad property, all while leaving notes signed 'Las Gorras Blancas.' Their legend has grown into folklore: said to appear on moonless nights as ghostly riders on black horses, their white caps glowing in the dark. Some stories claim they could disappear into the arroyos like spirits, or that their leader was a curandero with supernatural powers. The historical Gorras Blancas movement was a response to the systematic dispossession of Hispanic landholders after the Mexican-American War, when the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo's land guarantees were violated. In Chicano folklore, the White Caps have become symbols of resistance against colonial dispossession and the fight for land rights, merging with the figure of El Justiciero — the righteous avenger — in borderlands storytelling.
source: New Mexican Hispano folklore; Chicano resistance history; Weigle, Marta 'New Mexican Folklore'

title: Jesús Malverde — The Generous Bandit
summary: Jesús Malverde (1870–1909) is a legendary Mexican bandit turned folk saint whose devotion has spread to Mexican-American communities throughout the US Southwest. Born in Sinaloa, Malverde was said to rob from the rich and give to the poor, earning him the nickname 'The Generous Bandit' or 'The Robin Hood of Sinaloa.' He was hanged by government authorities in 1909, and his body was left to rot, but local people began to build a shrine at his grave. Today his shrine in Culiacán, Sinaloa, is visited by thousands who pray for protection, luck, and miracles. In the United States, Malverde is especially venerated by Mexican-American immigrants for his association with crossing borders, navigating danger, and surviving on society's margins. His image — a handsome man with a mustache wearing a white shirt and scarf — appears alongside Santa Muerte in botanicas from Los Angeles to Houston. Though the Catholic Church does not recognize him, his folk canonization speaks to the need for saints who understand the struggles of the poor, the criminalized, and the dispossessed. Malverde's legend raises complex questions about morality, justice, and who society chooses to sanctify.
source: Mexican folk sainthood traditions; borderlands oral traditions; Chesnut, R. Andrew 'Devoted to Death' (2018)

title: El Silbón — The Whistling Specter of the Plains
summary: El Silbón (The Whistler) is a terrifying specter from the folklore of Mexico's northern plains and the US Southwest borderlands. He is described as a tall, thin figure dressed in white, carrying a bundle of bones on his back. His most distinctive feature is his whistle — a melody that begins softly and grows increasingly louder, signaling his approach. Those who hear his whistle must not move; snip if the whistle sounds close, the Silbón is far away, but if it sounds distant, he is right behind you. According to legend, the Silbón was a young man who murdered his father after the father killed the young man's wife in a fit of rage. The grandfather, as punishment, set the dogs on him and cursed him to wander forever carrying his father's bones. In Mexican-American ranching communities, El Silbón is a warning to those who would harm their families, and his story is told to keep children from wandering out at night. His legend has evolved in the borderlands into a uniquely bi-cultural ghost story, blending Mexican spectral traditions with the open landscapes of the American Southwest.
source: Mexican folklore; Texas-Mexico border oral traditions

title: La Loba — The Wolf Woman of the Desert
summary: La Loba (The Wolf Woman) is a figure from New Mexican and Chicano folklore who protects the wild places and animals of the Southwestern desert. Older than any human, she is the guardian of wolf and coyote spirits, and it is said that when a wolf dies, La Loba collects its bones and sings over them until the animal is reborn. She is described as an ancient woman who lives in a cave in the mountains, surrounded by the skulls and bones of animals. Children who get lost in the desert are said to be guided home by her, and hunters who kill for sport rather than need find their guns mysteriously jammed. The figure was popularized by Clarissa Pinkola Estés in her book 'Women Who Run With the Wolves,' which linked La Loba to the archetype of the wild feminine. In Chicano folklore, La Loba represents the deep connection between Hispanic peoples and the land of the Southwest, the wisdom of curanderas, and the power of women as healers and protectors of nature. Her legend also connects to the nahual tradition of shapeshifting, as La Loba is sometimes said to transform between human and wolf form.
source: New Mexican Hispano folklore; Estés, Clarissa Pinkola 'Women Who Run With the Wolves' (1992); oral traditions

title: Doña Sebastiana — The Skeleton in the Cart
summary: Doña Sebastiana is a folk figure from New Mexican Hispano culture, depicted as a skeleton riding a wooden cart or chariot, carrying a bow and arrow. She is the northern New Mexican variant of the Grim Reaper, brought by Spanish colonists and merged with Indigenous death imagery. Unlike the anonymous European Death, Doña Sebastiana has a name and personality — she is said to be vengeful toward sinners but merciful to the faithful. Her image appears in the santero tradition of New Mexican folk art, particularly in the bultos (wooden carvings) of the penitentes. In some stories, she is the same figure as Santa Muerte, while in others she is a separate entity — a skeletal woman who appears to those about to die, giving them a final chance to confess their sins. Her cart is said to be heard rattling through the mountain villages of northern New Mexico on the night before someone dies. The figure of Doña Sebastiana represents the unique religious synthesis of Spanish Catholicism and Puebloan spirituality that characterizes the Hispano culture of New Mexico, a tradition that has endured for over 400 years.
source: New Mexican Hispano santero tradition; Weigle, Marta 'Brothers of Light, Brothers of Blood' (1976)

title: El Tesoro de los Jesuitas — The Jesuit Gold of the Border
summary: The legend of the Jesuit Treasure is one of the most enduring treasure myths of the US Southwest and northern Mexico. When the Spanish Crown expelled the Jesuits from New Spain in 1767, the order was said to have hidden vast stores of gold and silver throughout the Sonoran Desert rather than let it fall into royal hands. The most famous hiding place is said to be in the Sierra Madre, near a cave marked with a cryptic Jesuit symbol — a heart pierced by an arrow above three crosses. Over the centuries, countless treasure hunters — Mexican, Anglo, and Native — have searched for this gold. Some claim the treasure is guarded by the spirit of a Jesuit priest who appears as a ghostly figure in black robes near the hiding place. Others say the treasure tunnels form a network connecting Jesuit missions across the Southwest. In Mexican-American folklore, the legend speaks to the history of Spanish colonization, the power and mystery of the Jesuit order, and the enduring hope of discovering hidden wealth in the desert. The story also reflects the painful history of dispossession, as the treasure rightfully belonged to the Indigenous and Hispanic peoples of the region.
source: Sonoran Desert folklore; Southwestern treasure legends; Texas Folklore Society

title: La Planchada — The Ghost Nurse in White
summary: La Planchada (The Ironed One, referring to her perfectly pressed uniform) is a ghost legend from Mexico City hospitals that has spread through Mexican-American communities in the US Southwest. According to the story, a beautiful nurse named Eulalia was deeply in love with a doctor who rejected her. Heartbroken, she took her own life, but her ghost now returns to the hospital where she once worked. Dressed in an immaculately starched white nurse's uniform, La Planchada appears in hospital wards at night, administering medicines and comforting patients. Some patients claim to have recovered miraculously after being visited by her, while others say she only appears to those who are about to die. The key warning is that if you see the hem of her dress or her feet, you will die within days — a version of the classic 'don't look at their feet' motif found in many ghost legends worldwide. La Planchada has been reported in Mexican-American hospitals and clinics along the border, where she is sometimes viewed as a benevolent guardian spirit of the sick and dying, particularly those without family to comfort them.
source: Mexican hospital folklore; oral tradition in US border hospitals; urban legend collections

title: El Jinete Sin Cabeza del Valle — The Headless Horseman of the Valley
summary: While the headless horseman is a figure known in many cultures, the Hispanic version from the Rio Grande Valley and northern Mexico has unique characteristics. Known as El Jinete Sin Cabeza, this specter is said to be the ghost of a Spanish conquistador or a Mexican rancher who lost his head in battle — either during the Mexican War of Independence or the Mexican-American War. He rides a black horse through the moonlit mesquite at midnight, carrying his head under his arm. Unlike the more playful Halloween version, this headless horseman is a fearsome omen: to see him foretells death, war, or disaster. In some versions, he is searching for his head, which was cut off by Comanche raiders. If you encounter him, you must not look into the eyes of his severed head, or you will be doomed to ride beside him forever. Some Chicano versions of the legend place him at the Battle of the Alamo or at the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo, connecting the supernatural figure to the traumatic history of Mexican dispossession in Texas.
source: Texas-Mexican border folklore; oral traditions; Texas Folklore Society

title: María Sabina — The Mushroom Shaman of Oaxaca
summary: María Sabina (1894–1985) was a Mazatec curandera and shaman from Huautla de Jiménez, Oaxaca, whose use of sacred psilocybin mushrooms in healing ceremonies gained worldwide fame. Her legend — part historical, part mythic — tells of a poor indigenous woman who discovered the sacred mushrooms as a child and was called by the spirits to become a healer. She conducted veladas (night ceremonies) in which she consumed the 'little saints' (as Mazatecs call the mushrooms) and channeled spirits who could diagnose and cure illnesses. In 1955, she was visited by American banker and amateur mycologist R. Gordon Wasson, whose Life magazine article 'Seeking the Magic Mushroom' (1957) introduced psychedelic mushrooms to the Western world. María Sabina later expressed regret about this exposure, saying it had broken the sacred tradition. In Chicano and Mexican-American folklore, María Sabina represents the profound healing knowledge of Indigenous Mexico, the power of women as spiritual leaders, and the tension between preserving sacred traditions and the outside world's hunger for indigenous wisdom. Her story continues to inspire spiritual seekers, artists, and activists in both Mexico and the United States.
source: Mazatec oral tradition; Wasson, R. Gordon 'The Wondrous Mushroom' (1957); Estrada, Álvaro 'Vida de María Sabina' (1977)

title: El Trauco — The Cursing Dwarf of the Forest
summary: El Trauco is a legendary figure from the folklore of Chiloé, Chile, but his legend has traveled through Latin American diaspora communities into the US Southwest. Described as a small, ugly, deformed dwarf — no more than three feet tall — who wears a hat made of a type of vine, has no teeth, and carries a wooden club or axe. He lives in deep forests and is known for two terrifying powers: his gaze can deform or curse those who meet his eyes, and his breath is believed to possess the power to impregnate young women who wander alone in the woods. According to legend, if a young woman has an unexplained pregnancy, it is blamed on El Trauco. Despite his fearsome reputation, he is also said to be a protector of the forest who punishes those who destroy trees and wildlife. In Chilean-American communities in the US, El Trauco serves as a cautionary figure against premarital sex and as a guardian of old-world traditions in a new environment. His story represents the persistence of rural folklore in urban immigrant communities and the adaptation of Latin American legends to the American landscape.
source: Chilean folklore (Chiloé); Mexican adaptation of Latin American lore; oral tradition
